topics.title: Realization of Compensators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT636 - ADVANCED CONTROL THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Realization of Compensators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Compensator** is an additional component (or network) inserted into a control system—typically in cascade with the forward path or in the feedback path—to reshape the open-loop transfer function so that the closed-loop system meets desired specifications of **stability, transient response, and steady-state accuracy**.

**Realization** is the physical implementation of a mathematically designed compensator transfer function $G_c(s)$ using passive electrical elements (R, L, C), active elements (operational amplifiers, transistors), mechanical components, or digital processors.

The three fundamental forms encountered in KTU Module 1 are:

| Compensator Type | Transfer Function Form | Pole–Zero Relationship |
|---|---|---|
| Lead | $G_c(s) = K \dfrac{s+z}{s+p}$ | $p > z > 0$ (pole farther from origin) |
| Lag | $G_c(s) = K \dfrac{s+z}{s+p}$ | $z > p > 0$ (zero farther from origin) |
| Lag–Lead | $G_c(s) = K \dfrac{(s+z_1)(s+z_2)}{(s+p_1)(s+p_2)}$ | $p_1 > z_1$ (lead part); $z_2 > p_2$ (lag part) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A compensator is *always defined by its zero/pole placement*. Whether it acts as Lead or Lag depends entirely on the **ratio $\alpha = p/z$**. If $\alpha > 1 \Rightarrow$ **LEAD**; if $\alpha < 1 \Rightarrow$ **LAG**. Examiners frequently test this distinction.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Steering Tuner" Picture

Imagine driving a car (the plant) with a slightly misaligned steering wheel. You insert a small mechanical device between the steering column and the wheels. This device is your **compensator**—it *adds phase advance* to make the response snappier (lead), *adds attenuation at high frequencies* to filter noise (lag), or *does both* (lag-lead).

When an engineer says "realize the compensator," they are essentially asking: *"Given the math equation of this steering-tuner, what resistors, capacitors, and op-amps must I solder together on a breadboard to bring this equation to life?"*

- **Passive realization** = pure R, L, C components → no external power needed, but introduces **signal attenuation** (gain loss).
- **Active realization** = uses op-amps → provides **gain boost and isolation**, but requires a DC supply.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used in KTU Problems:** The attenuation factor $\alpha = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2}$ and the design parameter $\beta = \dfrac{1}{\alpha}$ are considered **mandatory** to compute when realizing a passive lead/lag compensator.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Compensators Are Needed — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Plant Inadequacy:** A given plant $G(s)$ alone may yield poor transient response (high overshoot, slow settling) or unsatisfactory steady-state error.
2. **Specification Demand:** The engineer specifies target values for $M_p$, $t_s$, $K_v$, phase margin $\phi_m$, and gain margin $GM$.
3. **Pole-Zero Augmentation:** A compensator $G_c(s)$ is mathematically designed to shift the root-locus leftward, raise the phase margin, or increase the low-frequency gain.
4. **Physical Realization:** The designed $G_c(s)$ must be constructed using real, purchasable components.
5. **Verification:** The realized network's measured frequency response must match the Bode plot of $G_c(s)$.

### 2.2 General First-Order Compensator Structure

For both lead and lag, the canonical first-order form is:

$$G_c(s) = K \cdot \frac{s+z}{s+p}$$

- **DC Gain** $= K \cdot z/p$
- **High-Frequency Gain** $= K$
- **Phase Contribution** at corner frequencies gives *lead* (positive phase) or *lag* (negative phase).

### 2.3 Passive RC Lead Network — The Voltage Divider Realization

Consider two cascaded impedances $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ acting as a voltage divider:

- $Z_1 = R_1 + \dfrac{R_2}{1+sR_2 C}$
- $Z_2 = \dfrac{R_2}{1+sR_2 C}$

The transfer function is $G_c(s) = \dfrac{Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}$.

After algebraic simplification:

$$G_c(s) = \frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} \cdot \frac{1 + sR_1 C}{1 + s \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_1+R_2} C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because $R_1 R_2/(R_1+R_2) < R_1$, the pole is **farther** from the origin than the zero. **This confirms a LEAD network.** The DC attenuation factor is $\beta = R_2/(R_1+R_2)$.

### 2.4 Passive RC Lag Network

By **swapping** the resistor and capacitor positions in the same divider:

$$G_c(s) = \frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} \cdot \frac{1 + sR_2 C}{1 + s(R_1+R_2) C}$$

Here, $R_2 < R_1+R_2$ means the zero is closer to the origin → **LAG network**. The attenuation factor is $\alpha = R_2/(R_1+R_2) < 1$.

### 2.5 Lag-Lead Network — Bridged-T Configuration

A lag-lead passive network uses four elements: $R_1, R_2, C_1, C_2$. Its transfer function takes the form:

$$G_c(s) = \frac{(1+sR_1 C_1)(1+sR_2 C_2)}{(1+sR_1 C_1)(1+sR_2 C_2) + sR_1 C_2}$$

Proper component selection yields the required lead and lag portions.

### 2.6 Active (Op-Amp) Realization

For the general compensator $G_c(s) = \dfrac{b_0 s + b_1}{a_0 s + a_1}$, the **inverting op-amp** configuration gives:

$$G_c(s) = -\frac{Z_f(s)}{Z_i(s)}$$

By choosing:
- $Z_i = R_i$ (input resistor)
- $Z_f = R_f + \dfrac{1}{sC_f}$ (feedback resistor + capacitor)

we obtain a **Proportional + Integral (PI)** compensator → useful for **lag** action.
By swapping R and C in $Z_f$, we obtain a **Proportional + Derivative (PD)** compensator → useful for **lead** action.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Relation | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_c(s) = \dfrac{Z_2}{Z_1+Z_2}$ | Voltage divider realization |
| 2 | $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$ | Attenuation factor (Lead passive) |
| 3 | $\alpha = \dfrac{R_1+R_2}{R_2}$ | Inverse of $\beta$ |
| 4 | $z_{\text{lead}} = \dfrac{1}{R_1 C}$ | Zero of passive lead network |
| 5 | $p_{\text{lead}} = \dfrac{R_1+R_2}{R_1 R_2 C}$ | Pole of passive lead network |
| 6 | $z_{\text{lag}} = \dfrac{1}{R_2 C}$ | Zero of passive lag network |
| 7 | $p_{\text{lag}} = \dfrac{1}{(R_1+R_2) C}$ | Pole of passive lag network |
| 8 | $G_c(s) = -\dfrac{Z_f(s)}{Z_i(s)}$ | Op-amp (inverting) realization |
| 9 | $\alpha = \dfrac{p}{z}$ | Pole-zero ratio; if $\alpha>1 \Rightarrow$ LEAD |
| 10 | $\phi_{\max} = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha-1}{\alpha+1}\right)$ | Max phase lead angle |

> [!WARNING]
> Do not confuse **attenuation factor** $\alpha$ in compensator design with the **pole-zero ratio**. Both use the symbol $\alpha$ in some textbooks but represent different quantities. KTU examiners will mark down on ambiguous notation.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Servo motor controllers** in CNC machines use passive lead networks for stability.
- **PID controllers** in industrial process plants (temperature, pressure) are active compensator realizations.
- **Avionics autopilots** use lag-lead compensators for handling qualities.
- **Audio amplifiers** use lead networks to extend bandwidth and prevent oscillation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Passive RC Lead Compensator Transfer Function

**Given:** A two-port network with $Z_1$ in series with the source and $Z_2$ across the output. The component arrangement is: $R_1$ in series, then a parallel combination of $R_2$ and $C$.

**Step 1 — Identify Impedances**

$$Z_1 = R_1, \quad Z_2 = R_2 \,\vert\vert\, \frac{1}{sC} = \frac{R_2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{sC}}{R_2 + \dfrac{1}{sC}} = \frac{R_2}{1+sR_2 C}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Voltage Divider Rule**

$$\frac{V_o(s)}{V_i(s)} = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1+Z_2} = \frac{\dfrac{R_2}{1+sR_2 C}}{R_1 + \dfrac{R_2}{1+sR_2 C}}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply Numerator and Denominator by $(1+sR_2 C)$**

$$\frac{V_o(s)}{V_i(s)} = \frac{R_2}{R_1(1+sR_2 C) + R_2}$$

**Step 4 — Expand and Group**

$$\frac{V_o(s)}{V_i(s)} = \frac{R_2}{(R_1+R_2)\!\left[1 + s\dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_1+R_2} C\right]}$$

**Step 5 — Factor Out DC Gain $\beta = R_2/(R_1+R_2)$**

$$\frac{V_o(s)}{V_i(s)} = \beta \cdot \frac{1}{1+s\beta R_1 C} = \frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} \cdot \frac{1 + sR_1 C}{1 + s\dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_1+R_2} C}$$

> **Result Interpretation:**
> - Zero at $s = -1/(R_1 C)$
> - Pole at $s = -(R_1+R_2)/(R_1 R_2 C)$
> - Since $R_1 R_2/(R_1+R_2) < R_1$, pole magnitude $>$ zero magnitude ⇒ **LEAD**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Active Op-Amp Realization of a General Compensator

**Given:** Target compensator $G_c(s) = \dfrac{b_1 s + b_0}{a_1 s + a_0}$.

**Step 1 — Choose Inverting Op-Amp Topology**

For an inverting amplifier: $G_c(s) = -\dfrac{Z_f(s)}{Z_i(s)}$.

**Step 2 — Pick Input Impedance $Z_i$**

Select $Z_i(s) = R_i$ (a simple resistor).

**Step 3 — Construct Feedback Impedance $Z_f$**

To realize a general first-order compensator with possible lead-lag behavior:

$$Z_f(s) = R_f \parallel \frac{1}{sC_f} = \frac{R_f}{1+sR_f C_f}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and Form Transfer Function**

$$G_c(s) = -\frac{R_f}{R_i} \cdot \frac{1}{1+sR_f C_f}$$

This produces a **lag-type** action with pole at $s=-1/(R_f C_f)$.

**Step 5 — Add Series Element in Feedback for Lead**

To realize **lead**, add a resistor in series with $C_f$ in feedback:

$$Z_f(s) = R_{f1} + \frac{R_{f2}}{1+sR_{f2}C_f}$$

Substituting:

$$G_c(s) = -\frac{1}{R_i}\left[R_{f1} + \frac{R_{f2}}{1+sR_{f2}C_f}\right]$$

After algebraic expansion, this yields a zero at $s=-1/(R_{f1}+R_{f2})C_f$ and a pole at $s=-1/(R_{f2} C_f)$. Since $R_{f2} < R_{f1}+R_{f2}$, the pole is farther from origin ⇒ **LEAD**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lag-Lead Network Component Selection

**Given:** Target $G_c(s) = \dfrac{(s+z_1)(s+z_2)}{(s+p_1)(s+p_2)}$ with $p_1 > z_1 > 0$ and $z_2 > p_2 > 0$.

**Step 1 — Choose $R_1$ and $C_1$ for the Lag Portion (Low-Frequency)**

$$R_1 = \frac{1}{z_1 C_1}, \quad \text{where } C_1 \text{ is chosen by designer (typically 1 μF–10 μF)}$$

**Step 2 — Choose $R_2$ and $C_2$ for the Lead Portion (High-Frequency)**

$$R_2 = \frac{1}{z_2 C_2}$$

**Step 3 — Verify Pole Locations**

Compute $p_1 = \dfrac{1}{R_1 C_1 + R_1 C_2}$ and $p_2 = \dfrac{1}{R_2 C_2}$. Confirm $p_1 > z_1$ and $z_2 > p_2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Larger $C$ values yield slower time constants; smaller $C$ values are sensitive to parasitic capacitance. KTU standard problems use $C = 1\,\mu F$ unless stated otherwise.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Passive Lead | Passive Lag | Active (Op-Amp) Lead |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gain at $\omega \to 0$ | $\beta < 1$ (attenuated) | $1$ (unity) | Adjustable via $R_f/R_i$ |
| Gain at $\omega \to \infty$ | $1$ (unity) | $\beta < 1$ | Adjustable |
| Phase contribution | Positive (lead) | Negative (lag) | Both possible |
| Power source needed | No | No | Yes ($\pm 15$ V) |
| Loading effect | Yes | Yes | Negligible (high $Z_{in}$) |
| KTU exam frequency | **Very High** | **High** | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Passive RC Lead Compensator — Electrical Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    Vi["Vi (Input Voltage)"] --> nodeR1["R1 (Series Resistor)"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeJ["Node A (Junction)"]
    nodeJ --> nodeR2["R2 (Shunt Resistor)"]
    nodeJ --> nodeC["C (Shunt Capacitor)"]
    nodeR2 --> nodeGND1["Ground"]
    nodeC --> nodeGND2["Ground"]
    nodeJ --> Vo["Vo (Output Voltage)"]
```

**Interpretation:** $R_2$ and $C$ are in **parallel** between Node A and ground. This parallel $R_2 \,\vert\vert\, C$ forms the impedance $Z_2$, while $R_1$ alone is $Z_1$. The voltage across $Z_2$ is the output.

---

### 4.2 Passive RC Lag Compensator — Electrical Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    Vi["Vi (Input)"] --> nodeR1["R1 (Series)"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeJ["Node A"]
    nodeJ --> nodeR2["R2 (Shunt)"]
    nodeJ --> nodeC["C (Series with R2)"]
    nodeC --> nodeGND["Ground"]
    nodeR2 --> nodeGND2["Ground"]
    nodeJ --> Vo["Vo (Output)"]
```

**Interpretation:** Here $C$ is in **series** with the shunt path, and $R_2$ alone shunts to ground. The effective $Z_2$ has the capacitor blocking DC, giving LAG behavior.

---

### 4.3 Active Op-Amp Lead Compensator — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    Vin["Vin (Input)"] --> Zi["Zi = Ri (Input Impedance)"]
    Zi --> nodeS["Summing Node (-)"]
    nodeS --> opamp["Op-Amp (Inverting)"]
    opamp --> Vout["Vout (Compensator Output)"]
    nodeS --> Zf["Zf (Feedback Network: Rf1 + Rf2 || Cf)"]
    Zf --> opamp
    Vplus["+ Vcc"] --> opamp
    Vneg["- Vee"] --> opamp
```

**Interpretation:** The feedback impedance $Z_f$ contains both a resistor (in series) and a parallel $R_f \,\vert\vert\, C_f$ combination, yielding **lead behavior**. Gain is set by $-Z_f/R_i$.

---

### 4.4 Lag-Lead Bridge Network — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph "Lag Section (Low Frequency)"
        L1["R1 in series"]
        L2["C1 in shunt"]
        L1 --> L2
    end
    subgraph "Lead Section (High Frequency)"
        LD1["R2 in series"]
        LD2["C2 in shunt"]
        LD1 --> LD2
    end
    L1 --> LD1
    L2 --> LD2
    Vi["Input"] --> L1
    LD2 --> Vo["Output"]
```

**Interpretation:** A cascade where the **lag section** dominates at low frequencies (improving steady-state error) and the **lead section** dominates at high frequencies (improving transient response). This is the most exam-relevant block for KTU Module 1 problems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between lead and lag compensators with respect to pole-zero placement and frequency response.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Lead Compensator | Lag Compensator |
|---|---|---|
| Pole-zero position | Pole is farther from origin than zero | Zero is farther from origin than pole |
| Pole-zero ratio $\alpha = p/z$ | $\alpha > 1$ | $\alpha < 1$ |
| Phase contribution | Positive (advances phase) | Negative (delays phase) |
| Effect on system | Increases bandwidth, improves transient response | Reduces steady-state error |
| Bode plot slope | $+20$ dB/decade between zero and pole | $-20$ dB/decade between pole and zero |

*(Examiner's note: Award full 3 marks only if the student provides both pole-zero relation AND phase behavior. Mentioning only one aspect gets 1 mark.)*

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the significance of the attenuation factor $\beta$ in passive lead compensator realization?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The attenuation factor $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$ represents the **DC gain loss** introduced by the passive lead network. It is always less than unity ($\beta < 1$), meaning the passive compensator attenuates the signal at low frequencies. This loss must be compensated by increasing the amplifier gain downstream by a factor of $1/\beta$. In active op-amp realizations, this attenuation is overcome naturally by the op-amp gain $R_f/R_i$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Derive the transfer function of a passive RC lead compensator realized using a voltage divider network with $Z_1 = R_1$ and $Z_2 = R_2 \,\vert\vert\, (1/sC)$. Show clearly the location of pole and zero. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the lead compensator with transfer function $G_c(s) = 10 \cdot \dfrac{s+2}{s+10}$, design the passive RC realization by choosing $C = 1\,\mu F$. Specify $R_1$ and $R_2$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the complete derivation. The student must show:

1. Identification of $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ — 1 mark
2. Voltage divider application — 1 mark
3. Multiplication by $(1+sR_2 C)$ — 1 mark
4. Factoring $\beta$ — 1 mark
5. Final expression $G_c(s) = \beta \dfrac{1+sR_1 C}{1+s\beta R_1 C}$ — 1 mark
6. Pole and zero locations with comparison — 2 marks

**Part (b) — Component Design (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Identify the zero and pole:
- Zero: $z = 2$ rad/s
- Pole: $p = 10$ rad/s

**Step 2:** Match the standard form $\beta \dfrac{1+sR_1 C}{1+s\beta R_1 C}$ with $G_c(s) = 10 \cdot \dfrac{s+2}{s+10}$.

Rewriting $G_c(s)$:

$$G_c(s) = 10 \cdot \frac{2\left(\dfrac{s}{2}+1\right)}{10\left(\dfrac{s}{10}+1\right)} = 2 \cdot \frac{1 + 0.5 s}{1 + 0.1 s}$$

So $\beta = 2$ and $\beta R_1 C = 0.1 \Rightarrow R_1 C = 0.05$.

**Step 3:** Solve component values:

Given $C = 1\,\mu F = 10^{-6}\,F$:

$$R_1 = \frac{0.05}{10^{-6}} = 50{,}000\,\Omega = 50\,k\Omega$$

From $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} = 2$... **This gives $\beta > 1$, which is impossible for passive.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall:** When $\beta > 1$ or DC gain compensation $> 1$, the **passive network alone cannot realize the compensator** — an amplifier stage is mandatory. Students lose 3–4 marks here by ignoring this. The correct approach: cascade an op-amp with gain $10/\beta = 5$ to provide both the gain AND $\beta = 2$ matching.

**Step 4 — Corrected Realization with Amplifier:**

Cascade the passive network with an inverting amplifier of gain $-5$. The passive part then uses $\beta = 0.5$:

$$R_1 = 50\,k\Omega, \quad R_2 = \frac{\beta R_1}{1-\beta} = \frac{0.5 \times 50k}{0.5} = 50\,k\Omega$$

**Final Design:** $R_1 = 50\,k\Omega$, $R_2 = 50\,k\Omega$, $C = 1\,\mu F$, with amplifier gain $= -5$.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat circuit diagram the realization of an active lead compensator using an op-amp. Derive its transfer function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unity feedback system has plant $G(s) = \dfrac{4}{s(s+2)}$. A lag compensator $G_c(s) = \dfrac{s+0.1}{s+0.01}$ is inserted in cascade. Show that this is indeed a lag compensator and compute the steady-state error for a unit ramp input before and after compensation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Active Op-Amp Lead Compensator (7 marks):**

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
The op-amp is connected in inverting configuration. The input impedance $Z_i = R_i$ (pure resistor). The feedback impedance $Z_f$ consists of $R_{f1}$ in series with the parallel combination of $R_{f2}$ and $C_f$:

$$Z_f(s) = R_{f1} + \frac{R_{f2}}{1+sR_{f2}C_f} = \frac{R_{f1}(1+sR_{f2}C_f) + R_{f2}}{1+sR_{f2}C_f}$$

**Transfer Function Derivation (3 marks):**

$$\frac{V_o(s)}{V_i(s)} = -\frac{Z_f(s)}{R_i} = -\frac{1}{R_i} \cdot \frac{R_{f1} + R_{f2} + sR_{f1}R_{f2}C_f}{1+sR_{f2}C_f}$$

Rewriting:

$$G_c(s) = -K \cdot \frac{1 + s(R_{f1} \,\vert\vert\, R_{f2})C_f}{1+sR_{f2}C_f}$$

where $K = (R_{f1}+R_{f2})/R_i$.

**Pole-Zero Verification (2 marks):**
- Zero: $z = -1/[(R_{f1} \,\vert\vert\, R_{f2}) C_f]$
- Pole: $p = -1/(R_{f2} C_f)$
- Since $R_{f1} \,\vert\vert\, R_{f2} < R_{f2}$, the zero is closer to origin ⇒ **LEAD** action confirmed.

**Part (b) — Lag Compensator Analysis (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Given $G_c(s) = \dfrac{s+0.1}{s+0.01}$.

Zero at $z = 0.1$, Pole at $p = 0.01$.

$$\alpha = \frac{p}{z} = \frac{0.01}{0.1} = 0.1 < 1$$

Since $\alpha < 1$, this is a **LAG** compensator. ✓ **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Compute steady-state error for unit ramp before compensation. **(3 marks)**

Open-loop TF: $G(s) = \dfrac{4}{s(s+2)}$

Velocity constant:
$$K_v = \lim_{s\to 0} sG(s) = \lim_{s\to 0} s \cdot \frac{4}{s(s+2)} = \frac{4}{2} = 2$$

Steady-state error:
$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{K_v} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

**Step 3:** Compute steady-state error after compensation. **(3 marks)**

Open-loop TF: $G_c(s)G(s) = \dfrac{s+0.1}{s+0.01} \cdot \dfrac{4}{s(s+2)}$

New velocity constant:
$$K_v' = \lim_{s\to 0} s \cdot \frac{(s+0.1) \cdot 4}{(s+0.01) \cdot s(s+2)} = \frac{0.1}{0.01} \cdot \frac{4}{2} = 10 \cdot 2 = 20$$

New steady-state error:
$$e_{ss}' = \frac{1}{K_v'} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05$$

**Conclusion:** Compensation reduces the ramp steady-state error from $0.5$ to $0.05$ — a **10× improvement**. This illustrates the prime purpose of a lag compensator.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
>
> 1. **Pole-Zero Confusion (–2 marks):** Many students write "lead" when zero $>$ pole. Always confirm by computing $\alpha = p/z$ first.
> 2. **Forgetting the Amplifier in Passive Realization (–3 marks):** When DC gain $K > 1$, the passive network alone cannot deliver it; an amplifier stage is mandatory.
> 3. **Sign Error in Op-Amp Output (–1 mark):** Inverting op-amp gives a negative sign; check whether the problem asks for the magnitude or signed TF.
> 4. **Wrong Attenuation Factor Definition (–2 marks):** Some texts use $\alpha = R_1/R_2$, others use $\alpha = (R_1+R_2)/R_2$. State the convention you adopt at the start of the answer.
> 5. **Skipping the Verification Step (–1 mark):** Always verify the final design by substituting back into the transfer function and checking pole-zero locations.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Realization of Compensators**

- ✅ Compensator $=$ additional network added to alter open-loop characteristics.
- ✅ Lead compensator: $p > z$ → positive phase → faster transient response.
- ✅ Lag compensator: $z > p$ → negative phase → better steady-state accuracy.
- ✅ Lag-lead: combination for both transient + steady-state improvement.
- ✅ Passive realization uses only R, L, C → introduces attenuation ($\beta < 1$).
- ✅ Active realization uses op-amps → adjustable gain, no loading.
- ✅ Voltage divider is the core of passive lead/lag realization.
- ✅ The transfer function of passive lead is $\beta \cdot \dfrac{1+sR_1 C}{1+s\beta R_1 C}$.
- ✅ The transfer function of passive lag is $\dfrac{1+sR_2 C}{1+s(R_1+R_2)C}$.
- ✅ Attenuation factor $\beta = R_2/(R_1+R_2)$ — compensate by adding amplifier gain $1/\beta$.
- ✅ Op-amp inverting topology: $G_c(s) = -Z_f/Z_i$.
- ✅ Use $Z_i = R_i$, $Z_f = R_f \,\vert\vert\, C_f$ for lag and $Z_f = R_{f1} + R_{f2} \,\vert\vert\, C_f$ for lead.
- ✅ Always verify by computing actual pole and zero from the realized network.
- ✅ For exam: state pole and zero locations explicitly; do not skip derivations.
- ✅ KTU expects you to **draw the circuit AND derive the TF AND specify component values** — missing any one costs marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lag, Lead and Lag-Lead
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT636 - ADVANCED CONTROL THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Lag, Lead and Lag-Lead

# Lag, Lead and Lag-Lead Compensators

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Focus (KTU 2024 Scheme):** This topic falls under the design of compensation networks using frequency response and root locus techniques. The module weightage typically demands proficiency in transfer function derivation, pole-zero interpretation, and Bode-plot reasoning.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Compensator?

A **compensator** is an additional network (electrical, mechanical, or software-defined) inserted in the forward path, feedback path, or cascade with the plant to alter the open-loop transfer function such that the **closed-loop system meets specified performance criteria** (transient response, steady-state error, bandwidth, stability margins).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A compensator $G_c(s)$ is a controller transfer function placed in series (cascade) with the original plant $G(s)$ such that the compensated open-loop transfer function becomes $G_c(s)G(s)H(s)$, and the resulting closed-loop system achieves the desired dynamic specifications.

### 1.2 The Three Standard Compensators

| Type | Effect on Phase | Primary Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Lead Compensator** | Adds **positive** phase (lead) | Improves transient response & stability margins |
| **Lag Compensator** | Adds **negative** phase (lag) | Improves steady-state accuracy |
| **Lag-Lead Compensator** | Lag at low frequency, lead at high frequency | Improves *both* steady-state and transient response |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The Car Steering Analogy:**
> - **Lead compensator** is like a *sharp, quick steering response*. The moment you see a curve, the wheels turn aggressively. This makes the car more responsive (faster transient) and stable around bends (greater phase margin).
> - **Lag compensator** is like *reducing the engine's fuel map at cruise*. It sacrifices a little speed for fuel economy (steady-state precision). It corrects slow steady-state drift without altering fast reactions.
> - **Lag-Lead compensator** is a *dual-mode suspension*: soft and steady on long highway stretches (lag for steady-state), but firm and quick over sharp bumps (lead for transient). It gives the best of both worlds.

### 1.4 Why Compensators Are Needed

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight:** Increasing the loop gain $K$ alone can reduce steady-state error, but it also pushes the system toward instability (closed-loop poles migrate toward the right-half plane). Compensators allow **independent tuning** of steady-state accuracy and transient response by reshaping the Bode plot or root locus.

**Key metrics a compensator must satisfy:**
- Phase Margin $\geq 40^\circ$ to $\geq 60^\circ$
- Gain Margin $\geq 6$ dB to $\geq 12$ dB
- Steady-state error constants ($K_p, K_v, K_a$) meet design specs
- Bandwidth adequate for desired speed of response
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Lead Compensator (Phase Lead Network)

### 2.1.1 Transfer Function Forms

The lead compensator has a **zero closer to the origin than the pole** on the negative real axis.

**Form 1 — Time-constant form (most common in KTU problems):**

$$G_c(s) = K \cdot \frac{1 + aTs}{1 + Ts}, \quad a > 1$$

**Form 2 — Pole-Zero form:**

$$G_c(s) = K \cdot \frac{s + \frac{1}{aT}}{s + \frac{1}{T}} \cdot \frac{1}{a}$$

- **Zero** at $s = -\dfrac{1}{aT}$
- **Pole** at $s = -\dfrac{1}{T}$
- Since $a > 1$, the zero is **closer to the imaginary axis** than the pole.

**Form 3 — Frequency-Response normalized form:**

$$G_c(j\omega) = K \cdot \frac{1 + j\omega a T}{1 + j\omega T} = K \cdot a \cdot \frac{1 + j\omega a T}{1 + j\omega a T \cdot a}$$

The ratio $\dfrac{aT}{T} = a$ is called the **lead ratio**.

### 2.1.2 Frequency-Response Characteristics

- The compensator produces **maximum phase lead** $\phi_m$ at the geometric mean frequency $\omega_m$:

$$\omega_m = \frac{1}{T\sqrt{a}}$$

$$\sin(\phi_m) = \frac{a - 1}{a + 1} \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; a = \frac{1 + \sin\phi_m}{1 - \sin\phi_m}$$

- At $\omega = \omega_m$, the magnitude of $G_c(j\omega_m)$ (excluding the gain $K$) is:

$$\left|\frac{G_c(j\omega_m)}{K}\right| = \sqrt{a} \;\;\text{(or } 10\log_{10}(a) \text{ dB)}$$

### 2.1.3 Effect on the Closed-Loop System

- **Phase margin increases** $\Rightarrow$ reduced overshoot, improved damping.
- **Bandwidth increases** $\Rightarrow$ faster rise time.
- **High-frequency gain is amplified** by factor $a$ $\Rightarrow$ may amplify noise.
- Steady-state error is **not significantly improved** (the integrator/low-frequency gain is unchanged).

---

## 2.2 Lag Compensator (Phase Lag Network)

### 2.2.1 Transfer Function Forms

The lag compensator has a **pole closer to the origin than the zero**.

**Form 1 — Time-constant form:**

$$G_c(s) = K \cdot \frac{1 + Ts}{1 + aTs}, \quad a > 1$$

**Form 2 — Pole-Zero form:**

$$G_c(s) = K \cdot \frac{s + \frac{1}{T}}{s + \frac{1}{aT}}$$

- **Zero** at $s = -\dfrac{1}{T}$
- **Pole** at $s = -\dfrac{1}{aT}$
- The pole is **closer to the origin** (and the imaginary axis) than the zero.

### 2.2.2 Frequency-Response Characteristics

- The compensator produces **negative phase** (lag) at all frequencies.
- The phase is most negative around the geometric mean frequency $\omega_m = \dfrac{1}{T\sqrt{a}}$.
- At very low frequencies, magnitude $\approx 1$ (i.e., 0 dB).
- At very high frequencies, magnitude $\approx \dfrac{1}{a}$ (i.e., $-20\log_{10}(a)$ dB attenuation).

### 2.2.3 Effect on the Closed-Loop System

- **Static error constants increase** by factor $a$ at low frequencies.
- **Steady-state error decreases** (e.g., $K_v$ becomes $aK_v$).
- Transient response is **largely unaffected** if pole/zero are placed sufficiently close to origin.
- The high-frequency attenuation by factor $1/a$ allows the gain crossover to be shifted.

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU pitfall: students confuse lead and lag. **Memory trick:** Lead = Larger numerator time-constant ($aT > T$, so numerator is $1 + aTs$ in lead). Lag = Larger denominator time-constant ($aT > T$, so denominator is $1 + aTs$ in lag). The bigger $aT$ goes on the *numerator* in lead, *denominator* in lag.

---

## 2.3 Lag-Lead Compensator

A cascade combination of a lag section and a lead section:

$$G_c(s) = \underbrace{\frac{1 + aTs}{1 + Ts}}_{\text{Lead}} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{1 + bTs}{1 + \beta Ts}}_{\text{Lag}}, \quad a > 1, \; \beta > b > 1$$

Alternatively, using the parallel decomposition:

$$G_c(s) = K \cdot \frac{(s + z_1)(s + z_2)}{(s + p_1)(s + p_2)}$$

with pole-zero placement:
- For the **lag part**: $0 < p_1 < z_1$ (pole inside zero, near origin)
- For the **lead part**: $0 < z_2 < p_2$ (zero inside pole, away from origin)

> [!NOTE]
> The lag section is designed first to improve steady-state accuracy, and the lead section to recover the lost phase margin and improve transient response. This is the classical frequency-domain design procedure.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Lead | Lag | Lag-Lead |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Standard form | $K\dfrac{1+aTs}{1+Ts},\;a>1$ | $K\dfrac{1+Ts}{1+aTs},\;a>1$ | Product of the two |
| 2 | Zero location | $s = -\dfrac{1}{aT}$ | $s = -\dfrac{1}{T}$ | Two zeros |
| 3 | Pole location | $s = -\dfrac{1}{T}$ | $s = -\dfrac{1}{aT}$ | Two poles |
| 4 | $\alpha$ (lead ratio) or $\beta$ (lag ratio) | $a$ | $a$ | $a$ and $\beta$ |
| 5 | Maximum phase frequency | $\omega_m = \dfrac{1}{T\sqrt{a}}$ | $\omega_m = \dfrac{1}{T\sqrt{a}}$ | Two such frequencies |
| 6 | $\sin(\phi_m)$ relation | $\sin\phi_m = \dfrac{a-1}{a+1}$ | $\sin(-\phi_m) = \dfrac{a-1}{a+1}$ | Each section independent |
| 7 | Magnitude at $\omega_m$ | $\sqrt{a}$ (gain boost) | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{a}}$ (attenuation) | Product of both |
| 8 | Effect on $K_v$ (Type-1) | None (if no integrator) | Multiplied by $a$ | Multiplied by $\beta$ |
| 9 | Effect on bandwidth | Increases | Slight decrease | Net increase possible |
| 10 | Effect on phase margin | **Increases** | Slight decrease | **Increases** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> - **Lead**: Used in radar antenna position control, hard-disk-drive head positioning, robotic arm tracking — wherever fast, accurate transient response is needed.
> - **Lag**: Used in process control (temperature, level, flow loops) where steady-state offset is critical and slow response is acceptable.
> - **Lag-Lead**: Used in flight-control autopilots, industrial servo systems, and any high-performance application requiring *both* accuracy and speed.

---

## 2.5 Realization (Physical Implementation)

A passive electrical network realization of a lead compensator:

| Component | Position | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Resistor $R_1$ | Series arm | $R_1$ |
| Resistor $R_2$ | Parallel arm | $R_2$ |
| Capacitor $C$ | Parallel arm | $C$ |

$$G_c(s) = \frac{1 + R_1 C s}{1 + (R_1 + R_2) C s} = \frac{1 + aTs}{1 + Ts}$$

where $T = (R_1 + R_2)C$ and $aT = R_1 C$, so $a = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} < 1$ — note this is for the *inverse* form; the lead ratio $1/a > 1$ is used in the standard form.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Maximum Phase Lead Angle $\phi_m$

**Starting point:**

$$G_c(j\omega) = \frac{1 + j\omega a T}{1 + j\omega T}$$

**Step 1 — Split into real and imaginary parts.** Multiply numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate of the denominator:

$$G_c(j\omega) = \frac{(1 + j\omega a T)(1 - j\omega T)}{(1 + j\omega T)(1 - j\omega T)} = \frac{(1 + \omega^2 a T^2) + j\omega T(a - 1)}{1 + \omega^2 T^2}$$

**Step 2 — Define the phase angle $\phi$.**

$$\phi(\omega) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega T (a-1)}{1 + \omega^2 a T^2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Maximize $\phi$ by differentiating with respect to $\omega$ and setting $\dfrac{d\phi}{d\omega} = 0$.** Equivalently, use the identity:

$$\tan\phi = \frac{\text{Numerator of imaginary part}}{\text{Denominator of real part}} = \frac{\omega T (a-1)}{1 + \omega^2 a T^2}$$

Let $u = \omega T$. Then:

$$\tan\phi = \frac{(a-1)u}{1 + a u^2}$$

**Step 4 — Maximize.** Differentiate $\tan\phi$ with respect to $u$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{d}{du}\!\left[\frac{(a-1)u}{1 + a u^2}\right] = \frac{(a-1)(1 + a u^2) - (a-1)u(2au)}{(1 + a u^2)^2} = 0$$

This gives:

$$(a-1)(1 + a u^2 - 2a u^2) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 1 - a u^2 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; u = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}$$

Since $u = \omega T$, the maximum occurs at:

$$\omega_m = \frac{1}{T\sqrt{a}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{aT \cdot T}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \cdot \frac{1}{aT}}$$

This is the **geometric mean** of the pole frequency $\dfrac{1}{T}$ and the zero frequency $\dfrac{1}{aT}$.

**Step 5 — Substitute $u = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{a}}$ into $\tan\phi$:**

$$\tan\phi_m = \frac{(a-1) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}}{1 + a \cdot \frac{1}{a}} = \frac{(a-1)/\sqrt{a}}{2} = \frac{a-1}{2\sqrt{a}}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to $\sin\phi_m$.** Using the identity:

$$\sin\phi_m = \frac{\tan\phi_m}{\sqrt{1 + \tan^2\phi_m}}$$

$$\tan^2\phi_m = \frac{(a-1)^2}{4a}$$

$$1 + \tan^2\phi_m = \frac{4a + (a-1)^2}{4a} = \frac{a^2 + 2a + 1}{4a} = \frac{(a+1)^2}{4a}$$

$$\sin\phi_m = \frac{(a-1)/(2\sqrt{a})}{(a+1)/(2\sqrt{a})} = \frac{a-1}{a+1}$$

**Final result:**

$$\boxed{\;\sin\phi_m = \frac{a-1}{a+1} \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; a = \frac{1+\sin\phi_m}{1-\sin\phi_m}\;}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Magnitude at $\omega_m$

At $u = \omega T = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{a}}$:

$$|G_c(j\omega_m)| = \frac{|1 + j\omega_m aT|}{|1 + j\omega_m T|} = \frac{\sqrt{1 + a^2 T^2 \omega_m^2}}{\sqrt{1 + T^2 \omega_m^2}}$$

Substitute $\omega_m^2 = \dfrac{1}{aT^2}$:

$$T^2 \omega_m^2 = \frac{1}{a}, \quad a^2 T^2 \omega_m^2 = a$$

$$|G_c(j\omega_m)| = \frac{\sqrt{1 + a}}{\sqrt{1 + 1/a}} = \frac{\sqrt{1+a}}{\sqrt{(a+1)/a}} = \sqrt{a}$$

**Final result:**

$$\boxed{\;|G_c(j\omega_m)| = \sqrt{a}\;}$$

In decibels: $20\log_{10}(\sqrt{a}) = 10\log_{10}(a)$.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Example — Finding Compensator Parameters

**Problem:** A unity-feedback system has open-loop transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{4}{s(s+2)}$. Design a lead compensator to provide a phase margin of $40^\circ$ while the velocity error constant $K_v$ is at least $8$ sec$^{-1}$.

**Step 1 — Original system analysis.**
The velocity error constant is $K_v = \lim_{s\to 0} sG(s) = \lim_{s\to 0} \dfrac{4s}{s(s+2)} = 2$ sec$^{-1}$.
Phase margin of the uncompensated system: at gain crossover $\omega_{gc} = 2$ rad/s (where $|G(j\omega)| = 1$), phase $=-90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega/2) = -90^\circ - 45^\circ = -135^\circ$, giving PM = $180^\circ - 135^\circ = 45^\circ$. 

For KTU exam, exact original PM is computed similarly. Let's instead solve a generic parametric problem to show the design algorithm.

**Step 2 — Compute required $\phi_m$.**
Required additional phase: $\phi_m = PM_{\text{desired}} - PM_{\text{existing}} + \text{compensation shift} + \text{safety margin} = 40^\circ - 45^\circ + 12^\circ = 7^\circ$ (with $\approx 5^\circ - 12^\circ$ safety margin typically added).

**Step 3 — Compute $a$.**
$$a = \frac{1 + \sin\phi_m}{1 - \sin\phi_m} = \frac{1 + \sin 7^\circ}{1 - \sin 7^\circ} = \frac{1 + 0.1219}{1 - 0.1219} = \frac{1.1219}{0.8781} \approx 1.278$$

**Step 4 — Compute gain boost.**
Gain boost in dB: $10\log_{10}(a) = 10\log_{10}(1.278) \approx 1.06$ dB.

**Step 5 — Place $\omega_m$ at the new gain crossover.** Find where the original magnitude plot is at $-1.06$ dB; that frequency becomes $\omega_m$.

**Step 6 — Compute $T$.**
$$T = \frac{1}{\omega_m \sqrt{a}}$$

**Step 7 — Final compensator:**
$$G_c(s) = \frac{1 + 1.278\,Ts}{1 + Ts}$$

with $K$ chosen to set $K_v = 8$ sec$^{-1}$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Lag Compensator Phase

$$G_c(j\omega) = \frac{1 + j\omega T}{1 + j\omega a T}, \quad a > 1$$

$$\phi(\omega) = \tan^{-1}(\omega T) - \tan^{-1}(\omega a T) < 0$$

Maximum (most negative) lag occurs at $\omega_m = \dfrac{1}{T\sqrt{a}}$, and:

$$\sin(-\phi_m) = \frac{a - 1}{a + 1}$$

Magnitude at $\omega_m$: $|G_c(j\omega_m)| = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{a}}$ (attenuation).

---

## 3.5 Comparison Table — Polar Plot Behavior

| Frequency Range | Lead Phase | Lead Magnitude | Lag Phase | Lag Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Low ($\omega \to 0$) | $\approx 0^\circ$ | $\approx 0$ dB | $\approx 0^\circ$ | $\approx 0$ dB |
| Mid ($\omega = \omega_m$) | $+\phi_m$ positive | $+\dfrac{1}{2}\cdot 20\log_{10}(a)$ dB | $-\phi_m$ negative | $-\dfrac{1}{2}\cdot 20\log_{10}(a)$ dB |
| High ($\omega \to \infty$) | $\approx 0^\circ$ | $+20\log_{10}(a)$ dB | $\approx 0^\circ$ | $-20\log_{10}(a)$ dB |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pole-Zero Configuration (S-Plane)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph LEAD["LEAD COMPENSATOR (a greater than 1)"]
        ZL["Zero at s = -1 by aT"]:::zeroNode
        PL["Pole at s = -1 by T"]:::poleNode
        O1["Origin"]:::origin
        ZL --- O1
        O1 --- PL
    end
    subgraph LAG["LAG COMPENSATOR (a greater than 1)"]
        ZG["Zero at s = -1 by T"]:::zeroNode
        PG["Pole at s = -1 by aT"]:::poleNode
        O2["Origin"]:::origin
        PG --- O2
        O2 --- ZG
    end
    classDef zeroNode fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#00aa00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef poleNode fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef origin fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** In a **lead** compensator, the **zero is closer** to the imaginary axis (jω-axis) and the **pole is farther left**. In a **lag** compensator, the **pole is closer** to the imaginary axis and the **zero is farther left**.

---

## 4.2 Bode-Plot Frequency-Response Shapes

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph MAG["MAGNITUDE PLOT (dB)"]
        direction LR
        MA1["Low Freq: 0 dB"]:::levelA
        MA2["Slope transitions from 0 to plus 20 dB by decade"]:::rampA
        MA3["High Freq: plus 20 log a dB asymptote"]:::levelA
    end
    subgraph PHS["PHASE PLOT (degrees)"]
        direction LR
        P1["Low Freq: 0 deg"]:::levelB
        P2["Rises to plus phi_m at omega_m"]:::rampB
        P3["High Freq: 0 deg"]:::levelB
    end
    classDef levelA fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef levelB fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef rampA fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef rampB fill:#fff8e6,stroke:#996600,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Lead:** Phase *rises* above zero (positive hump), magnitude slope *increases* at high frequencies.
> **For Lag:** Phase *dips* below zero (negative hump), magnitude slope *decreases* at high frequencies.

---

## 4.3 Block-Diagram Architecture of Compensated System

```mermaid
graph LR
    R["Reference Input R(s)"]:::inputNode
    SUM["Comparator: Plus Minus Node"]:::sumNode
    EC["Error Signal E(s)"]:::sigNode
    CC["Compensator Gc(s)"]:::ctrlNode
    PL["Plant G(s)"]:::plantNode
    FB["Feedback H(s) = 1 for unity"]:::fbNode
    Y["Output Y(s)"]:::outNode
    R --> SUM
    SUM --> EC
    EC --> CC
    CC --> PL
    PL --> Y
    Y --> FB
    FB --> SUM
    classDef inputNode fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef sumNode fill:#fff0e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef sigNode fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef ctrlNode fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef plantNode fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef fbNode fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#330099,color:#000
    classDef outNode fill:#fffacd,stroke:#996600,color:#000
```

**Closed-loop transfer function:**

$$T(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_c(s)\, G(s)}{1 + G_c(s)\, G(s)\, H(s)}$$

For unity feedback ($H(s) = 1$):

$$T(s) = \frac{G_c(s)\, G(s)}{1 + G_c(s)\, G(s)}$$

---

## 4.4 Lag-Lead Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN["Input U(s)"]:::inNode
    LAG["Lag Section: 1 plus bTs over 1 plus beta Ts"]:::lagBlock
    LEAD["Lead Section: 1 plus aTs over 1 plus Ts"]:::leadBlock
    OUT["Output V(s)"]:::outNode
    IN --> LAG
    LAG --> LEAD
    LEAD --> OUT
    classDef inNode fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef lagBlock fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef leadBlock fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef outNode fill:#fffacd,stroke:#996600,color:#000
```

**Mathematical relationship:**

$$V(s) = \underbrace{\frac{1 + aTs}{1 + Ts}}_{\text{Lead}} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{1 + bTs}{1 + \beta Ts}}_{\text{Lag}} \cdot U(s), \quad a > 1,\;\beta > b > 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design sequence in KTU exam questions:**
> 1. Compute the **lag ratio $\beta$** from the required increase in steady-state error constant.
> 2. Choose lag zero $z_1 = -1/T_1$ and pole $p_1 = -1/(\beta T_1)$ near the origin.
> 3. Design the **lead section** to compensate the phase lost by the lag section and meet phase margin.
> 4. Verify all specs on the Bode plot or root locus.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**
*Distinguish between lead, lag, and lag-lead compensators in terms of their pole-zero locations and primary applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **lead compensator** has the transfer function $G_c(s) = K\dfrac{1 + aTs}{1 + Ts}$ with $a > 1$. Its **zero is closer to the origin** ($s = -1/aT$) and its **pole is farther left** ($s = -1/T$). It adds **positive phase**, thereby improving transient response and phase margin. Application: servo tracking systems.

A **lag compensator** has the transfer function $G_c(s) = K\dfrac{1 + Ts}{1 + aTs}$ with $a > 1$. Its **pole is closer to the origin** ($s = -1/aT$) and its **zero is farther left** ($s = -1/T$). It adds **negative phase** but increases low-frequency gain, improving steady-state accuracy. Application: process control loops.

A **lag-lead compensator** is a cascade of both, providing lag action at low frequencies (steady-state improvement) and lead action at high frequencies (transient improvement). Application: high-performance flight control systems.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]**
*State the formula for maximum phase lead $\phi_m$ offered by a lead compensator having transfer function $G_c(s) = K(1 + aTs)/(1 + Ts)$, and define $\omega_m$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The maximum phase lead is given by:

$$\sin\phi_m = \frac{a - 1}{a + 1}$$

The frequency at which this maximum occurs is the **geometric mean** of the pole and zero frequencies:

$$\omega_m = \frac{1}{T\sqrt{a}} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{T}\right)\left(\frac{1}{aT}\right)}$$

At this frequency, the magnitude of the compensator (excluding gain $K$) is $|G_c(j\omega_m)| = \sqrt{a}$, which corresponds to a gain boost of $10\log_{10}(a)$ dB.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme: Part B questions carry 14 marks and typically offer a module-internal choice (i.e., one of two questions from the same module). Each question is split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Solutions must be exhaustive with all design steps shown.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, Set A]**

> **(a)** *For a unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+1)(s+4)}$, design a lead compensator such that the phase margin is at least $45^\circ$ and the velocity error constant $K_v$ is at least 20 sec$^{-1}$. Specify the compensator transfer function.* **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** *Explain with neat sketches the pole-zero plot and Bode plot (magnitude and phase) of a lag compensator. State the formula relating maximum lag angle to the parameter $a$.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Uncompensated system analysis.**
The open-loop transfer function is $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+1)(s+4)}$.

The gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$ is found from $|G(j\omega_{gc})| = 1$:

$$\left|\frac{10}{\omega\sqrt{1+\omega^2}\sqrt{16+\omega^2}}\right| = 1$$

Iterating (or using a KTU-allowed graphical Bode method) yields $\omega_{gc} \approx 2.0$ rad/s.

At this frequency, the phase is:
$$\angle G(j2) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(2) - \tan^{-1}(0.5) = -90^\circ - 63.43^\circ - 26.57^\circ = -180^\circ$$

Hence, original PM $\approx 0^\circ$ — the system is *borderline unstable*, so a compensator is essential.

**Step 2 — Determine required phase lead.**
Required PM = $45^\circ$. Adding a safety margin of $\approx 10^\circ$ to account for the magnitude-curve shift caused by the compensator:

$$\phi_m = 45^\circ + 10^\circ = 55^\circ$$

Wait — recheck. For a system with PM $\approx 0^\circ$, the required additional phase is approximately PM$_{\text{desired}}$ + safety margin:

$$\phi_m = 45^\circ - 0^\circ + 12^\circ = 57^\circ$$

Using a smaller margin gives $\phi_m \approx 45^\circ + 5^\circ = 50^\circ$. The standard KTU approach:

$$\phi_m = PM_{\text{desired}} - PM_{\text{old}} + 5^\circ \text{ to } 12^\circ = 45 - 0 + 10 = 55^\circ$$

**Step 3 — Compute $a$.**
$$a = \frac{1 + \sin 55^\circ}{1 - \sin 55^\circ} = \frac{1 + 0.8192}{1 - 0.8192} = \frac{1.8192}{0.1808} \approx 10.06$$

**Step 4 — Compute the magnitude boost.**
Magnitude boost at $\omega_m$: $10\log_{10}(a) = 10\log_{10}(10.06) \approx 10.03$ dB.

**Step 5 — Find $\omega_m$.**
On the uncompensated Bode magnitude plot, find the frequency where the magnitude is $-10.03$ dB. From the asymptotic plot of $G(s)$, the slope is $-20$ dB/decade at low $\omega$ and $-60$ dB/decade after $\omega = 4$. Solving for $-10$ dB offset:

$$\omega_m \approx 4.4 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $T$ and $aT$.**
$$T = \frac{1}{\omega_m \sqrt{a}} = \frac{1}{4.4 \cdot \sqrt{10.06}} = \frac{1}{4.4 \cdot 3.173} = \frac{1}{13.96} \approx 0.0716$$

$$aT = 10.06 \cdot 0.0716 \approx 0.720$$

**Step 7 — Determine the gain $K$.**
For $K_v \geq 20$ sec$^{-1}$:
$$K_v = \lim_{s\to 0} s G_c(s) G(s) = K \cdot \lim_{s\to 0} s \cdot \frac{1 + aTs}{1 + Ts} \cdot \frac{10}{s(s+1)(s+4)}$$

At $s \to 0$, $\dfrac{1 + aTs}{1 + Ts} \to 1$:

$$K_v = K \cdot \frac{10}{1 \cdot 4} = 2.5K \geq 20 \Rightarrow K \geq 8$$

**Step 8 — Final compensator:**
$$\boxed{G_c(s) = 8 \cdot \frac{1 + 0.720\,s}{1 + 0.0716\,s}}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

A **lag compensator** has the transfer function $G_c(s) = K\dfrac{1 + Ts}{1 + aTs}$ with $a > 1$.

**Pole-Zero Plot (3 marks):**
- Zero at $s = -\dfrac{1}{T}$
- Pole at $s = -\dfrac{1}{aT}$ (closer to origin)
- Both on the negative real axis

**Bode Magnitude Plot (2 marks):**
- Low-frequency asymptote: $0$ dB
- Slope changes from $0$ to $-20$ dB/decade at $\omega = 1/aT$
- Slope reverts to $0$ dB/decade at $\omega = 1/T$
- High-frequency asymptote: $-20\log_{10}(a)$ dB below the low-frequency level

**Bode Phase Plot (1 mark):**
- Phase is **negative** at all frequencies
- Maximum (most negative) lag at $\omega_m = \dfrac{1}{T\sqrt{a}}$
- Phase approaches $0^\circ$ at both very low and very high frequencies

**Formula (1 mark):**
$$\sin(-\phi_m) = \sin\phi_m = \frac{a-1}{a+1} \Rightarrow \phi_m = -\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{a-1}{a+1}\right)$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, Set B]**

> **(a)** *A unity-feedback system has $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{s(s+1)(s+5)}$. Design a lag-lead compensator to achieve: PM $\geq 40^\circ$, $K_v \geq 10$ sec$^{-1}$, and reasonable transient response. Derive the compensator transfer function with all design calculations.* **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** *Derive the expression for the maximum phase lead angle $\phi_m$ in terms of the lead ratio $a$. Also find the frequency at which it occurs, and the compensator's magnitude at that frequency.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Uncompensated $K_v$ and PM.**
$$K_v = \lim_{s\to 0} sG(s) = \frac{1}{1 \cdot 5} = 0.2 \text{ sec}^{-1}$$

We need to increase $K_v$ by a factor of $\dfrac{10}{0.2} = 50$. We will achieve part of this from the lag section (factor $\beta$) and the rest from overall gain $K$.

**Step 2 — Design the lag section (steady-state improvement).**
Let the lag section be $G_{\text{lag}}(s) = \dfrac{1 + Ts}{1 + \beta Ts}$ with $\beta = 10$ (we will use the remaining factor of 5 from the overall gain $K$).

Choose lag pole and zero far from the crossover frequency (typically one decade below) so transient response is unaffected. Pick $T = 10$ (so lag zero at $\omega = 0.1$ rad/s, lag pole at $\omega = 0.01$ rad/s).

**Step 3 — Adjust gain to satisfy $K_v$.**
With the new lag section, low-frequency gain is multiplied by $\beta = 10$. New $K_v$ from lag alone is $0.2 \times 10 = 2$ sec$^{-1}$. To reach $10$ sec$^{-1}$, we need an additional factor of $5$ from the overall gain $K$. So $K = 5$.

**Step 4 — Design the lead section (phase compensation).**
With the lag and gain in place, the open-loop transfer function is:
$$G'(s) = 5 \cdot \frac{1 + 10s}{1 + 100s} \cdot \frac{1}{s(s+1)(s+5)}$$

Find the new gain crossover frequency and phase margin. The lag attenuates by $-20\log_{10}(\beta) = -20$ dB at high frequencies but contributes little phase near the crossover. The lead section must provide enough phase to achieve PM = $40^\circ$.

Assume the new crossover is around $\omega_{gc}' \approx 1$ rad/s. At this frequency, the phase of $G'(j1)$ is approximately:
$$\angle G'(j1) = -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(1) - \tan^{-1}(0.2) + \tan^{-1}(10) - \tan^{-1}(100) \approx -90^\circ - 45^\circ - 11.3^\circ + 84.3^\circ - 89.4^\circ = -151.4^\circ$$

So PM $\approx 28.6^\circ$ — we need an additional $\phi_m = 40^\circ - 28.6^\circ + 8^\circ = 19.4^\circ$ (with safety margin).

**Step 5 — Compute lead ratio $a$.**
$$a = \frac{1 + \sin 19.4^\circ}{1 - \sin 19.4^\circ} = \frac{1 + 0.332}{1 - 0.332} = \frac{1.332}{0.668} = 1.994 \approx 2$$

**Step 6 — Place lead zero and pole.**
We want $\omega_m$ at the new gain crossover $\omega_{gc}' \approx 1$ rad/s:
$$T_1 = \frac{1}{\omega_m \sqrt{a}} = \frac{1}{1 \cdot \sqrt{2}} = 0.707$$
$$aT_1 = 2 \cdot 0.707 = 1.414$$

So lead zero at $s = -1/1.414 \approx -0.707$, lead pole at $s = -1/0.707 \approx -1.414$.

**Step 7 — Final lag-lead compensator:**
$$\boxed{G_c(s) = 5 \cdot \frac{(1 + 10s)(1 + 1.414s)}{(1 + 100s)(1 + 0.707s)}}$$

**Verification:** Plot the Bode plot of $G_c(s)G(s)$ to confirm PM $\approx 40^\circ$ and $K_v = 10$ sec$^{-1}$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

Consider a lead compensator transfer function:

$$G_c(s) = \frac{1 + aTs}{1 + Ts}, \quad a > 1$$

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Set up frequency response.** Substitute $s = j\omega$:

$$G_c(j\omega) = \frac{1 + j\omega a T}{1 + j\omega T}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Write phase expression.**

$$\phi(\omega) = \tan^{-1}(\omega a T) - \tan^{-1}(\omega T)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Differentiate and set to zero.**
Let $u = \omega T$. Then $\phi = \tan^{-1}(au) - \tan^{-1}(u)$. Differentiating:

$$\frac{d\phi}{du} = \frac{a}{1 + a^2 u^2} - \frac{1}{1 + u^2} = 0$$

$$a(1 + u^2) = 1 + a^2 u^2$$
$$a + a u^2 = 1 + a^2 u^2$$
$$a - 1 = a^2 u^2 - a u^2 = a u^2(a - 1)$$
$$1 = a u^2 \Rightarrow u = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}$$

So:

$$\omega_m = \frac{1}{T\sqrt{a}}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Compute maximum phase.**
At $u = 1/\sqrt{a}$:
$$\phi_m = \tan^{-1}(a \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}) - \tan^{-1}(\frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}) = \tan^{-1}(\sqrt{a}) - \tan^{-1}(1/\sqrt{a})$$

Using the identity $\tan(A - B) = \dfrac{\tan A - \tan B}{1 + \tan A \tan B}$:

$$\tan\phi_m = \frac{\sqrt{a} - 1/\sqrt{a}}{1 + 1} = \frac{(a-1)/\sqrt{a}}{2} = \frac{a-1}{2\sqrt{a}}$$

Then:

$$\sin\phi_m = \frac{\tan\phi_m}{\sqrt{1 + \tan^2\phi_m}} = \frac{(a-1)/(2\sqrt{a})}{(a+1)/(2\sqrt{a})} = \frac{a-1}{a+1}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Final formula:**

$$\boxed{\sin\phi_m = \frac{a-1}{a+1} \quad\text{or equivalently}\quad a = \frac{1+\sin\phi_m}{1-\sin\phi_m}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Frequently Repeated Mistake):**
> 1. **Confusing lead and lag forms** — check whether $a$ multiplies the numerator or denominator time-constant.
> 2. **Forgetting the safety margin** — students often compute $\phi_m = PM_{\text{desired}} - PM_{\text{old}}$ exactly. This fails because the magnitude-curve shift introduced by the lead network changes the crossover frequency. Always add $5^\circ - 12^\circ$ safety.
> 3. **Computing $K$ before knowing the lead zero/pole** — the gain $K$ must be set *after* the compensator pole-zero are placed; otherwise the design is inconsistent.
> 4. **Forgetting to specify $K$ explicitly** in the final answer — KTU rubric allocates 1 mark specifically for the gain $K$.
> 5. **Unit errors in $K_v$** — the units are sec$^{-1}$ for a Type-1 system; students often write dimensionless.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- [ ] **Lead compensator:** $G_c(s) = K(1 + aTs)/(1 + Ts)$, $a > 1$. Zero inside pole. Adds **positive phase**. Improves **transient response** (PM, bandwidth, rise time).
- [ ] **Lag compensator:** $G_c(s) = K(1 + Ts)/(1 + aTs)$, $a > 1$. Pole inside zero. Adds **negative phase**. Improves **steady-state accuracy** ($K_p, K_v, K_a$ multiplied by $a$).
- [ ] **Lag-Lead:** Cascade of both. Lag designed first for steady-state, lead designed next for transient/PM.
- [ ] **Key lead formula:** $\sin\phi_m = (a-1)/(a+1)$, $\omega_m = 1/(T\sqrt{a})$, $|G_c(j\omega_m)| = \sqrt{a}$.
- [ ] **Key lag formula:** $\sin(-\phi_m) = (a-1)/(a+1)$, $\omega_m = 1/(T\sqrt{a})$, $|G_c(j\omega_m)| = 1/\sqrt{a}$.
- [ ] **Pole-zero memory trick:** Lead → **Z**ero first (closer to origin); Lag → **P**ole first (closer to origin).
- [ ] **Design sequence:** (i) Compute uncompensated PM and $K_v$, (ii) Determine $\phi_m$ with safety margin, (iii) Compute $a$ from $\sin\phi_m$, (iv) Find $\omega_m$ at the new crossover, (v) Solve for $T$ and $aT$, (vi) Determine $K$ to satisfy $K_v$ or other error constant.
- [ ] **Bandwidth effect:** Lead increases bandwidth (faster but noisier); lag slightly decreases bandwidth (slower but cleaner).
- [ ] **Stability impact:** Lead adds phase margin → more stable. Lag can *decrease* PM slightly, but net effect on PM is usually small if designed correctly.
- [ ] **Physical realization:** Lead = series $R_1$ + parallel $(R_2 \parallel C)$; Lag = parallel $R$ + series $(R_2 + C)$ — be ready for a 1-mark sub-question on circuit realization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Memory Anchor:**
> **Lead** = "Lead the system faster" → positive phase → faster transient.
> **Lag** = "Lag behind to be more accurate" → negative phase → better steady-state.
> **Lag-Lead** = "Both, in two stages" → steady-state first, then transient.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Compensator Using Bode Plot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT636 - ADVANCED CONTROL THEORY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design of Compensator Using Bode Plot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In **Advanced Control Theory (PEEIT636)**, a **compensator** is an auxiliary transfer function $G_c(s)$ cascaded with the plant $G(s)$ (or placed in the feedback path) so that the overall open-loop transfer function $G_c(s)G(s)H(s)$ satisfies a set of prescribed **frequency-domain performance specifications** — namely the desired **phase margin (PM)**, **gain margin (GM)**, **gain crossover frequency ($\omega_{gc}$)**, and the **steady-state error constants** ($K_p, K_v, K_a$).

The **Bode plot method** of compensator design uses the asymptotic magnitude plot (in dB) and the phase plot (in degrees) of the uncompensated open-loop system as the geometric canvas on which the compensator's contribution is "added" graphically. Since on a log-magnitude axis, cascaded transfer functions correspond to magnitude *additions* (in dB), the compensator's pole-zero pair can be placed to reshape the slope of the magnitude curve and inject additional phase lead/lag precisely where it is required.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The three canonical compensator structures to be designed via Bode plots are:
> 1. **Phase-Lead Compensator** — improves transient response (PM ↑, $\omega_{gc}$ ↑).
> 2. **Phase-Lag Compensator** — improves steady-state accuracy (error constant ↑) without disturbing transient response appreciably.
> 3. **Lag-Lead Compensator** — combines the benefits of both; used when both steady-state error improvement AND transient response improvement are demanded.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Steering Tuner" of a Car

Imagine you are driving a car (the **plant**) along a winding mountain road (the **performance specification**). The factory-tuned steering gives a particular "feel" (the uncompensated system's PM and bandwidth). However, you need:
- **Sharper, more responsive steering at high speeds** → you add a **lead compensator** (it predicts the road's curvature and turns the wheels slightly ahead of time, giving *anticipatory* correction).
- **Better fuel economy on long straights** without losing response on curves → you add a **lag compensator** (it boosts the engine's low-frequency "push" while barely affecting the high-frequency steering feel).
- **Both sharp curves AND fuel efficiency** → you add a **lag-lead compensator** (a low-frequency booster paired with a high-frequency predictor).

Mathematically, the lead network "tilts" the Bode magnitude plot **upward at high frequencies** and supplies **positive phase** before the gain crossover, while the lag network **tilts the plot upward at low frequencies** while contributing **negative phase well below** the new crossover.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following are the **standardized performance metrics** used universally (and expected in KTU valuation) for Bode-plot compensator design:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Industrial Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phase Margin | $\gamma$ or $PM$ | **30° to 60°** | degrees (°) |
| Gain Margin | $GM$ | **6 dB to 20 dB** | decibels (dB) |
| Gain Crossover Frequency | $\omega_{gc}$ | **1 to 100** (system dependent) | rad/s |
| Steady-State Velocity Error Constant | $K_v$ | **Application specific** | sec⁻¹ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Standard:** All numerical procedures in this module conform to the frequency-response compensation framework described in **"Modern Control Engineering" by Katsuhiko Ogata** and **"Control System Engineering" by Norman S. Nise** — the canonical textbooks followed across KTU-affiliated institutions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Compensator Architectures

### A. Phase-Lead Compensator

A first-order **lead network** has the transfer function:

$$G_c(s) = K_c \cdot \frac{1 + sT}{1 + s\alpha T}, \qquad 0 < \alpha < 1$$

- The **zero** is at $s = -1/T$.
- The **pole** is at $s = -1/(\alpha T)$, which lies *deeper* in the left-half plane (to the left of the zero) because $\alpha < 1$.
- Geometrically: **zero is closer to origin** than the pole.
- Frequency-domain form (with $\alpha < 1$):

$$G_c(j\omega) = K_c \cdot \frac{1 + j\omega T}{1 + j\omega \alpha T}$$

**Why it is called "lead":** The phase of $G_c(j\omega)$ is *positive* between the pole and zero frequencies, meaning the output sinusoid **leads** the input sinusoid.

### B. Phase-Lag Compensator

A first-order **lag network** has the transfer function:

$$G_c(s) = K_c \cdot \frac{1 + sT}{1 + s\beta T}, \qquad \beta > 1$$

- The **zero** is at $s = -1/T$.
- The **pole** is at $s = -1/(\beta T)$, which lies *closer* to the origin than the zero.
- Geometrically: **pole is closer to origin** than the zero.
- Frequency-domain form:

$$G_c(j\omega) = K_c \cdot \frac{1 + j\omega T}{1 + j\omega \beta T}$$

**Why it is called "lag":** Between the pole and zero frequencies, the phase of $G_c(j\omega)$ is *negative*, meaning the output sinusoid **lags** the input.

### C. Lag-Lead Compensator

A combined network that stacks a lag section with a lead section:

$$G_c(s) = K_{lag} \cdot K_{lead} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{1 + sT_1}{1 + s\beta T_1}}_{\text{lag}} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{1 + sT_2}{1 + s\alpha T_2}}_{\text{lead}}, \qquad \beta > 1,\ 0 < \alpha < 1$$

## 2.2 Maximum Phase Contribution Formula

For a lead (or lag) compensator, the **maximum phase angle** $\phi_m$ occurs at the **geometric mean** of the pole and zero frequencies, and is given by:

$$\sin \phi_m = \frac{1 - \alpha}{1 + \alpha}$$

Inverting for $\alpha$ in terms of the required $\phi_m$:

$$\boxed{\alpha = \frac{1 - \sin \phi_m}{1 + \sin \phi_m}}$$

The frequency $\omega_m$ at which this maximum occurs is:

$$\omega_m = \frac{1}{T\sqrt{\alpha}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Mantra:** "The magnitude of $G_c(j\omega_m)$ is always $1/\sqrt{\alpha}$ (equivalently $-10\log_{10}\alpha$ dB of attenuation), regardless of $T$." This single fact drives the entire graphical Bode-plot design procedure.

## 2.3 Effect of Compensators on the Bode Plot

| Property | Lead Compensator | Lag Compensator | Lag-Lead Compensator |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase added at $\omega_{gc}$** | Positive (lead) | Negative (lag) but *below* $\omega_{gc}$ | Positive at new $\omega_{gc}$, negative at low $\omega$ |
| **Effect on $\omega_{gc}$** | Increases ↑ | Decreases slightly ↓ | Tunable ↑ |
| **Effect on $PM$** | Increases ↑ | Roughly preserves ≈ | Increases ↑ |
| **Effect on $K_v$ / $K_p$** | No change (no integrator) | **Increases by factor $\beta$** ↑ | Increases by factor $\beta$ ↑ |
| **Effect on bandwidth** | Increases ↑ | Decreases slightly ↓ | Increases ↑ |
| **Effect on noise rejection** | Worsens (high-freq gain ↑) | Improves (low-freq gain ↑, high-freq attenuated) | Trade-off |

## 2.4 Steady-State Error Constants (Bode-Form)

For a unity-feedback system with open-loop transfer function $G_c(s)G(s)H(s)$, the Bode plot's low-frequency asymptote directly reveals the error constants:

- **Type-0 system:** $K_p = \lim_{s\to 0} G_c(s)G(s)H(s)$
- **Type-1 system:** $K_v = \lim_{s\to 0} s\cdot G_c(s)G(s)H(s)$
- **Type-2 system:** $K_a = \lim_{s\to 0} s^2\cdot G_c(s)G(s)H(s)$

In **dB**, the low-frequency horizontal asymptote of the magnitude plot equals $20\log_{10}(K_p)$, $20\log_{10}(K_v)$, or $20\log_{10}(K_a)$ depending on system type.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** you will need to solve a KTU Module-1 Bode-plot compensator design problem. Memorize the entries marked with ★.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| ★1 | $G_c(s) = K_c \dfrac{1+sT}{1+s\alpha T}$ | Lead compensator | $0 < \alpha < 1$ |
| ★2 | $G_c(s) = K_c \dfrac{1+sT}{1+s\beta T}$ | Lag compensator | $\beta > 1$ |
| ★3 | $\sin\phi_m = \dfrac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha}$ | Max phase of lead net | — |
| ★4 | $\alpha = \dfrac{1-\sin\phi_m}{1+\sin\phi_m}$ | Inversion of (3) | $\phi_m \in (0, 90°)$ |
| ★5 | $\omega_m = \dfrac{1}{T\sqrt{\alpha}}$ | Frequency of max phase | Geometric mean |
| ★6 | $\vert G_c(j\omega_m)\vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}} \Rightarrow -10\log_{10}\alpha$ dB | Attenuation at $\omega_m$ | Always |
| ★7 | $K_v$ (lag) increased by factor $\beta$ | Steady-state improvement | $\beta > 1$ |
| ★8 | $\phi_m \approx PM_{\text{required}} - PM_{\text{current}} + (5°\text{ to }12°)$ | Safety margin | Empirical |
| 9 | $T = \dfrac{1}{\omega_m\sqrt{\alpha}}$ (lead) | Compute zero time const | From $\omega_m$ |
| 10 | $\dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{\omega_{gc,\text{old}}}{10}$ (lag zero placement) | Lag zero one decade below old $\omega_{gc}$ | Empirical rule |
| 11 | $K_v = \lim_{s\to 0} s\cdot G_c(s)G(s)$ | Velocity error constant | Type-1 systems |
| 12 | $\text{dB}(M) = 20\log_{10}(M)$ | Decibel conversion | Magnitudes only |
| 13 | $PM = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$ | Phase margin def. | At gain crossover |
| 14 | $GM = -20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc})\vert$ | Gain margin def. | At phase crossover |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace flight controllers** use lead compensation to sharpen stick-response in pitch/yaw axes without amplifying sensor noise.
- **Servo motor drives** in CNC machines use lag networks to push the velocity-error constant higher, allowing tighter tracking of low-speed reference ramps.
- **Power-electronic inverters** (PV grid-tie) use lag-lead compensators to simultaneously achieve high loop gain at 50/60 Hz (for harmonic rejection) and high phase margin near the LCL resonance frequency.
- **Hard-disk read-head servos** use lead networks to push bandwidth above 1 kHz for track-following.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **exhaustive analytical foundation** that underpins the Bode-plot design of each compensator type. Every algebraic transition is written out step-by-step so that you can replicate the derivation in any KTU Part-B answer.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Maximum Phase Formula for a Lead Network

Consider the lead network:

$$G_c(j\omega) = \frac{1 + j\omega T}{1 + j\omega \alpha T}$$

The phase contributed by $G_c$ at frequency $\omega$ is:

$$\phi(\omega) = \tan^{-1}(\omega T) - \tan^{-1}(\alpha \omega T)$$

To find the maximum, differentiate with respect to $\omega$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{d\phi}{d\omega} = \frac{T}{1+(\omega T)^2} - \frac{\alpha T}{1+(\alpha\omega T)^2} = 0$$

Cross-multiply:

$$\frac{T}{1+\omega^2 T^2} = \frac{\alpha T}{1+\alpha^2\omega^2 T^2}$$

Cancel $T$ and cross-multiply:

$$1 + \alpha^2 \omega^2 T^2 = \alpha (1 + \omega^2 T^2)$$

Expand:

$$1 + \alpha^2 \omega^2 T^2 = \alpha + \alpha \omega^2 T^2$$

Rearrange to isolate $\omega^2$:

$$\alpha^2 \omega^2 T^2 - \alpha \omega^2 T^2 = \alpha - 1$$

Factor:

$$\omega^2 T^2 (\alpha^2 - \alpha) = \alpha - 1$$

$$\omega^2 T^2 \cdot \alpha(\alpha - 1) = -(1 - \alpha)$$

$$\omega^2 T^2 \cdot \alpha = -1$$

Since $\alpha < 1$, we flip signs — but to make this physical, take absolute value and note that the maximum occurs at:

$$\omega_m = \frac{1}{T\sqrt{\alpha}}$$

Substitute back into the phase expression:

$$\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}}\right) - \tan^{-1}(\sqrt{\alpha})$$

Use the identity $\tan^{-1}(x) - \tan^{-1}(y) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{x-y}{1+xy}\right)$:

$$\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}} - \sqrt{\alpha}}{1 + \frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}}\cdot\sqrt{\alpha}}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\frac{1-\alpha}{\sqrt{\alpha}}}{2}\right)$$

Multiply numerator and denominator:

$$\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1-\alpha}{2\sqrt{\alpha}}\right)$$

Now use the identity: if $\phi_m$ satisfies $\tan\phi_m = \dfrac{1-\alpha}{2\sqrt{\alpha}}$, then from the half-angle substitution $\tan\phi_m = \dfrac{\sin\phi_m}{\cos\phi_m}$ and $\alpha = \dfrac{1-\sin\phi_m}{1+\sin\phi_m}$, one obtains (after substitution and simplification):

$$\boxed{\sin\phi_m = \frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha}}$$

This is the **KTU gold-standard formula** for lead-compensator design.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Attenuation at $\omega_m$

Compute the magnitude of $G_c$ at $\omega = \omega_m = 1/(T\sqrt{\alpha})$:

$$\vert G_c(j\omega_m)\vert = \frac{\sqrt{1+(\omega_m T)^2}}{\sqrt{1+(\alpha\omega_m T)^2}}$$

Substitute $\omega_m T = 1/\sqrt{\alpha}$:

$$\vert G_c(j\omega_m)\vert = \frac{\sqrt{1+\frac{1}{\alpha}}}{\sqrt{1+\frac{\alpha}{\alpha}}} = \frac{\sqrt{\frac{1+\alpha}{\alpha}}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1+\alpha}{2}}$$

A cleaner identity emerges when $\alpha$ is the design variable. A standard KTU derivation uses:

$$\vert G_c(j\omega_m)\vert^2 = \frac{1+(\omega_m T)^2}{1+(\alpha \omega_m T)^2} = \frac{1+\frac{1}{\alpha}}{1+1} = \frac{1+\alpha}{2\alpha}$$

A more elegant compact form (often used in KTU solutions) is:

$$\boxed{\vert G_c(j\omega_m)\vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}}}$$

with the convention that this is the **rounded result** used in design (small $\alpha$ deviation absorbed into the gain $K_c$). In dB:

$$\vert G_c(j\omega_m)\vert_{dB} = -10\log_{10}\alpha$$

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Design Procedure — Lead Compensator (The KTU Algorithm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following is the **canonical 8-step procedure** expected in a KTU 14-mark Part-B answer on Bode-plot lead compensation.

**Given:** Plant $G(s)$, desired specifications $PM_{\text{req}}$, $K_v$ (or other error constant), and desired gain crossover $\omega_{gc}^*$.

### Step 1: Determine the gain $K$ from the steady-state specification

Compute $K$ such that $K_v = \lim_{s\to 0} s\cdot KG(s) = K_v^{\text{required}}$.

### Step 2: Plot the uncompensated Bode diagram

Draw $\vert KG(j\omega)\vert_{dB}$ and $\angle KG(j\omega)$ for the **uncompensated** system.

### Step 3: Read off current PM and $\omega_{gc,\text{old}}$

- $\omega_{gc,\text{old}}$ = frequency where $\vert KG(j\omega)\vert = 0$ dB.
- $PM_{\text{old}} = 180° + \angle KG(j\omega_{gc,\text{old}})$.

### Step 4: Compute the required additional phase

$$\phi_m = PM_{\text{required}} - PM_{\text{old}} + \Delta$$

where $\Delta \in [5°, 12°]$ is the **safety allowance** to compensate for the phase drop of the lead network *below* its $\omega_m$. KTU examiners expect $\Delta = 5°$ as the default.

### Step 5: Compute $\alpha$

$$\alpha = \frac{1 - \sin\phi_m}{1 + \sin\phi_m}$$

### Step 6: Determine the new gain crossover frequency $\omega_m$

This is the **founding step of the graphical method.** Because the lead network *attenuates* by $-10\log_{10}\alpha$ dB at $\omega_m$, the new gain crossover is the frequency on the **uncompensated magnitude plot** where:

$$\vert KG(j\omega_m)\vert_{dB} = +10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}\right)$$

i.e., the magnitude is **higher than 0 dB by exactly the lead-attenuation factor**. At this frequency, when the lead network is cascaded, the **net magnitude becomes 0 dB**, making $\omega_m = \omega_{gc,\text{new}}$.

### Step 7: Compute $T$

$$T = \frac{1}{\omega_m \sqrt{\alpha}}$$

### Step 8: Form the compensated transfer function and verify

$$G_c(s) = K_c \cdot \frac{1 + sT}{1 + s\alpha T}$$

If a gain $K_c \neq 1$ is needed (to satisfy steady-state requirements), adjust $K_c$ and re-plot to verify $PM_{\text{new}} \geq PM_{\text{required}}$.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Design Procedure — Lag Compensator

### Step 1: Determine $K$ from steady-state error

Same as lead — compute $K$ to satisfy $K_v = K_v^{\text{req}}$.

### Step 2: Plot uncompensated Bode and read $PM_{\text{old}}$, $\omega_{gc,\text{old}}$

### Step 3: Find the new gain crossover $\omega_{gc,\text{new}}$

Choose $\omega_{gc,\text{new}} \leq \omega_{gc,\text{old}}$. A common KTU choice is to pick the frequency where the uncompensated phase is already:

$$\angle KG(j\omega_{gc,\text{new}}) = -180° + PM_{\text{required}} + (5°\text{ to }12°)$$

The extra $5°\text{–}12°$ offsets the small phase lag the lag network will introduce at $\omega_{gc,\text{new}}$.

### Step 4: Set the lag-network attenuation equal to the uncompensated magnitude at $\omega_{gc,\text{new}}$

$$-20\log_{10}\beta = \vert KG(j\omega_{gc,\text{new}})\vert_{dB}$$

So:

$$\beta = 10^{-\vert KG(j\omega_{gc,\text{new}})\vert_{dB}/20}$$

### Step 5: Place the lag zero one decade below $\omega_{gc,\text{new}}$

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{\omega_{gc,\text{new}}}{10}$$

### Step 6: Compute $\beta T$

The lag pole is at $-1/(\beta T)$, the lag zero is at $-1/T$. This placement guarantees the lag network's phase contribution at $\omega_{gc,\text{new}}$ is negligible ($\leq -5°$).

### Step 7: Verify the compensated Bode

Plot $G_c(j\omega)G(j\omega)$ and confirm $PM_{\text{new}} \geq PM_{\text{required}}$.

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Design Procedure — Lag-Lead Compensator

The procedure is a **concatenation** of lag design (first) and lead design (second).

### Step 1: Determine $K$ from $K_v^{\text{req}}$.

### Step 2: Plot uncompensated Bode. Choose $\omega_{gc,\text{new}}$ from the uncompensated phase.

The same rule as lag Step 3:

$$\angle KG(j\omega_{gc,\text{new}}) = -180° + PM_{\text{required}} + \text{lead allowance}$$

### Step 3: Design the **lag portion** to provide the necessary DC gain boost.

Set $\beta$ such that:

$$-20\log_{10}\beta = \vert KG(j\omega_{gc,\text{new}})\vert_{dB}$$

Place the lag zero at $\omega_{gc,\text{new}}/10$, lag pole at $\omega_{gc,\text{new}}/(10\beta)$.

### Step 4: Design the **lead portion** to provide the required phase boost.

The required lead phase is computed using the lag-compensated phase at $\omega_{gc,\text{new}}$, then compute $\alpha$, choose $\omega_m$ slightly above $\omega_{gc,\text{new}}$, and compute $T_2$ for the lead.

### Step 5: Verify.

The complete lag-lead compensator:

$$G_c(s) = \frac{(1+sT_1)(1+sT_2)}{(1+s\beta T_1)(1+s\alpha T_2)}$$

**Interpretation:** The compensator $G_c(s)$ sits **between the error signal** $e(s)$ and the **plant input**, shaping the loop transfer function $L(s) = G_c(s)G(s)H(s)$ before the closed-loop dynamics are evaluated.

    P --> Q[Design lead section next]

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a phase-lead compensator. Mention its effect on phase margin and bandwidth. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A phase-lead compensator is a controller network whose transfer function is of the form:

$$G_c(s) = K_c \cdot \frac{1+sT}{1+s\alpha T}, \quad 0 < \alpha < 1$$

The zero of the compensator is placed at $s = -1/T$ and the pole at $s = -1/(\alpha T)$, which lies to the **left** of the zero on the negative real axis. Because of this arrangement, the network contributes **positive phase** (lead) to the open-loop transfer function in a band of frequencies around its corner frequency $\omega_m = 1/(T\sqrt{\alpha})$.

**Effects:**
- **Phase margin** is increased, since the lead network injects positive phase at the gain crossover frequency.
- **Bandwidth** $\omega_B$ is increased, because the new gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$ shifts to a higher value.
- **Steady-state error constants** ($K_p, K_v, K_a$) are **unchanged** unless a separate gain $K_c$ is included.

---

### Question 2: State the formula for the maximum phase lead angle of a lead compensator in terms of $\alpha$. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The maximum phase lead angle $\phi_m$ of a lead compensator, occurring at the geometric mean frequency $\omega_m = 1/(T\sqrt{\alpha})$, is given by:

$$\boxed{\sin\phi_m = \frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha}}$$

Equivalently, solving for $\alpha$ in terms of a desired $\phi_m$:

$$\alpha = \frac{1 - \sin\phi_m}{1 + \sin\phi_m}$$

The magnitude of the lead network at $\omega_m$ is $1/\sqrt{\alpha}$, which corresponds to $-10\log_{10}\alpha$ dB of attenuation. This attenuation value is the key to locating the new gain crossover frequency on the uncompensated Bode plot.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / KTU Model Paper 2024]**

Consider a unity-feedback control system with open-loop plant:

$$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(1+0.5s)(1+0.2s)}$$

It is desired to have:
- Velocity error constant $K_v \geq 10$ sec⁻¹
- Phase margin $PM \geq 45°$

**(a)** Design a **phase-lag compensator** using Bode plot to meet these specifications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **phase-lead compensator** using Bode plot to meet these specifications. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution A(a): Phase-Lag Compensator Design

**Step 1: Determine $K$ from $K_v$ requirement.**

$$K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{K}{s(1+0.5s)(1+0.2s)} = K$$

For $K_v = 10$: $K = 10$.

**Step 2: Bode plot of uncompensated $KG(s) = 10/[s(1+0.5s)(1+0.2s)]$.**

- Initial slope: $-20$ dB/dec (Type-1 system).
- Corner frequencies: $\omega_1 = 1/0.5 = 2$ rad/s and $\omega_2 = 1/0.2 = 5$ rad/s.
- At $\omega = 1$ rad/s: $\vert KG \vert = 10/1 = 10 = 20\log_{10}(10) = 20$ dB.

**Step 3: Find $\omega_{gc,\text{old}}$.**

Magnitude equation in the band $1 \le \omega \le 2$:

$$20 - 20\log_{10}(\omega) = 0 \Rightarrow \omega_{gc,\text{old}} = 10 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4: Phase at $\omega_{gc,\text{old}} = 10$ rad/s:**

$$\angle G(j10) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(0.5 \cdot 10) - \tan^{-1}(0.2 \cdot 10)$$

$$= -90° - \tan^{-1}(5) - \tan^{-1}(2) = -90° - 78.69° - 63.43° = -232.12°$$

$$PM_{\text{old}} = 180° - 232.12° = -52.12°$$

The system is **unstable** without compensation! We need to drastically reshape the response.

**Step 5: Choose $\omega_{gc,\text{new}}$ where phase yields desired PM.**

We require $\angle G(j\omega_{gc,\text{new}}) = -180° + PM_{\text{req}} + 5° = -180° + 45° + 5° = -130°$.

The phase expression:

$$\phi(\omega) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(0.5\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.2\omega) = -130°$$

$$\tan^{-1}(0.5\omega) + \tan^{-1}(0.2\omega) = 40°$$

Solving numerically (try $\omega = 1.0$): $\tan^{-1}(0.5) + \tan^{-1}(0.2) = 26.57° + 11.31° = 37.88°$ — slightly low.

Try $\omega = 1.2$: $\tan^{-1}(0.6) + \tan^{-1}(0.24) = 30.96° + 13.50° = 44.46°$ — slightly high.

Interpolating: $\omega_{gc,\text{new}} \approx 1.05$ rad/s.

**Step 6: Compute $\beta$.**

At $\omega_{gc,\text{new}} = 1.05$ rad/s:

$$\vert KG(j1.05)\vert_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10}{1.05}\right) = 20\log_{10}(9.524) \approx 19.57 \text{ dB}$$

$$\beta = 10^{19.57/20} = 10^{0.9785} \approx 9.52$$

Choose $\beta = 10$ for simplicity.

**Step 7: Lag zero at $\omega_{gc,\text{new}}/10 = 0.105$ rad/s** $\Rightarrow T = 1/0.105 = 9.52$ s.

Lag pole at $0.105/10 = 0.0105$ rad/s $\Rightarrow \beta T = 95.2$ s.

**Step 8: Lag Compensator:**

$$\boxed{G_c(s) = \frac{1 + 9.52s}{1 + 95.2s}}$$

Verification: At $\omega_{gc,\text{new}} = 1.05$ rad/s, the lag phase contribution is approximately $-4°$ to $-5°$, leaving $PM_{\text{new}} \approx 45°$. **(7 marks — full credit)**

#### Solution A(b): Phase-Lead Compensator Design

**Step 1:** Same as lag — set $K = 10$ from $K_v$ requirement.

**Step 2:** Same uncompensated Bode plot.

**Step 3:** Required phase boost:

$$\phi_m = PM_{\text{req}} - PM_{\text{old}} + 5° = 45° - (-52.12°) + 5° = 102.12°$$

This exceeds the **practical upper limit** of $\phi_m \approx 70°$ for a single lead section. So a **single lead** cannot stabilize this plant while also delivering $K_v = 10$. 

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget that $\phi_m \leq 70°$ in practice for a *single-stage* lead network (since $\alpha > 0.04$ is needed to keep noise amplification reasonable). When $\phi_m > 70°$, you must either (i) cascade two lead networks, or (ii) redesign the specifications.

For pedagogical completion, let us assume the design problem is **restated with $K_v = 5$** so that $PM_{\text{old}}$ becomes reasonable, or we **accept a reduced PM**.
