The following Mermaid diagram conceptually represents the pole placement action — moving poles from open-loop positions to desired closed-loop positions in the s-plane.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    openPole1[Open Loop Pole s1 at sigma1 j omega1] --> moveArrow1[Translate via State Feedback K]
    openPole2[Open Loop Pole s2 at sigma2 j omega2] --> moveArrow2[Translate via State Feedback K]
    moveArrow1 --> closedPole1[Desired Closed Loop Pole at minus 1 plus j]
    moveArrow2 --> closedPole2[Desired Closed Loop Pole at minus 1 minus j]
    closedPole1 --> response1[Response Damped Oscillation zeta equals 0.707]
    closedPole2 --> response2[Response Damped Oscillation zeta equals 0.707]
```

**Engineering Insight:** The state feedback gain $\mathbf{K}$ acts as a *geometric transformation* on the eigenvalue positions. The **magnitude** of $\mathbf{K}$ is inversely related to how far the closed-loop poles are pushed from the open-loop poles.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State the necessary and sufficient condition for arbitrary pole placement in a state feedback control system. Define the controllability matrix for an $n$-th order single-input system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The necessary and sufficient condition for arbitrary pole placement is that the pair $(\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B})$ must be **completely state controllable**, i.e., the controllability matrix $\mathcal{C}$ must have **full rank**.

$$\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}) = n$$

The controllability matrix for an $n$-th order single-input system is:

$$\mathcal{C} = \begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{B} & \mathbf{AB} & \mathbf{A}^2\mathbf{B} & \cdots & \mathbf{A}^{n-1}\mathbf{B}\end{bmatrix}_{n \times n}$$

If the system has multiple inputs ($m > 1$), the matrix is $n \times nm$ and must still have rank $n$. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Write Ackermann's formula for computing the state feedback gain matrix $\mathbf{K}$ for a single-input controllable system. Explain the meaning of $\alpha_d(\mathbf{A})$ in the formula.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Ackermann's formula is:

$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix}0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}_{1 \times n} \mathcal{C}^{-1} \alpha_d(\mathbf{A})$$

**Explanation:** $\alpha_d(\mathbf{A})$ is the **matrix polynomial** obtained by substituting the system matrix $\mathbf{A}$ into the desired characteristic polynomial $\alpha_d(s) = s^n + \alpha_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + \alpha_n$, treating the scalar variable $s$ as the matrix $\mathbf{A}$:

$$\alpha_d(\mathbf{A}) = \mathbf{A}^n + \alpha_1 \mathbf{A}^{n-1} + \cdots + \alpha_{n-1}\mathbf{A} + \alpha_n \mathbf{I}$$

It is an $n \times n$ matrix whose product with $\mathcal{C}^{-1}$ yields the gain vector. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Modified]`

**(a)** Consider the LTI system:

$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix}0 & 1 \\ 0 & -5\end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix}0 \\ 10\end{bmatrix}$$

Verify controllability and design a state feedback gain $\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix}k_1 & k_2\end{bmatrix}$ such that the closed-loop poles are at $s = -10 \pm j10$. Use **Ackermann's formula**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same system, suppose the desired closed-loop poles are changed to $s_1 = -8$ and $s_2 = -4$. Compute the new gain matrix using the **Bass-Gura formula**. Verify the design. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Controllability Check (2 marks)**

$$\mathcal{C} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{B} & \mathbf{A}\mathbf{B} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0 & 10 \\ 10 & -50\end{bmatrix}$$

$$\det(\mathcal{C}) = (0)(-50) - (10)(10) = -100 \neq 0$$

So $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}) = 2$ and the system is **completely controllable**. ✓

**Step 2: Desired Characteristic Polynomial (1 mark)**

For poles at $s = -10 \pm j10$:

$$\alpha_d(s) = (s+10-j10)(s+10+j10) = (s+10)^2 + 100 = s^2 + 20s + 200$$

So $\alpha_1 = 20$ and $\alpha_2 = 200$.

**Step 3: Apply Ackermann's Formula (3 marks)**

Compute $\mathcal{C}^{-1}$:

$$\mathcal{C}^{-1} = \frac{1}{-100}\begin{bmatrix}-50 & -10 \\ -10 & 0\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0.5 & 0.1 \\ 0.1 & 0\end{bmatrix}$$

Compute $\alpha_d(\mathbf{A}) = \mathbf{A}^2 + 20\mathbf{A} + 200\mathbf{I}$:

$$\mathbf{A}^2 = \begin{bmatrix}0 & 1 \\ 0 & -5\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}0 & 1 \\ 0 & -5\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0 & -5 \\ 0 & 25\end{bmatrix}$$

$$20\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix}0 & 20 \\ 0 & -100\end{bmatrix}, \quad 200\mathbf{I} = \begin{bmatrix}200 & 0 \\ 0 & 200\end{bmatrix}$$

$$\alpha_d(\mathbf{A}) = \begin{bmatrix}0 & -5 \\ 0 & 25\end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix}0 & 20 \\ 0 & -100\end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix}200 & 0 \\ 0 & 200\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}200 & 15 \\ 0 & 125\end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix}0 & 1\end{bmatrix} \mathcal{C}^{-1} \alpha_d(\mathbf{A}) = \begin{bmatrix}0 & 1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}0.5 & 0.1 \\ 0.1 & 0\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}200 & 15 \\ 0 & 125\end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix}0.1 & 0\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}200 & 15 \\ 0 & 125\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}20 & 1.5\end{bmatrix}$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix}20 & 1.5\end{bmatrix}}$$

**Step 4: Verification (1 mark)**

$$\mathbf{A}_{cl} = \mathbf{A} - \mathbf{B}\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix}0 & 1 \\ 0 & -5\end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix}0 \\ 10\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}20 & 1.5\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0 & 1 \\ -200 & -20\end{bmatrix}$$

$$\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}_{cl}) = s(s+20) + 200 = s^2 + 20s + 200 \quad \checkmark$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Open-Loop Polynomial (1 mark)**

$$\alpha_o(s) = \det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = s(s+5) - 0 = s^2 + 5s$$

So $a_1 = 5$ and $a_2 = 0$.

**Step 2: Desired Polynomial (1 mark)**

$$\alpha_d(s) = (s+8)(s+4) = s^2 + 12s + 32$$

So $\alpha_1 = 12$ and $\alpha_2 = 32$.

**Step 3: Bass-Gura Transformation (2 marks)**

$$\mathbf{W} = \begin{bmatrix}a_1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}5 & 1 \\ 1 & 0\end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{T} = \mathcal{C}\mathbf{W} = \begin{bmatrix}0 & 10 \\ 10 & -50\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}5 & 1 \\ 1 & 0\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}10 & 0 \\ 0 & 10\end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{T}^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix}0.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Apply Bass-Gura (2 marks)**

$$\begin{bmatrix}\alpha_2 - a_2 & \alpha_1 - a_1\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}32 - 0 & 12 - 5\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}32 & 7\end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix}32 & 7\end{bmatrix} \mathbf{T}^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix}32 & 7\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}0.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}3.2 & 0.7\end{bmatrix}$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix}3.2 & 0.7\end{bmatrix}}$$

**Step 5: Verification (1 mark)**

$$\mathbf{A}_{cl} = \begin{bmatrix}0 & 1 \\ 0 & -5\end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix}0 \\ 10\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}3.2 & 0.7\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0 & 1 \\ -32 & -12\end{bmatrix}$$

$$\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}_{cl}) = s(s+12) + 32 = s^2 + 12s + 32 \quad \checkmark$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the **pole placement design problem** in detail. State clearly the conditions under which arbitrary pole placement is possible. Show that in the **controllability canonical form**, the state feedback gain can be obtained by direct coefficient matching. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the system:

$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 1 \\ 0 & -2\end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix}1 \\ 0\end{bmatrix}$$

**(i)** Check controllability. **(2 marks)**

**(ii)** Design a state feedback gain $\mathbf{K}$ to place the closed-loop poles at $s = -3$ and $s = -4$ using any one method. Show all calculations. **(5 marks)**

---

##### **Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Pole Placement Design Problem (3 marks):**

Given a controllable LTI system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}$, find a constant gain matrix $\mathbf{K}$ such that the control law $\mathbf{u} = -\mathbf{K}\mathbf{x}$ produces a closed-loop system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = (\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{B}\mathbf{K})\mathbf{x}$ with eigenvalues (poles) at designer-specified locations $\{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\}$.

**Condition for Arbitrary Pole Placement (2 marks):**

The pair $(\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B})$ must be **completely state controllable**, i.e., the controllability matrix $\mathcal{C} = [\mathbf{B} \; \mathbf{AB} \; \cdots \; \mathbf{A}^{n-1}\mathbf{B}]$ must have **full rank $n$**. This is both **necessary** (without controllability, unreachable modes exist and cannot be moved) and **sufficient** (full controllability guarantees a unique $\mathbf{K}$ for any desired pole set, with complex poles in conjugate pairs).

**Coefficient Matching in Controllability Canonical Form (2 marks):**

Using the similarity transformation $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{T}\mathbf{z}$ with $\mathbf{T} = \mathcal{C}\mathbf{W}$, the system becomes:

$$\tilde{\mathbf{A}} = \begin{bmatrix}0 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & & & \ddots & \vdots \\ -a_n & -a_{n-1} & \cdots & & -a_1\end{bmatrix}, \quad \tilde{\mathbf{B}} = \begin{bmatrix}0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 1\end{bmatrix}$$

The state feedback $\tilde{\mathbf{u}} = -\tilde{\mathbf{K}}\tilde{\mathbf{z}}$ modifies only the last row of $\tilde{\mathbf{A}}$. The closed-loop last row becomes:

$$\begin{bmatrix}-a_n - \tilde{k}_1 & -a_{n-1} - \tilde{k}_2 & \cdots & -a_1 - \tilde{k}_n\end{bmatrix}$$

For this to match the desired polynomial $s^n + \alpha_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + \alpha_n$, the required gains are:

$$\tilde{k}_i = \alpha_i - a_i, \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

Transforming back: $\mathbf{K} = \tilde{\mathbf{K}} \mathbf{T}^{-1}$.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Controllability Check (2 marks):**

$$\mathbf{A}\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 1 \\ 0 & -2\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}1 \\ 0\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}1 \\ 0\end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathcal{C} = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0\end{bmatrix}, \quad \det(\mathcal{C}) = 0$$

Since $\det(\mathcal{C}) = 0$, the system is **NOT completely controllable**. **Pole placement may be infeasible for arbitrary pole locations.** (Note: the open-loop poles are $1$ and $-2$; the pole at $s = 1$ is unstable but possibly uncontrollable.)

**Check the uncontrollable mode:** The eigenvector of $\mathbf{A}$ for $\lambda = 1$ is $\mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix}1 \\ 0\end{bmatrix}$. Is $\mathbf{v}$ in the controllable subspace? Since $\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix}1 \\ 0\end{bmatrix}$ is parallel to $\mathbf{v}$, the mode at $\lambda = 1$ is **not controllable**. Therefore, we **cannot** place the pole at $s = -4$ (since that requires moving the mode at $\lambda = 1$).

**Reconciling:** Since the system has an uncontrollable mode at $s = 1$, the achievable closed-loop pole set is **constrained**. One pole must remain fixed at $s = 1$ (or another achievable location) and only the other pole can be moved.

Actually, in many KTU-style problems, this matrix is presented to test students' **ability to detect the issue**. Some versions of this problem have a controllable $\mathbf{B}$. Let us proceed assuming a **corrected controllable version**: $\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix}0 \\ 1\end{bmatrix}$ (or alternatively, if $\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix}1 \\ 0\end{bmatrix}$ as given, we note the infeasibility).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** If a problem gives an **uncontrollable system**, do not blindly apply Ackermann's formula. First verify $\det(\mathcal{C}) \neq 0$. If the system is uncontrollable, explicitly **state the limitation** and identify the **fixed (uncontrollable) modes**. This earns full credit and demonstrates mastery.

**(ii) Pole Placement (5 marks) — Using corrected $\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix}0 \\ 1\end{bmatrix}$:**

$$\mathcal{C} = \begin{bmatrix}0 & 1 \\ 1 & -2\end{bmatrix}, \quad \det(\mathcal{C}) = -1 \neq 0 \quad \checkmark \text{Controllable}$$

**Desired polynomial:** $\alpha_d(s) = (s+3)(s+4) = s^2 + 7s + 12$, so $\alpha_1 = 7$, $\alpha_2 = 12$.

**Open-loop polynomial:** $\alpha_o(s) = (s-1)(s+2) = s^2 + s - 2$, so $a_1 = 1$, $a_2 = -2$.

**Ackermann's formula:**

$$\mathcal{C}^{-1} = \frac{1}{-1}\begin{bmatrix}-2 & -1 \\ -1 & 0\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}2 & 1 \\ 1 & 0\end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{A}^2 = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 1 \\ 0 & -2\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}1 & 1 \\ 0 & -2\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}1 & -1 \\ 0 & 4\end{bmatrix}$$

$$\alpha_d(\mathbf{A}) = \mathbf{A}^2 + 7\mathbf{A} + 12\mathbf{I} = \begin{bmatrix}1 & -1 \\ 0 & 4\end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix}7 & 7 \\ 0 & -14\end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix}12 & 0 \\ 0 & 12\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}20 & 6 \\ 0 & 2\end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix}0 & 1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}2 & 1 \\ 1 & 0\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}20 & 6 \\ 0 & 2\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 0\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}20 & 6 \\ 0 & 2\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}20 & 6\end{bmatrix}$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix}20 & 6\end{bmatrix}}$$

**Verification:**

$$\mathbf{A}_{cl} = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 1 \\ 0 & -2\end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix}0 \\ 1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}20 & 6\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 1 \\ -20 & -8\end{bmatrix}$$

$$\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}_{cl}) = (s-1)(s+8) + 20 = s^2 + 7s - 8 + 20 = s^2 + 7s + 12 \quad \checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Skipping the controllability check** (-2 marks): Always compute $\det(\mathcal{C})$ first.
> 2. **Arithmetic errors in matrix polynomial evaluation** (-2 marks): Verify $\alpha_d(\mathbf{A})$ by independent recomputation.
> 3. **Forgetting to verify the answer** (-1 mark): Always compute $\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A} + \mathbf{BK})$ and confirm it equals $\alpha_d(s)$.
> 4. **Complex poles without conjugate pairing** (-1 mark): If you specify $s = -1 + j$, you must also place a pole at $s = -1 - j$ for real-valued $\mathbf{K}$.
> 5. **Mixing up row vs. column vector in Ackermann's formula** (-1 mark): The selector vector is a **row** vector; $\mathbf{K}$ is a **row** vector (for single-input systems).
> 6. **Not reducing to controllable canonical form properly** (-1 mark): When using Bass-Gura, show the Sylvester matrix $\mathbf{W}$ explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this section for last-minute exam revision!**

- ☐ **Pole placement** is the design of state feedback gain $\mathbf{K}$ such that eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{BK}$ are at desired locations.
- ☐ **Pre-requisite:** Pair $(\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B})$ must be **completely controllable** — $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}) = n$.
- ☐ **Controllability Matrix:** $\mathcal{C} = [\mathbf{B} \; \mathbf{AB} \; \mathbf{A}^2\mathbf{B} \; \cdots \; \mathbf{A}^{n-1}\mathbf{B}]$.
- ☐ **Control Law:** $\mathbf{u} = -\mathbf{Kx} + \mathbf{r}$ (negative feedback convention).
- ☐ **Closed-Loop Matrix:** $\mathbf{A}_{cl} = \mathbf{A} - \mathbf{BK}$.
- ☐ **Desired Polynomial:** $\alpha_d(s) = \prod (s - s_i) = s^n + \alpha_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + \alpha_n$.
- ☐ **Open-Loop Polynomial:** $\alpha_o(s) = \det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n$.
- ☐ **Ackermann's Formula:** $\mathbf{K} = [0 \; 0 \; \cdots \; 0 \; 1] \mathcal{C}^{-1} \alpha_d(\mathbf{A})$.
- ☐ **Bass-Gura Formula:** $\mathbf{K} = [\alpha_n - a_n \; \alpha_{n-1} - a_{n-1} \; \cdots \; \alpha_1 - a_1] \mathbf{T}^{-1}$, where $\mathbf{T} = \mathcal{C}\mathbf{W}$ and $\mathbf{W}$ is the Sylvester matrix of $\alpha_o(s)$ coefficients.
- ☐ **Stability Rule:** All closed-loop poles must have **negative real parts** (LHP).
- ☐ **Complex Conjugate Pairing:** Always required for real-valued $\mathbf{K}$.
- ☐ **Second-Order Design:** $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2$ — relate $t_s$ and $\%M_p$ to pole positions.
- ☐ **Limitation:** Pole placement does **not** guarantee zero steady-state error — augment with integral action if tracking is required.
- ☐ **Verification Step:** Always compute $\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A} + \mathbf{BK})$ and confirm it equals $\alpha_d(s)$.
- ☐ **Exam Strategy:** State controllability → form polynomials → apply formula → verify. **Never skip a step.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sampled Data Control System: Pulse Transfer Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT636 - ADVANCED CONTROL THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sampled Data Control System: Pulse Transfer Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Sampled-Data Control System (SDCS)** is a control system in which the reference input and/or feedback signals are sampled at discrete instants of time using a **sampler (A/D converter)**, while the plant or process under control may still operate in the continuous-time domain. The reconstruction is typically performed using a **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** or a higher-order hold device (D/A converter).

A **Pulse Transfer Function (PTF)** — also called the **Discrete Transfer Function** — is defined as the ratio of the **Z-transform of the sampled output sequence** to the **Z-transform of the sampled input sequence**, assuming **zero initial conditions**:

$$G(z) \;=\; \frac{C(z)}{R(z)} \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} g(kT)\,z^{-k}$$

where:
- $g(kT)$ is the sampled value of the impulse response $g(t)$ of the continuous plant at instants $kT$, with $T$ = sampling period (in **seconds**).
- $R(z)$ and $C(z)$ are the **Z-transforms** of the input and output sampled sequences, respectively.
- The frequency of sampling is $f_s = 1/T$ Hz, and the sampling angular frequency is $\omega_s = 2\pi/T$ rad/sec.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim phrasing style):** *"The Pulse Transfer Function of a linear time-invariant sampled-data system is the ratio of the Z-transform of the output sampled signal to the Z-transform of the input sampled signal, with all initial conditions equal to zero."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English

Imagine you are teaching a child how to catch a ball. You can either:
- **Watch the ball continuously** (continuous system) — you track its position every microsecond.
- **Glance at the ball only at every "tick" of a clock** (sampled system) — say, every $0.1$ second, you look, decide, and send a command to your hand.

The **Pulse Transfer Function** is like a **"score card"** that records: *"For every standard input pulse I throw at the system at the clock-tick, here is the height the ball reaches at the next clock-tick."* It converts an infinite continuous function $g(t)$ into a countable set of numbers $g(0), g(T), g(2T), g(3T),\dots$ — a much more **compact and digital-friendly** description.

In essence: **Continuous Transfer Function $G(s)$ answers "what is the Laplace-domain input-output ratio?"; Pulse Transfer Function $G(z)$ answers the same question but in the Z-domain, suitable for digital computers and microcontrollers.**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Sampling period | $T$ | **0.01 – 1.0 seconds** (process control) |
| Sampling frequency | $f_s$ | **1 Hz – 100 kHz** |
| Nyquist frequency | $f_N = f_s / 2$ | Half of $f_s$ |
| Starred Laplace variable | $s^*$ | $e^{sT}$ in Z-domain |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sampling Theorem (Shannon–Nyquist):** To reconstruct a bandlimited signal without aliasing, $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$, where $f_{max}$ is the highest frequency component of the input signal. The minimum sampling frequency is $f_s = 2 f_{max}$ — called the **Nyquist rate**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why a Separate Theory for Sampled Systems?

In **continuous-time** control, we use the **Laplace transform** because differentiation and integration become algebraic operations. In **discrete-time** systems, signals are sequences. The **Z-transform** plays the same role for sequences as the Laplace transform plays for continuous signals — it converts **delay/shift operations** into algebraic multiplications by $z^{-1}$.

The connection between Laplace and Z-domain is given by the fundamental mapping:

$$z \;=\; e^{sT} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad s \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\ln(z)$$

## 2.2 The Sampling Process — Mathematical Model

An **ideal impulse sampler** with sampling period $T$ converts a continuous signal $r(t)$ into a train of impulses:

$$r^{*}(t) \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT)\,\delta(t - kT)$$

The Laplace transform of $r^*(t)$ is:

$$R^{*}(s) \;=\; \mathcal{L}\{r^{*}(t)\} \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT)\,e^{-kTs}$$

This is a **periodic function of $s$** with period $j\omega_s = j2\pi/T$.

## 2.3 The Starred Laplace Transform (Master Tool for PTF Derivation)

If a continuous system $G(s)$ is preceded by an ideal impulse sampler, the output $C(s)$ is a continuous function of $s$, but the **sampled output** $C^*(s)$ is:

$$C^{*}(s) \;=\; \left[G(s)\,R^{*}(s)\right]^{*} \;=\; G^{*}(s)\,R^{*}(s)$$

This is the **pivotal step** — the star operator can be factored out because $R^*(s)$ is itself periodic in $s$. The starred Laplace transform is defined as:

$$G^{*}(s) \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} G\!\left(s + j\frac{2\pi k}{T}\right)$$

Replacing $e^{sT}$ with $z$ yields the **Z-transform** of $G(s)$:

$$G(z) \;=\; \left.G^{*}(s)\right|_{z=e^{sT}} \;=\; \mathcal{Z}\{G(s)\}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Z-transform definition | $G(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} g(kT)\,z^{-1}$ | Forward transform |
| 2 | Laplace ↔ Z mapping | $z = e^{sT}$ ; $s = \frac{1}{T}\ln z$ | Fundamental relationship |
| 3 | Shifting (delay) property | $\mathcal{Z}\{f(t - nT)\} = z^{-n}F(z)$ | $n \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ |
| 4 | Shifting (advance) property | $\mathcal{Z}\{f(t + nT)\} = z^{n}\!\left[F(z) - \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} f(kT)z^{-k}\right]$ | For initial conditions |
| 5 | Final value theorem | $\lim_{k\to\infty} f(kT) = \lim_{z\to 1}(1 - z^{-1})F(z)$ | System stable in Z-domain |
| 6 | Initial value theorem | $f(0) = \lim_{z\to\infty} F(z)$ | Discrete-time |
| 7 | Stability region in z-plane | $\vert z \vert < 1$ (unit disc) | Inside unit circle |
| 8 | Starred Laplace factorisation | $[G(s)R(s)]^{*} = G^{*}(s)R^{*}(s)$ | Only when R is sampled |
| 9 | ZOH transfer function (s-domain) | $G_{ZOH}(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}$ | Standard reconstruction |
| 10 | ZOH–equivalent pulse TF | $G_{ZOH}(z) = (1 - z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_{p}(s)}{s}\right\}$ | $G_p$ = plant TF |
| 11 | Open-loop PTF (sampler at input) | $C(z)/R(z) = G(z)$ | $G(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{G(s)\}$ |
| 12 | Closed-loop PTF (sampler in error path) | $\dfrac{C(z)}{R(z)} = \dfrac{G(z)}{1 + GH(z)}$ | Standard unity feedback |
| 13 | Closed-loop PTF (sampler at output) | $\dfrac{C(z)}{R(z)} = \dfrac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)H(z)}$ | After sampler modification |
| 14 | Digital controller form | $C(z) = D(z)\,[R(z) - B(z)]$ | $D(z)$ = digital controller |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TRAP:** The expression $C(z)/R(z) = G(z)/[1 + G(z)H(z)]$ is **NOT always valid**. It applies **only** when there is a sampler in the error channel and a sampler at the output. If the sampler configuration changes, the formula changes — this is a **very common 7-mark board question**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital PID controllers** in industrial process control (temperature, pressure, flow).
- **Flight control systems** in modern aircraft (sampled at 50–1000 Hz).
- **Robotic manipulators** with on-board microcontrollers.
- **Hard disk read-head servos** in computer storage systems.
- **DC motor speed control** with digital encoders.
- **Power electronic converters** (PWM-based inverters) sampled at the switching frequency.

The **pulse transfer function is the bridge** that lets an engineer design a digital controller using classical techniques (root locus, Bode plot) directly in the Z-plane.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Pulse Transfer Function from Starred Laplace Transform

**Problem Setup:** A continuous LTI system $G(s)$ is driven by a sampled input $r^*(t)$ through an ideal impulse sampler. Determine the pulse transfer function $G(z) = C(z)/R(z)$.

### Step 1 — Express the input as an impulse train

The output of the ideal sampler is:

$$r^{*}(t) \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT)\,\delta(t - kT)$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Laplace transform of the sampler output

$$\mathcal{L}\{r^{*}(t)\} \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT)\,e^{-kTs} \;\equiv\; R^{*}(s)$$

### Step 3 — Compute the (unsampled) output Laplace transform

For a continuous LTI system with transfer function $G(s)$:

$$C(s) \;=\; G(s)\,R^{*}(s)$$

### Step 4 — Sample the output to get the discrete sequence

$$C^{*}(s) \;=\; \left[G(s)\,R^{*}(s)\right]^{*} \;=\; G^{*}(s)\,R^{*}(s)$$

The last equality uses the **starred-Laplace factorisation property**: when one factor is already periodic in $s$ (i.e., $R^*(s)$), it passes through the star operator unchanged.

### Step 5 — Convert to Z-domain by the substitution $z = e^{sT}$

$$C(z) \;=\; G(z)\,R(z)$$

where $G(z) \equiv \mathcal{Z}\{G(s)\} = G^*(s)\vert_{z=e^{sT}}$.

### Step 6 — Form the ratio (with zero initial conditions)

$$\boxed{\;\frac{C(z)}{R(z)} \;=\; G(z) \;=\; \mathcal{Z}\{G(s)\}\;}$$

> **Conclusion:** The pulse transfer function of a continuous system driven by a sampled input is simply the **Z-transform of the continuous transfer function** $G(s)$, computed with the table lookup using the relation $z = e^{sT}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Closed-Loop Pulse Transfer Function (Sampler in Error Path)

**Standard SDCS configuration:** Reference $R(s) \rightarrow$ Sampler $\rightarrow$ Error $E(s) \rightarrow$ Continuous plant $G(s) \rightarrow$ Output $C(s)$; Feedback $H(s)$ continuous; output sampled separately for comparison.

### Step 1 — Write the error equation (continuous form)

$$E(s) \;=\; R(s) - H(s)\,C(s)$$

### Step 2 — Output equation

$$C(s) \;=\; G(s)\,E(s)$$

### Step 3 — Eliminate $E(s)$ to obtain continuous closed-loop TF

$$C(s) \;=\; G(s)\bigl[R(s) - H(s)C(s)\bigr] \;\Rightarrow\; C(s)\bigl[1 + G(s)H(s)\bigr] \;=\; G(s)R(s)$$

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

### Step 4 — Star both sides and use the factorisation property

$$C^{*}(s) \;=\; \left[\frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}\right]^{*} R^{*}(s)$$

### Step 5 — Take the Z-transform

$$\boxed{\;\frac{C(z)}{R(z)} \;=\; \mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}\right\}\;}$$

> **Important:** You **cannot** simply replace $s$ with $z$ in the closed-loop expression! You must first combine the continuous block $G(s)/[1 + G(s)H(s)]$, simplify it into a single transfer function, and **then** take the Z-transform of the simplified result.

> [!WARNING]
> **BOARD-VALUATION PITFALL:** Many students write $C(z)/R(z) = G(z)/[1 + G(z)H(z)]$ and lose full marks. This is correct **only** if there is a sampler between $G(s)$ and $H(s)$ — i.e., **the feedback signal is also sampled**. Otherwise, you must take the Z-transform of the entire equivalent $G(s)/[1 + G(s)H(s)]$ block.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Pulse Transfer Function with Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)

**Most common industrial configuration:** Digital controller output is converted to analog via a **Zero-Order Hold** before driving the plant.

### Step 1 — ZOH mathematical model

A zero-order hold holds the sampled value constant for one sampling period:

$$G_{ZOH}(s) \;=\; \frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}$$

### Step 2 — Cascade ZOH with plant $G_p(s)$

The combined continuous transfer function seen by the sampler is:

$$G_{eq}(s) \;=\; G_{ZOH}(s)\cdot G_{p}(s) \;=\; \left(\frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}\right)G_{p}(s)$$

### Step 3 — Apply the time-shift property of Laplace

Note that multiplication by $e^{-sT}$ corresponds to a delay of $T$ seconds. So:

$$G_{eq}(s) \;=\; \frac{G_{p}(s)}{s} - \frac{e^{-sT}\,G_{p}(s)}{s}$$

### Step 4 — Take the Z-transform

Using the delay property $\mathcal{Z}\{e^{-sT}F(s)\} = z^{-1}F(z)$:

$$G_{eq}(z) \;=\; \mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_{p}(s)}{s}\right\} - z^{-1}\,\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_{p}(s)}{s}\right\}$$

### Step 5 — Factor the result

$$G_{eq}(z) \;=\; (1 - z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_{p}(s)}{s}\right\}$$

$$\boxed{\;G_{ZOH}(z) \;=\; (1 - z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_{p}(s)}{s}\right\} \;=\; \frac{z - 1}{z}\,\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_{p}(s)}{s}\right\}\;}$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical)

**Given:** $G_p(s) = \dfrac{1}{s(s+1)}$, $T = 1$ sec. Find $G_{ZOH}(z)$.

**Solution:**

$$\frac{G_p(s)}{s} \;=\; \frac{1}{s^{2}(s+1)} \;=\; \frac{1}{s^{2}} - \frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{s+1}$$

(using partial fractions: $\dfrac{1}{s^{2}(s+1)} = \dfrac{A}{s^{2}} + \dfrac{B}{s} + \dfrac{C}{s+1}$ with $A=1, B=-1, C=1$.)

Now apply standard Z-transform pairs ($T = 1$):

$$\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{1}{s^{2}}\right\} = \frac{Tz}{(z-1)^{2}} = \frac{z}{(z-1)^{2}}$$

$$\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{1}{s}\right\} = \frac{z}{z-1}$$

$$\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{1}{s+1}\right\} = \frac{z}{z - e^{-T}} = \frac{z}{z - e^{-1}}$$

Therefore:

$$\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\} \;=\; \frac{z}{(z-1)^{2}} - \frac{z}{z-1} + \frac{z}{z - e^{-1}}$$

Combine into a single fraction (use common denominator $(z-1)^{2}(z-e^{-1})$):

$$= \frac{z(z-1)(z-e^{-1}) - z(z-1)^{2}(z-e^{-1})/(z-1) + z(z-1)^{2}}{(z-1)^{2}(z-e^{-1})}$$

After algebraic simplification:

$$\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\} \;=\; \frac{z\bigl[(e^{-1} - 1)z + (1 - 2e^{-1} + e^{-1})\bigr]}{(z-1)^{2}(z-e^{-1})}$$

Since $e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$:

$$\approx \frac{z(-0.6321\,z + 0.2642)}{(z-1)^{2}(z - 0.3679)}$$

Multiplying by $(1 - z^{-1}) = (z-1)/z$:

$$G_{ZOH}(z) \;=\; \frac{(z-1)}{z} \cdot \frac{z(-0.6321\,z + 0.2642)}{(z-1)^{2}(z - 0.3679)}$$

$$\boxed{\;G_{ZOH}(z) \;=\; \frac{0.3679\,z + 0.2642}{(z - 1)(z - 0.3679)}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Shortcut:** The factor $(1 - z^{-1})$ always cancels **one** factor of $z$ and **one** factor of $(z-1)$ from the term $\mathcal{Z}\{1/s^{n}\}$. This dramatically simplifies final answers — keep this in mind during exams.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Configuration → Pulse Transfer Function

| Configuration | Block diagram description | $C(z)/R(z)$ | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A. Sampler at input only** | $R(s) \rightarrow \text{Sampler} \rightarrow G(s) \rightarrow C(s)$ | $G(z)$ | Simplest case |
| **B. ZOH + plant, sampler before ZOH** | $R \to S \to G_{ZOH}G_p \to C$ | $(1 - z^{-1})\mathcal{Z}\{G_p(s)/s\}$ | Most common digital control |
| **C. Closed loop, sampler in error path** | $R \to S \to E \to G \to C$; continuous feedback $H$ | $\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\dfrac{G(s)}{1 + GH(s)}\right\}$ | Take Z of equivalent block |
| **D. Closed loop, sampler also in feedback** | $R \to S \to E \to G \to C$; $H$ sampled at output | $\dfrac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)H(z)}$ | Standard form |
| **E. Output sampler only** | $R \to G \to S \to C$ | $\dfrac{G(z)\,R(z)}{1}$ | Trivial case |
| **F. Digital cascade** | $D(z) \to G_{ZOH}G_p \to C$, sampler at input | $D(z)\cdot(1 - z^{-1})\mathcal{Z}\{G_p(s)/s\}$ | $D(z)$ = digital controller |
