# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q:** Define Pulse Transfer Function. What is its significance in the analysis of sampled-data control systems?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Pulse Transfer Function (PTF)** of a linear time-invariant sampled-data system is defined as the ratio of the **Z-transform of the sampled output sequence** $C(z)$ to the **Z-transform of the sampled input sequence** $R(z)$, under zero initial conditions:

$$G(z) \;=\; \frac{C(z)}{R(z)} \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} g(kT)\,z^{-k}$$

**Significance (2 marks):**
- It provides a **discrete-time input–output model** of the system, suitable for digital computer implementation.
- It is the **Z-domain analogue of the continuous transfer function** $G(s)$, enabling classical techniques like root locus, Bode plots, and Nyquist plots to be applied in the Z-plane.
- It is the **fundamental tool** for stability analysis, transient response, and digital controller design of SDCS.
- It allows synthesis of controllers via **algebraic manipulation** in the Z-domain, similar to $s$-domain compensator design.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q:** State the Shannon–Nyquist sampling theorem. What is aliasing, and how is it prevented?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Shannon–Nyquist Sampling Theorem (2 marks):** A bandlimited continuous signal $f(t)$ with maximum frequency component $f_{max}$ Hz can be **completely reconstructed** from its samples if the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies:

$$f_s \;\geq\; 2 f_{max}$$

The minimum sampling frequency $f_s = 2f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist rate**, and $f_N = f_s/2$ is the **Nyquist frequency**.

**Aliasing and Prevention (1 mark):** Aliasing is the **phenomenon of distortion** that occurs when high-frequency components of a signal masquerade as lower-frequency components in the sampled version (i.e., when $f_s < 2 f_{max}$). It is prevented by using a **pre-filter (anti-aliasing low-pass filter)** before the sampler to ensure the input is sufficiently bandlimited.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question B1 (Option A) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the **Z-transform** and the **Laplace transform** for a sampled signal. Show that $G(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{G(s)\}$.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the closed-loop sampled-data control system shown below, find the **closed-loop pulse transfer function** $C(z)/R(z)$:

- Forward path: $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$
- Feedback path: $H(s) = 1$ (unity)
- **Sampler present in the error path** with sampling period $T = 0.5$ sec.  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Consider a continuous LTI system $G(s)$ driven by a sampled input $r^*(t)$ where:

$$r^{*}(t) \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT)\,\delta(t - kT)$$

**Step 1 — Laplace transform of sampled input:**

$$R^{*}(s) \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT)\,e^{-kTs} \quad\text{...(1)}$$

**Step 2 — Laplace transform of system output:**

$$C(s) \;=\; G(s)\,R^{*}(s) \quad\text{...(2)}$$

**Step 3 — Sample the output:**

$$C^{*}(s) \;=\; \left[G(s)\,R^{*}(s)\right]^{*} \;=\; G^{*}(s)\,R^{*}(s) \quad\text{...(3)}$$

The factorisation $C^*(s) = G^*(s) R^*(s)$ holds because $R^*(s)$ is **periodic in $s$** with period $j\omega_s$, and only the periodic part passes through the star operator unchanged.

**Step 4 — Define the starred Laplace transform:**

$$G^{*}(s) \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} G\!\left(s + j\frac{2\pi k}{T}\right) \quad\text{...(4)}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $z = e^{sT}$:**

Let $z = e^{sT}$, so $e^{-kTs} = z^{-k}$. Then equation (3) becomes:

$$C(z) \;=\; G(z)\,R(z) \quad\text{where}\quad G(z) \;\equiv\; \mathcal{Z}\{G(s)\}$$

**Step 6 — Form the ratio:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{C(z)}{R(z)} \;=\; G(z) \;=\; \mathcal{Z}\{G(s)\} \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} g(kT)\,z^{-k}\;}$$

This establishes that **the Z-transform of $G(s)$ is the pulse transfer function of the system**.

---

**(b) Closed-Loop PTF Derivation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$, $H(s) = 1$, sampler in error path, $T = 0.5$ sec.

**Step 1 — Combine the closed-loop equivalent block first:**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)} \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}}{1 + \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}} \;=\; \frac{10}{s^{2} + 2s + 10}$$

**Step 2 — Find equivalent continuous time constants:**

$$G_{eq}(s) \;=\; \frac{10}{s^{2} + 2s + 10}$$

Write in standard second-order form. Complete the square:

$$s^{2} + 2s + 10 \;=\; (s + 1)^{2} + 9 \;=\; (s + 1)^{2} + 3^{2}$$

So $G_{eq}(s) = \dfrac{10}{(s+1)^{2} + 3^{2}}$.

**Step 3 — Use standard Laplace inverse / Z-transform table:**

Recall the Z-transform pair:

$$\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{\omega}{(s + a)^{2} + \omega^{2}}\right\} \;=\; \frac{z\,e^{-aT}\sin(\omega T)}{z^{2} - 2z e^{-aT}\cos(\omega T) + e^{-2aT}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply with $a = 1$, $\omega = 3$, $T = 0.5$:**

$$e^{-aT} = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065, \quad e^{-2aT} = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$$

$$\sin(\omega T) = \sin(1.5) \approx 0.9975, \quad \cos(\omega T) = \cos(1.5) \approx 0.0707$$

Now $G_{eq}(s) = \dfrac{10}{9} \cdot \dfrac{3}{(s+1)^{2} + 3^{2}}$, so:

$$G_{eq}(z) \;=\; \frac{10}{9} \cdot \frac{z\,e^{-0.5}\sin(1.5)}{z^{2} - 2z e^{-0.5}\cos(1.5) + e^{-1}}$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$G_{eq}(z) \;=\; \frac{10}{9} \cdot \frac{z(0.6065)(0.9975)}{z^{2} - 2z(0.6065)(0.0707) + 0.3679}$$

$$= \frac{10}{9} \cdot \frac{0.6050\,z}{z^{2} - 0.0858\,z + 0.3679}$$

$$\boxed{\;\frac{C(z)}{R(z)} \;=\; \frac{0.6722\,z}{z^{2} - 0.0858\,z + 0.3679}\;}$$

---

### Question B2 (Option B) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Explain the operation of a **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)**. Derive an expression for the **pulse transfer function of a ZOH cascaded with a plant** $G_p(s)$.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A plant with transfer function $G_p(s) = \dfrac{2}{s(s+3)}$ is preceded by a ZOH with sampling period $T = 0.2$ sec. Determine the **pulse transfer function** of the combined system.  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) ZOH Operation and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Operation (2 marks):** A Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) is a **signal reconstruction device** used at the output of a digital controller (or sampler) to convert a discrete-time signal $u(kT)$ into a piecewise-constant continuous-time signal $u_h(t)$ that holds the value of each sample constant over one sampling period $T$. The mathematical model is:

$$G_{ZOH}(s) \;=\; \frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}$$

**Derivation (5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Cascade ZOH with the plant:

$$G_{eq}(s) \;=\; G_{ZOH}(s)\,G_{p}(s) \;=\; \left(\frac{1 - e^{-sT}}{s}\right)G_{p}(s) \;=\; \frac{G_{p}(s)}{s} - \frac{e^{-sT}G_{p}(s)}{s}$$

**Step 2:** Take the Z-transform of both sides. By the time-shift (delay) property of Z-transform: $\mathcal{Z}\{e^{-sT}F(s)\} = z^{-1}F(z)$.

**Step 3:**

$$G_{eq}(z) \;=\; \mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_{p}(s)}{s}\right\} - z^{-1}\,\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_{p}(s)}{s}\right\} \;=\; (1 - z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_{p}(s)}{s}\right\}$$

$$\boxed{\;G_{ZOH}(z) \;=\; (1 - z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_{p}(s)}{s}\right\}\;}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The ZOH contributes a factor of $(1 - z^{-1})$ to the discrete model, which introduces a **half-period delay** in the system's response and accounts for the **staircase reconstruction** of the analog signal.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $G_p(s) = \dfrac{2}{s(s+3)}$, $T = 0.2$ sec.

**Step 1 — Compute $G_p(s)/s$:**

$$\frac{G_p(s)}{s} \;=\; \frac{2}{s^{2}(s+3)} \;=\; \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{s^{2}} + \frac{C}{s+3}$$

Use partial fractions. By Heaviside cover-up: $B = \lim_{s\to 0} s^{2}\cdot \frac{2}{s^{2}(s+3)} = \frac{2}{3}$.

$A = \lim_{s\to 0} \frac{d}{ds}\left[s^{2}\cdot\frac{2}{s^{2}(s+3)}\right] = \lim_{s\to 0}\frac{d}{ds}\frac{2}{s+3} = -\frac{2}{9}$.

$C = \lim_{s\to -3}(s+3)\cdot\frac{2}{s^{2}(s+3)} = \frac{2}{9}$.

So:

$$\frac{G_p(s)}{s} \;=\; -\frac{2}{9}\cdot\frac{1}{s} + \frac{2}{3}\cdot\frac{1}{s^{2}} + \frac{2}{9}\cdot\frac{1}{s+3}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Z-transform pairs (with $T = 0.2$):**

$$\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{1}{s}\right\} = \frac{z}{z-1}$$

$$\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{1}{s^{2}}\right\} = \frac{Tz}{(z-1)^{2}} = \frac{0.2\,z}{(z-1)^{2}}$$

$$\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{1}{s+3}\right\} = \frac{z}{z - e^{-3T}} = \frac{z}{z - e^{-0.6}}$$

Since $e^{-0.6} \approx 0.5488$:

$$\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\} \;=\; -\frac{2}{9}\cdot\frac{z}{z-1} + \frac{2}{3}\cdot\frac{0.2z}{(z-1)^{2}} + \frac{2}{9}\cdot\frac{z}{z - 0.5488}$$

$$= \frac{-2z(z-1)(z-0.5488) + \frac{2\cdot 0.2}{3}\cdot 9z(z-0.5488) + \frac{2}{9}\cdot 9z(z-1)^{2}}{9(z-1)^{2}(z-0.5488)}$$

After expanding and combining:

$$\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\} \;=\; \frac{z\bigl[(-2)(z-1)(z-0.5488) + 0.12\cdot 9(z-0.5488) + 2(z-1)^{2}\bigr]}{9(z-1)^{2}(z-0.5488)}$$

Computing the numerator bracket term by term:
- $(-2)(z-1)(z-0.5488) = -2(z^{2} - 1.5488z + 0.5488) = -2z^{2} + 3.0976z - 1.0976$
- $0.12\cdot 9(z - 0.5488) = 1.08z - 0.5927$
- $2(z-1)^{2} = 2z^{2} - 4z + 2$

Sum: $(-2z^{2} + 2z^{2}) + (3.0976z + 1.08z - 4z) + (-1.0976 - 0.5927 + 2)$
$= 0\cdot z^{2} + 0.1776z + 0.3097$

So:

$$\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\} \;\approx\; \frac{0.1776z + 0.3097}{9(z-1)^{2}(z-0.5488)} \;=\; \frac{0.01973z + 0.03441}{(z-1)^{2}(z-0.5488)}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by $(1 - z^{-1}) = (z-1)/z$:**

$$G_{ZOH}(z) \;=\; \frac{z-1}{z}\cdot\frac{0.01973z + 0.03441}{(z-1)^{2}(z-0.5488)} \;=\; \frac{0.01973z + 0.03441}{z(z-1)(z-0.5488)}$$

$$\boxed{\;G_{ZOH}(z) \;=\; \frac{0.01973z + 0.03441}{z^{3} - 1.5488z^{2} + 0.5488z}\;}$$

This is the **final pulse transfer function** of the ZOH + plant combination.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Confusing starred Laplace with Z-transform:** The star operator $\,^{*}\,$ refers to **starred Laplace**; only the substitution $z = e^{sT}$ converts it to Z-domain.
> 2. **Forgetting to simplify the closed-loop block before taking Z-transform:** This yields incorrect PTF forms.
> 3. **Sign errors in partial fractions:** Always verify by recombining the partial fractions.
> 4. **Numerical computation of $e^{-aT}$:** Carry at least **4 decimal places** in the intermediate steps.
> 5. **Ignoring the ZOH factor $(1 - z^{-1})$:** If the question specifies a ZOH, you **must** include this factor.
> 6. **Forgetting the initial-condition handling** in the advance Z-transform property.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Key Definitions
- **Pulse Transfer Function:** $G(z) = C(z)/R(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{g(t)\}$ under zero initial conditions.
- **Sampled signal:** $r^{*}(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r(kT)\delta(t - kT)$.
- **Z-transform mapping:** $z = e^{sT}$.

### 🔑 Master Formulae
1. $\mathcal{Z}\{f(t - nT)\} = z^{-n}F(z)$.
2. $[G(s)R(s)]^{*} = G^{*}(s)R^{*}(s)$ — only when $R(s)$ is already periodic.
3. $G_{ZOH}(z) = (1 - z^{-1})\mathcal{Z}\{G_p(s)/s\}$.
4. Closed-loop PTF: depends on **sampler location**.
5. Stability region: $\vert z \vert < 1$ (inside unit circle).

### 🔑 Configuration-to-Formula Mapping (Memorize)
| Sampler Location | $C(z)/R(z)$ |
|---|---|
| Input only | $G(z)$ |
| Error path (closed loop) | $\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\dfrac{G}{1 + GH}\right\}$ |
| Output of $G$ in closed loop | $\dfrac{G(z)}{1 + G(z)H(z)}$ |
| With ZOH | $(1 - z^{-1})\mathcal{Z}\{G/s\}$ |

### 🔑 Exam Strategy Checklist
- [ ] Identify the **sampler location(s)** first.
- [ ] Combine the closed-loop block **before** Z-transforming.
- [ ] Apply the ZOH factor $(1 - z^{-1})$ if a **hold device** is mentioned.
- [ ] Use **partial fractions** for inverse Z-transforms — never attempt direct integration.
- [ ] Always specify the **sampling period $T$** numerically in the final answer.
- [ ] For stability, check that **all closed-loop poles lie inside the unit circle** $\vert z \vert < 1$.

### 🔑 Real-World Connection
The pulse transfer function is the **mathematical heart** of every digitally implemented controller — from the cruise control in your car to the autopilot in an aircraft. Mastering it equips you to design, analyse, and **troubleshoot** any computer-controlled system.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stability of Sampled Data System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT636 - ADVANCED CONTROL THEORY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stability of Sampled Data System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability of a Sampled Data System:**
> A linear, time-invariant, causal sampled-data (discrete-time) system with pulse transfer function $G(z)$ is said to be **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) stable** if and only if every bounded input sequence $r(k)$ produces a bounded output sequence $c(k)$ for all $k \geq 0$.

In the z-domain, this translates to the following **poles-only criterion**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Condition (Pole Location Criterion):**
> A sampled-data system is **asymptotically stable** if and only if **all the poles of its closed-loop pulse transfer function lie strictly inside the unit circle** in the complex z-plane, i.e.,
> $$\vert z_i \vert < 1, \quad \forall \, i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$
> If **any pole** lies on the unit circle ($|z_i|=1$) and the rest are inside, the system is **marginally stable** (sustained oscillations). If **any pole** lies outside the unit circle ($|z_i|>1$), the system is **unstable** (output grows without bound).

The characteristic equation of the closed-loop sampled-data system is:
$$F(z) = 1 + G(z)H(z) = 0$$

The roots (zeros of $F(z)$) of this polynomial are the closed-loop poles that must be tested for stability.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> **🍕 Real-World Analogy — The "Restaurant Order Counter":**
>
> Imagine a fast-food counter where a chef prepares food only when the timer **beeps** (sampling instant $t = kT$). The chef's reaction depends on the previous order's result (feedback).
>
> - **Stable system (**|z| < 1|):** If the chef makes a small mistake today, that mistake **decays** by tomorrow. The kitchen settles into a steady rhythm.
> - **Marginal stability (**|z| = 1|):** If the chef undercooks today, he undercooks **exactly the same amount** every day — a perpetual, repeating error (oscillation).
> - **Unstable system (**|z| > 1|):** Every tiny mistake **amplifies** the next day. Soon, the kitchen is in chaos — orders pile up forever.
>
> The **unit circle** in the z-plane is the "boundary of disciplined behaviour." Poles inside = disciplined chef; poles outside = chaotic chef.

---

## 1.3 Why Sampled-Data Stability is Fundamentally Different from Continuous-Time Stability

| Continuous-Time System | Sampled-Data (Discrete) System |
|---|---|
| Stable if all poles lie in **Left Half of s-plane** ($\sigma < 0$). | Stable if all poles lie **inside the unit circle** in the z-plane. |
| Imaginary axis $j\omega$ is the stability boundary. | Unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ is the stability boundary. |
| $\sigma < 0 \;\Longleftrightarrow\;$ exponential decay. | $\vert z \vert < 1 \;\Longleftrightarrow\;$ exponential decay ($r^k$, $r<1$). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The imaginary axis of the s-plane **maps onto the unit circle** of the z-plane, not onto the real axis. This single fact changes *every* classical stability test (Routh, Hurwitz, Nyquist, Root Locus) into a different mathematical form.

---

## 1.4 Common KTU Mistake Alert

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #1:** Students often confuse "$|z|<1$" with "Re($z$)<0." They are **NOT** the same. The stability boundary in z-plane is a **circle**, not a vertical line. Re($z$)<0 is the stability region for the w-plane (after bilinear transformation).
> **Pitfall #2:** The imaginary axis of the s-plane maps to the *entire* unit circle (due to aliasing, multiple s-plane strips map onto the same z-plane). Always remember the **primary strip** and **complementary strips**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mapping Between s-plane and z-plane — The Geometric Foundation

The defining relationship between the Laplace variable $s$ and the z-transform variable $z$ is:

$$z = e^{sT}$$

Let $s = \sigma + j\omega$. Substituting:

$$z = e^{\sigma T} \cdot e^{j\omega T}$$

### Magnitude and Angle Components

$$\boxed{\vert z \vert = e^{\sigma T}, \quad \angle z = \omega T}$$

### Stability Mapping — The Three Cases

| s-plane location | $\sigma$ | $\vert z \vert$ | z-plane location | Stability Verdict |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Left half-plane | $\sigma < 0$ | $\vert z \vert < 1$ | **Inside** unit circle | **Stable** |
| Imaginary axis | $\sigma = 0$ | $\vert z \vert = 1$ | **On** unit circle | Marginally stable |
| Right half-plane | $\sigma > 0$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ | **Outside** unit circle | **Unstable** |

### Primary Strip and Complementary Strips

The mapping $z = e^{sT}$ is **many-to-one** because $\omega$ and $\omega + 2\pi n/T$ give the same $z$. Hence the entire s-plane is divided into **horizontal strips** of height $2\pi/T$:

$$-\frac{\pi}{T} < \omega \leq +\frac{\pi}{T} \quad \text{(Primary Strip)}$$

$$(2n-1)\frac{\pi}{T} < \omega \leq (2n+1)\frac{\pi}{T} \quad \text{(Complementary Strip, } n=\pm1,\pm2,\dots)$$

The primary strip (LHP portion) maps **one-to-one** onto the interior of the unit circle.

---

## 2.2 Stability Testing Methods — The Big Picture

KTU recognizes **three primary methods**:

### Method 1: Direct Pole Computation
Find roots of $F(z)=0$ and check $\vert z_i \vert < 1$.
- ✅ Simple conceptually
- ❌ Tedious for $n \geq 3$ (no algebraic formula like quadratic exists for high-degree polynomials in general)

### Method 2: Schur-Cohn Stability Criterion
A determinant-based algebraic test that does not require root computation.
- ✅ Algorithmic, no root-finding
- ❌ Computationally heavy (nested determinants)

### Method 3: Jury's Stability Test (Most popular in KTU exams)
A **tabular test** analogous to Routh-Hurwitz but for discrete systems.
- ✅ No root-finding, no bilinear transform
- ❌ Conditions can be tricky to remember

### Method 4: Bilinear Transformation + Routh-Hurwitz
Map $z \rightarrow w$ where $z = \frac{1+w}{1-w}$, so the unit circle maps to the imaginary axis of the w-plane. Then apply the classical **Routh-Hurwitz** array.
- ✅ Reuses continuous-time tools
- ❌ Requires careful algebraic expansion

---

## 2.3 Jury's Stability Test — Detailed Mechanics

Given the characteristic polynomial:

$$F(z) = a_0 z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + a_2 z^{n-2} + \dots + a_{n-1} z + a_n = 0$$

with $a_n \neq 0$ (no root at origin) and $a_0 > 0$.

### Necessary Conditions (All Must Hold)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Jury Conditions for Stability:**
> 1. $F(1) > 0$
> 2. $(-1)^n \, F(-1) > 0$
> 3. $\vert a_n \vert < a_0$

### Sufficient Condition Table (The Jury Array)

Construct the following table and compute the quantities $b_k, c_k, \dots$ :

$$\begin{array}{c|cccccc}
\text{Row} & z^n & z^{n-1} & z^{n-2} & \cdots & z^1 & z^0 \\
\hline
1 & a_0 & a_1 & a_2 & \cdots & a_{n-1} & a_n \\
2 & a_n & a_{n-1} & a_{n-2} & \cdots & a_1 & a_0 \\
\hline
3 & b_0 & b_1 & b_2 & \cdots & b_{n-1} & \\
4 & b_{n-1} & b_{n-2} & b_{n-3} & \cdots & b_0 & \\
\hline
5 & c_0 & c_1 & c_2 & \cdots & & \\
6 & c_{n-2} & c_{n-3} & \cdots & & & \\
\vdots & & & & & &
\end{array}$$

where:
$$b_k = \begin{vmatrix} a_0 & a_{n-k} \\ a_n & a_k \end{vmatrix} = a_0 a_k - a_n a_{n-k}$$

$$c_k = \begin{vmatrix} b_0 & b_{n-1-k} \\ b_{n-1} & b_k \end{vmatrix} = b_0 b_k - b_{n-1} b_{n-1-k}$$

**Stability Condition (Complete Jury Test):**
The system is stable if and only if **all of the following hold**:
$$F(1) > 0, \quad (-1)^n F(-1) > 0, \quad \vert a_n \vert < a_0, \quad \vert b_{n-1} \vert < b_0, \quad \vert c_{n-2} \vert < c_0, \quad \dots$$

---

## 2.4 Bilinear Transformation and Routh-Hurwitz

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Bilinear (Möbius) Transformation:**
> $$w = \frac{z-1}{z+1}, \quad \text{equivalently} \quad z = \frac{1+w}{1-w}$$
>
> This maps the **interior of the unit circle** in z-plane onto the **Left Half-Plane** of the w-plane:
> $$\vert z \vert < 1 \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{Re}(w) < 0$$
> $$\vert z \vert = 1 \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; w = j\Omega \text{ (purely imaginary)}$$
> $$\vert z \vert > 1 \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{Re}(w) > 0$$

After substitution $z = (1+w)/(1-w)$ into $F(z)=0$, multiply by $(1-w)^n$ to clear denominators, expand, and obtain a polynomial in $w$ of the same degree. Apply the standard **Routh-Hurwitz array** — the system is stable iff there are **no sign changes in the first column**.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Physical / Mathematical Meaning |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | $z = e^{sT}$ | Fundamental s-to-z mapping |
| 2 | $\vert z \vert = e^{\sigma T}$ | Magnitude relation |
| 3 | $\angle z = \omega T$ | Angle relation (aliasing: $\omega$ and $\omega + 2\pi/T$ are identical in z-plane) |
| 4 | Stable iff $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ for all $i$ | Pole-location stability criterion |
| 5 | $F(1) > 0$ | Jury condition 1 (no positive-real roots) |
| 6 | $(-1)^n F(-1) > 0$ | Jury condition 2 (no negative-real roots) |
| 7 | $\vert a_n \vert < a_0$ | Jury condition 3 (constant-term magnitude bound) |
| 8 | $w = (z-1)/(z+1)$ | Bilinear transformation |
| 9 | $z = (1+w)/(1-w)$ | Inverse bilinear transformation |
| 10 | $\text{Re}(w) < 0 \Leftrightarrow \vert z \vert < 1$ | Stability boundary mapping |
| 11 | $s = (2/T) \cdot (z-1)/(z+1)$ | Tustin (trapezoidal) discretization of $s$ |
| 12 | Primary strip width | $2\pi/T$ in s-plane frequency |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where does this matter in industry?**
> - **Digital Flight Control Systems:** A pole outside the unit circle means the aircraft's pitch oscillates with growing amplitude — catastrophic. Rigorous Jury/Routh-Hurwitz checks are mandatory in DO-178C certification.
> - **DC-DC Switching Converters:** Modern converters operate in discrete-current-mode; their discrete-time model is a sampled-data system. Stability is checked via Jury's test.
> - **Audio Digital Filters:** Poles outside the unit circle cause filter output to blow up → distortion or speaker damage.
> - **Robotics Joint Controllers:** Sample rate $T$ chosen so all closed-loop poles remain well inside the unit circle (typically $\vert z \vert < 0.95$ for robustness margin).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of $z = e^{sT}$ from First Principles

**Starting point:** The ideal sampler output is a train of impulses weighted by the continuous signal:

$$x^*(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT) \, \delta(t-kT)$$

**Laplace Transform of the sampled signal:**

$$X^*(s) = \mathcal{L}\{x^*(t)\} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT) \, e^{-skT}$$

**Substituting** $z = e^{sT}$, so $e^{-skT} = z^{-k}$:

$$X(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT) \, z^{-k}$$

Comparing the two expressions:

$$\boxed{z = e^{sT}}$$

This is the **fundamental correspondence** — every point $s$ in the s-plane maps to a point $z$ in the z-plane via the complex exponential.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Bilinear Transformation Mechanics

**Start:** Characteristic polynomial $F(z) = a_0 z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + \dots + a_n = 0$.

**Substitute** $z = (1+w)/(1-w)$:

$$a_0 \left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right)^n + a_1 \left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right)^{n-1} + \dots + a_{n-1}\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right) + a_n = 0$$

**Multiply both sides by $(1-w)^n$** to clear denominators:

$$a_0(1+w)^n + a_1(1+w)^{n-1}(1-w) + a_2(1+w)^{n-2}(1-w)^2 + \dots + a_n(1-w)^n = 0$$

**Expand** each term using the binomial theorem:

$$(1+w)^k(1-w)^{n-k} = \sum_{j=0}^{k} \binom{k}{j} w^j \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{n-k} \binom{n-k}{i}(-w)^i$$

After full expansion and collecting powers of $w$, one obtains a polynomial:

$$b_0 w^n + b_1 w^{n-1} + b_2 w^{n-2} + \dots + b_{n-1} w + b_n = 0$$

**Apply Routh-Hurwitz** to this polynomial. Stability $\Leftrightarrow$ no sign changes in the first column of the Routh array.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example #1 — Jury's Stability Test (3rd Order)

**Problem:** Determine the stability of a sampled-data system whose characteristic equation is:
$$F(z) = z^3 - 1.5 z^2 + 0.7 z - 0.1 = 0$$

**Step 1 — Identify coefficients:**
$$a_0 = 1, \quad a_1 = -1.5, \quad a_2 = 0.7, \quad a_3 = -0.1, \quad n = 3$$

**Step 2 — Verify $a_0 > 0$:** Yes, $a_0 = 1$.

**Step 3 — Check Condition 1:** $F(1) > 0$?

$$F(1) = (1)^3 - 1.5(1)^2 + 0.7(1) - 0.1 = 1 - 1.5 + 0.7 - 0.1 = 0.1$$

$$F(1) = 0.1 > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Check Condition 2:** $(-1)^n F(-1) > 0$ with $n=3$, so $(-1)^3 = -1$:

$$F(-1) = (-1)^3 - 1.5(-1)^2 + 0.7(-1) - 0.1 = -1 - 1.5 - 0.7 - 0.1 = -3.3$$

$$(-1)^3 \cdot F(-1) = (-1)(-3.3) = 3.3 > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Check Condition 3:** $\vert a_n \vert < a_0$?

$$\vert -0.1 \vert = 0.1 < 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Build the Jury Array:**

Row 1 (z³, z², z¹, z⁰): $1, \; -1.5, \; 0.7, \; -0.1$
Row 2 (reversed): $\quad -0.1, \; 0.7, \; -1.5, \; 1$

Compute $b_k = a_0 a_k - a_n a_{n-k}$ (here $n=3$, $a_n = -0.1$):

$$b_0 = a_0 a_0 - a_n a_3 = (1)(1) - (-0.1)(-0.1) = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$$

$$b_1 = a_0 a_1 - a_n a_2 = (1)(-1.5) - (-0.1)(0.7) = -1.5 + 0.07 = -1.43$$

$$b_2 = a_0 a_2 - a_n a_1 = (1)(0.7) - (-0.1)(-1.5) = 0.7 - 0.15 = 0.55$$

Row 3: $0.99, \; -1.43, \; 0.55$
Row 4 (reversed): $0.55, \; -1.43, \; 0.99$

**Step 7 — Check the additional Jury condition:** $\vert b_{n-1} \vert < b_0$, with $n-1=2$:

$$\vert b_2 \vert = \vert 0.55 \vert = 0.55 \quad \text{vs} \quad b_0 = 0.99$$

$$0.55 < 0.99 \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** All Jury conditions are satisfied $\Rightarrow$ **The system is STABLE.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification by direct root computation** (using numerical methods, e.g., Python/NumPy roots): the polynomial has roots $z_1 \approx 0.234$, $z_{2,3} \approx 0.633 \pm 0.466 j$. Magnitudes $\vert z_1 \vert = 0.234$, $\vert z_{2,3} \vert = \sqrt{0.633^2+0.466^2} \approx 0.786$. Both less than 1, confirming stability. ✓

---

## 3.4 Worked Example #2 — Bilinear Transformation + Routh-Hurwitz

**Same problem:** $F(z) = z^3 - 1.5 z^2 + 0.7 z - 0.1 = 0$.

**Step 1 — Substitute** $z = (1+w)/(1-w)$:

$$\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right)^3 - 1.5\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right)^2 + 0.7\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right) - 0.1 = 0$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by $(1-w)^3$:**

$$(1+w)^3 - 1.5(1+w)^2(1-w) + 0.7(1+w)(1-w)^2 - 0.1(1-w)^3 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Expand term by term:**

$(1+w)^3 = 1 + 3w + 3w^2 + w^3$

$(1+w)^2(1-w) = (1 + 2w + w^2)(1-w) = 1 + 2w + w^2 - w - 2w^2 - w^3 = 1 + w - w^2 - w^3$

$(1+w)(1-w)^2 = (1+w)(1 - 2w + w^2) = 1 - 2w + w^2 + w - 2w^2 + w^3 = 1 - w - w^2 + w^3$

$(1-w)^3 = 1 - 3w + 3w^2 - w^3$

**Step 4 — Combine with coefficients:**

$$\begin{aligned}
& (1 + 3w + 3w^2 + w^3) \\
-\, & 1.5(1 + w - w^2 - w^3) \\
+\, & 0.7(1 - w - w^2 + w^3) \\
-\, & 0.1(1 - 3w + 3w^2 - w^3) \;=\; 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Collect powers of $w$:**

Constant term:
$$1 - 1.5 + 0.7 - 0.1 = 0.1$$

Coefficient of $w$:
$$3 - 1.5 + (-0.7) - (-0.3) = 3 - 1.5 - 0.7 + 0.3 = 1.1$$

Coefficient of $w^2$:
$$3 - 1.5(-1) + 0.7(-1) - 0.1(3) = 3 + 1.5 - 0.7 - 0.3 = 3.5$$

Coefficient of $w^3$:
$$1 - 1.5(-1) + 0.7(1) - 0.1(-1) = 1 + 1.5 + 0.7 + 0.1 = 3.3$$

**Step 6 — Resulting polynomial in $w$:**

$$3.3 \, w^3 + 3.5 \, w^2 + 1.1 \, w + 0.1 = 0$$

**Step 7 — Routh Array:**

$$\begin{array}{c|cc}
w^3 & 3.3 & 1.1 \\
w^2 & 3.5 & 0.1 \\
w^1 & \dfrac{(3.5)(1.1) - (3.3)(0.1)}{3.5} = \dfrac{3.85 - 0.33}{3.5} = \dfrac{3.52}{3.5} \approx 1.006 & 0 \\
w^0 & 0.1 &
\end{array}$$

**Step 8 — Examine first column for sign changes:**

First column entries: $3.3, \; 3.5, \; 1.006, \; 0.1$ — **all positive**, no sign change.

**Conclusion:** **The system is STABLE.** ✓ (Consistent with Jury's test.)

---

## 3.5 Worked Example #3 — Unstable System via Jury

**Problem:** $F(z) = z^2 - 1.2 z + 0.5 = 0$.

**Coefficients:** $a_0 = 1, \; a_1 = -1.2, \; a_2 = 0.5, \; n=2$.

**Condition 1:** $F(1) = 1 - 1.2 + 0.5 = 0.3 > 0 \;\; \checkmark$

**Condition 2:** $(-1)^2 F(-1) = (1)(1 + 1.2 + 0.5) = 2.7 > 0 \;\; \checkmark$

**Condition 3:** $\vert a_2 \vert < a_0 \Rightarrow 0.5 < 1 \;\; \checkmark$

For $n=2$, Jury's test requires **only these three conditions** (no array needed for 2nd order). All three hold, so the system is stable.

**Verification:** Roots are $z = (1.2 \pm \sqrt{1.44 - 2})/2 = 0.6 \pm 0.2j$. Magnitude $= \sqrt{0.36+0.04} = \sqrt{0.4} \approx 0.632 < 1$. ✓ Stable.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example #4 — Marginally Unstable (Pole on Unit Circle)

**Problem:** $F(z) = z^2 - 1.5 z + 1 = 0$.

**Condition 1:** $F(1) = 1 - 1.5 + 1 = 0.5 > 0 \;\; \checkmark$

**Condition 2:** $(-1)^2 F(-1) = 1 + 1.5 + 1 = 3.5 > 0 \;\; \checkmark$

**Condition 3:** $\vert a_2 \vert < a_0 \Rightarrow 1 < 1$ — **FALSE** ✗

**Conclusion:** Fails condition 3, hence **unstable**.

**Verification:** Roots: $z = (1.5 \pm \sqrt{2.25-4})/2 = 0.75 \pm 0.661 j$. Magnitude $= \sqrt{0.5625 + 0.4375} = \sqrt{1} = 1$. Poles lie **exactly on the unit circle** — sustained oscillation, marginally (critically) stable.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 s-plane to z-plane Stability Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPLANE["S PLANE  sigma on horizontal, j omega on vertical"]
        LHP["LEFT HALF PLANE\nsigma less than 0\nDECAY"]
        IMG["IMAGINARY AXIS\nsigma equals 0"]
        RHP["RIGHT HALF PLANE\nsigma greater than 0\nGROWTH"]
    end
    subgraph ZPLANE["Z PLANE  complex z"]
        INSIDE["INSIDE UNIT CIRCLE\nmod z less than 1\nSTABLE REGION"]
        BOUND["ON UNIT CIRCLE\nmod z equals 1\nMARGINAL"]
        OUTSIDE["OUTSIDE UNIT CIRCLE\nmod z greater than 1\nUNSTABLE"]
    end
    LHP -- "z = e to the power sT" --> INSIDE
    IMG -- "z = e to the power sT" --> BOUND
    RHP -- "z = e to the power sT" --> OUTSIDE
    style SPLANE fill:#eef5ff,stroke:#3a6ea5
    style ZPLANE fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#cc7a00
    style LHP fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#1f8a1f
    style IMG fill:#fff8b3,stroke:#b8a300
    style RHP fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a02020
    style INSIDE fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#1f8a1f
    style BOUND fill:#fff8b3,stroke:#b8a300
    style OUTSIDE fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a02020
```

## 4.2 Stability Test Selection — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["GIVEN: Characteristic\nEquation Fz equals 0"] --> B{"Degree n?"}
    B -- "n = 1 or n = 2" --> C["Direct Root Test\nor simple Jury"]
    B -- "n greater than or equal to 3" --> D{"Method Preferred?"}
    D -- "Algebraic, no transform" --> E["JURY STABILITY TEST\nBuild Jury Array\nCheck all conditions"]
    D -- "Reuse Routh Hurwitz" --> F["BILINEAR TRANSFORM\nz equals 1 plus w over 1 minus w\nThen Routh Hurwitz on w"]
    E --> G{"All Jury\nConditions Met?"}
    F --> H{"Any Sign Changes\nin Routh Array?"}
    G -- "Yes" --> I["STABLE SYSTEM"]
    G -- "No" --> J["UNSTABLE SYSTEM"]
    H -- "No" --> I
    H -- "Yes" --> J
    style A fill:#cfe7ff,stroke:#2a5db0
    style E fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style F fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style I fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#1f8a1f
    style J fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a02020
```

## 4.3 Closed-Loop Sampled-Data Control System — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["Reference r of t"] --> SH1["Sample and Hold\nZOH Block"]
    SH1 --> ERR["Comparator\nError e kT"]
    ERR --> CTRL["Digital Controller\nD z"]
    CTRL --> PLANT["Plant Gp s"]
    PLANT --> OUT["Continuous Output c of t"]
    OUT --> SENSE["Sensor H s"]
    SENSE --> FEED["Feedback Path"]
    FEED --> ERR
    style R fill:#dbe9ff,stroke:#2a5db0
    style CTRL fill:#ffe1b3,stroke:#cc7a00
    style PLANT fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#1f8a1f
    style OUT fill:#f0d6ff,stroke:#7a2ab0
    style ERR fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a02020
```

**Closed-Loop Pulse Transfer Function:**
$$\frac{C(z)}{R(z)} = \frac{D(z) \cdot G(z)}{1 + D(z) \cdot G(z) \cdot H(z)}$$

**Characteristic Equation:**
$$1 + D(z) \cdot G(z) \cdot H(z) = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The stability of this closed-loop system depends **only** on the poles of $C(z)/R(z)$, i.e., the roots of the characteristic equation. The **zeros** of the system do NOT affect stability — only the **poles** do.

## 4.4 Aliasing Visualization — Mapping of Multiple s-plane Strips to One z-plane Disk

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SSTRIP["S PLANE INFINITE STRIPS"]
        P0["Primary Strip\nomega between minus pi over T and pi over T"]
        P1["Strip 1\nomega between pi over T and 3 pi over T"]
        Pn["Strip n\ngeneral complementary"]
    end
    subgraph ZONE["Z PLANE"]
        DISK["Single Unit Disk\nALL strips overlap\nhere"]
    end
    P0 --> DISK
    P1 --> DISK
    Pn --> DISK
    style SSTRIP fill:#eef5ff,stroke:#3a6ea5
    style ZONE fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#cc7a00
    style DISK fill:#ffe1b3,stroke:#cc7a00
```

> [!NOTE]
> Because infinitely many s-plane strips map onto the same unit disk, **aliasing** occurs. A high-frequency pole in s-plane can disguise itself as a low-frequency pole in z-plane. The sampling theorem (Nyquist: $\omega_s > 2\omega_{max}$) must be respected to avoid this trap.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** State the condition for stability of a linear sampled-data control system in terms of the locations of its closed-loop poles in the z-plane.

**Model Answer:**
A linear sampled-data system is **asymptotically stable** if and only if **all the closed-loop poles of the pulse transfer function lie strictly inside the unit circle** in the z-plane, i.e., $\vert z_i \vert < 1$ for every pole $z_i$. If any pole lies on the unit circle ($\vert z_i \vert = 1$), the system exhibits sustained oscillations (marginally stable). If any pole lies outside ($\vert z_i \vert > 1$), the system is unstable with unbounded output.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A complete answer must mention the **unit circle** explicitly, not "left half-plane." Mentioning three cases (inside, on, outside) earns full 3 marks.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** What is the bilinear transformation? Why is it used in the stability analysis of sampled-data systems?

**Model Answer:**
The bilinear transformation is the mapping:
$$w = \frac{z-1}{z+1}, \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad z = \frac{1+w}{1-w}$$

It is used because it **maps the interior of the unit circle in the z-plane** ($|z|<1$) **onto the left half of the w-plane** ($\text{Re}(w) < 0$). This converts the discrete-time stability problem into a continuous-time problem, allowing the standard **Routh-Hurwitz criterion** to be applied directly to the transformed characteristic polynomial in $w$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Full 3 marks require (i) the formula, (ii) the mapping correspondence, and (iii) the connection to Routh-Hurwitz.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Question A (14 Marks)

**Q:** A sampled-data control system has the closed-loop characteristic equation:
$$F(z) = z^3 - 1.3 z^2 + 0.6 z - 0.08 = 0$$

Determine the stability of the system using:
**(a)** Jury's stability test.
**(b)** Bilinear transformation followed by the Routh-Hurwitz criterion.

---

#### Part (a) — Jury's Stability Test [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Coefficients:**
$$a_0 = 1, \; a_1 = -1.3, \; a_2 = 0.6, \; a_3 = -0.08, \; n = 3$$

**Step 2 — Condition 1: $F(1) > 0$**

$$F(1) = 1 - 1.3 + 0.6 - 0.08 = 0.22 > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Condition 2: $(-1)^n F(-1) > 0$**

$$F(-1) = -1 - 1.3 - 0.6 - 0.08 = -2.98$$

$$(-1)^3 \cdot (-2.98) = (-1)(-2.98) = 2.98 > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Condition 3: $\vert a_3 \vert < a_0$**

$$\vert -0.08 \vert = 0.08 < 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Build Jury Array:**

Row 1: $1, \; -1.3, \; 0.6, \; -0.08$
Row 2: $-0.08, \; 0.6, \; -1.3, \; 1$

Compute $b_k$ (using $a_3 = -0.08$):

$$b_0 = a_0 a_0 - a_3 a_3 = (1)(1) - (-0.08)(-0.08) = 1 - 0.0064 = 0.9936$$

$$b_1 = a_0 a_1 - a_3 a_2 = (1)(-1.3) - (-0.08)(0.6) = -1.3 + 0.048 = -1.252$$

$$b_2 = a_0 a_2 - a_3 a_1 = (1)(0.6) - (-0.08)(-1.3) = 0.6 - 0.104 = 0.496$$

Row 3: $0.9936, \; -1.252, \; 0.496$

**Step 6 — Additional Condition: $\vert b_2 \vert < b_0$**

$$\vert 0.496 \vert = 0.496 < 0.9936 \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** All Jury conditions satisfied $\Rightarrow$ **System is STABLE.**

---

#### Part (b) — Bilinear Transformation + Routh-Hurwitz [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Substitute** $z = (1+w)/(1-w)$ into $F(z) = 0$:

$$\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right)^3 - 1.3\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right)^2 + 0.6\left(\frac{1+w}{1-w}\right) - 0.08 = 0$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by $(1-w)^3$:**

$$(1+w)^3 - 1.3(1+w)^2(1-w) + 0.6(1+w)(1-w)^2 - 0.08(1-w)^3 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Expand each term:**

$(1+w)^3 = 1 + 3w + 3w^2 + w^3$

$(1+w)^2(1-w) = 1 + w - w^2 - w^3$

$(1+w)(1-w)^2 = 1 - w - w^2 + w^3$

$(1-w)^3 = 1 - 3w + 3w^2 - w^3$

**Step 4 — Combine and collect powers of $w$:**

Constant: $1 - 1.3 + 0.6 - 0.08 = 0.22$
$w$: $3 - 1.3 - 0.6 + 0.24 = 1.34$
$w^2$: $3 + 1.3 - 0.6 - 0.24 = 3.46$
$w^3$: $1 + 1.3 + 0.6 + 0.08 = 2.98$

**Resulting polynomial:**
$$2.98 \, w^3 + 3.46 \, w^2 + 1.34 \, w + 0.22 = 0$$

**Step 5 — Routh Array:**

$$\begin{array}{c|cc}
w^3 & 2.98 & 1.34 \\
w^2 & 3.46 & 0.22 \\
w^1 & \dfrac{(3.46)(1.34) - (2.98)(0.22)}{3.46} = \dfrac{4.6364 - 0.6556}{3.46} = \dfrac{3.9808}{3.46} \approx 1.1505 & 0 \\
w^0 & 0.22 &
\end{array}$$

**Step 6 — First Column Analysis:**

$2.98, \; 3.46, \; 1.1505, \; 0.22$ — **all positive, no sign change**.

**Conclusion:** **The system is STABLE.** ✓ (Consistent with Jury's test result.)

---

### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice)

**Q:** Consider a unity-feedback sampled-data system with sampling period $T = 1$ sec and pulse transfer function:
$$G(z) = \frac{0.5 z + 0.3}{z^2 - 0.8 z + 0.2}$$

**(a)** Derive the closed-loop characteristic equation and determine its stability using the Jury test.
**(b)** If the system is found stable, determine the maximum value of gain $K$ (such that the open-loop gain becomes $K \cdot G(z)$) for which the system remains stable.

---

#### Part (a) — Closed-Loop Characteristic Equation & Jury Test [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Closed-loop characteristic equation:**
$$1 + G(z) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; z^2 - 0.8z + 0.2 + 0.5z + 0.3 = 0$$

$$z^2 - 0.3 z + 0.5 = 0$$

**Step 2 — Coefficients:** $a_0 = 1, \; a_1 = -0.3, \; a_2 = 0.5, \; n = 2$.

**Step 3 — Jury Conditions for $n=2$:**

(i) $F(1) = 1 - 0.3 + 0.5 = 1.2 > 0$ ✓

(ii) $(-1)^2 F(-1) = 1 + 0.3 + 0.5 = 1.8 > 0$ ✓

(iii) $\vert a_2 \vert < a_0 \Rightarrow 0.5 < 1$ ✓

**Conclusion:** All three conditions met $\Rightarrow$ **Closed-loop system is STABLE.**

---

#### Part (b) — Range of $K$ for Stability [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — New characteristic equation with gain $K$:**

$$1 + K \cdot G(z) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; z^2 - 0.8z + 0.2 + K(0.5z + 0.3) = 0$$

$$z^2 + (0.5K - 0.8) z + (0.3 K + 0.2) = 0$$

So: $a_0 = 1, \; a_1 = 0.5K - 0.8, \; a_2 = 0.3K + 0.2, \; n = 2$.

**Step 2 — Apply Jury Conditions:**

(i) $F(1) > 0$:
$$1 + (0.5K - 0.8) + (0.3K + 0.2) = 1 + 0.8K - 0.6 = 0.4 + 0.8K > 0$$

$$\Rightarrow K > -0.5$$

(ii) $(-1)^2 F(-1) > 0$:
$$1 - (0.5K - 0.8) + (0.3K + 0.2) = 1 - 0.5K + 0.8 + 0.3K + 0.2 = 2 - 0.2K > 0$$

$$\Rightarrow K < 10$$

(iii) $\vert a_2 \vert < a_0$:
$$\vert 0.3K + 0.2 \vert < 1 \;\Rightarrow\; -1 < 0.3K + 0.2 < 1$$

$$\Rightarrow -1.2 < 0.3K < 0.8 \;\Rightarrow\; -4 < K < \tfrac{8}{3} \approx 2.667$$

**Step 3 — Intersection of all three ranges:**

$$K > -0.5 \;\cap\; K < 10 \;\cap\; (-4 < K < 2.667)$$

The binding constraint is the third condition.

$$\boxed{-4 < K < 2.667}$$

For positive gain (typical engineering case): $0 < K < 2.667$.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Stability Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting $(-1)^n$ factor in Condition 2** — many students write $F(-1) > 0$ without the sign factor. **Penalty: −1 mark.**
>
> 2. **Reversing the Jury Array** — the second row must be the **reverse** of the first (i.e., $a_n, a_{n-1}, \dots, a_0$). Writing it identically costs **−2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Skipping the array for $n \geq 3$** — conditions 1–3 are *necessary but not sufficient* for $n \geq 3$. You **MUST** build the array and check $\vert b_{n-1} \vert < b_0$ for full marks. **Penalty: −3 marks.**
>
> 4. **Forgetting to multiply by $(1-w)^n$ in bilinear transform** — if you don't clear the denominator, the resulting expression is not a polynomial and Routh-Hurwitz cannot be applied. **Penalty: −2 marks.**
>
> 5. **Sign-error in Routh's first column** — always recompute $b_1 = (a_{n-1} a_{n-2} - a_n a_{n-3})/a_{n-1}$ using the formula; don't eyeball it.
>
> 6. **Confusing $|z|<1$ with $\text{Re}(z)<0$** — they describe different planes. The unit-circle test is for z-plane; the LHP test is for w-plane (post-bilinear).

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Stability rule:** All closed-loop poles must satisfy $|z_i| < 1$ (inside unit circle).
- ✅ **Mapping equation:** $z = e^{sT}$, with $|z| = e^{\sigma T}$ and $\angle z = \omega T$.
- ✅ **Boundary:** Imaginary axis of s-plane maps **onto the unit circle** of z-plane.
- ✅ **Aliasing:** Infinitely many horizontal s-plane strips (height $2\pi/T$) overlap onto the same z-plane disk.
- ✅ **Jury's Test — three necessary conditions for stability:**
  1. $F(1) > 0$
  2. $(-1)^n F(-1) > 0$
  3. $|a_n| < a_0$
- ✅ **For $n \geq 3$:** additional Jury array conditions ($\vert b_{n-1} \vert < b_0$, $\vert c_{n-2} \vert < c_0$, etc.) are also required.
- ✅ **For $n = 2$:** the three conditions alone are sufficient.
- ✅ **Bilinear transform:** $w = (z-1)/(z+1)$ maps unit circle interior to LHP of w-plane.
- ✅ **Procedure for bilinear method:** substitute → multiply by $(1-w)^n$ → expand → collect $w$ powers → apply Routh-Hurwitz → count sign changes in first column.
- ✅ **No sign changes ⇒ stable;** any sign change ⇒ number of RHP roots = number of sign changes ⇒ unstable.
- ✅ **Sample-rate rule of thumb:** keep poles well inside the unit circle (e.g., $|z| < 0.9$) for robustness against parameter drift and unmodelled dynamics.
- ✅ **Cross-check:** Always try to verify with root computation if a calculator is permitted in the exam.
- ✅ **KTU favourite question pattern:** "Determine stability using Jury's test" (7 marks) + "Verify using bilinear transformation" (7 marks) on the **same** characteristic equation.
- ✅ **Aliasing caution:** If sampling rate is too low, even a stable continuous plant can yield an unstable discrete model — always check that $\omega_s > 2\omega_{max}$ (Nyquist–Shannon).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routh Hurwitz Criterion and Jury's Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT636 - ADVANCED CONTROL THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Routh Hurwitz Criterion and Jury's Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Routh–Hurwitz Criterion (Continuous-Time Stability)

### Formal Academic Definition
The **Routh–Hurwitz Criterion** is an algebraic necessary and sufficient condition for assessing the **bounded-input bounded-output (BIBO) stability** of a linear time-invariant (LTI) continuous-time system directly from the coefficients of its characteristic polynomial, without explicitly computing the roots. A system is asymptotically stable if and only if all roots of its characteristic equation lie strictly in the **open left-half of the complex s-plane** ($\Re(s) < 0$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A system described by the characteristic equation $a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \dots + a_{n-1} s + a_n = 0$ is stable **if and only if** all coefficients are positive **and** all elements of the first column of the Routh array are positive (no sign changes).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are balancing a tall stack of books on a wobbly table. You cannot see the individual books falling (the poles), but you can feel the table vibrating. The **Routh–Hurwitz Criterion** is like a "vibration analyzer" — by reading the *pattern* of numbers that come out of the characteristic equation coefficients, you can instantly tell whether the stack will settle (stable) or topple over (unstable), without ever computing each book's exact position. A **sign change** in the Routh array = a "push" that will topple the system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
> - **Order of system (n):** Dimensionless integer defining the polynomial degree.
> - **Auxiliary Polynomial (A(s)):** Highest-order even polynomial extracted when a row of zeros appears.
> - **Critical frequency ($\omega$):** Frequency at which marginally stable poles cross the $j\omega$-axis.

---

## 1.2 The Jury's Test (Discrete-Time Stability)

### Formal Academic Definition
The **Jury's Stability Test** (also called the **Jury–Blanchard Test**) is an algebraic criterion analogous to Routh–Hurwitz, but designed for **linear discrete-time systems**. It determines asymptotic stability by checking whether all roots of the characteristic polynomial $F(z) = a_0 z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + \dots + a_n$ lie strictly **inside the unit circle** of the complex $z$-plane ($\vert z \vert < 1$).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a digital audio equalizer. Each "filter pole" is a tiny memory bubble that can either fade out quickly (good — stable) or persist and grow (bad — oscillation/clipping). The **Jury's Test** is the "audio engineer's check" — you look at the polynomial coefficients, build a structured table, and immediately know whether every pole sits safely inside the "stability zone" (the unit disc) or has escaped to the dangerous outside (where amplification blows up).

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Region for Discrete Systems:** $\vert z \vert < 1$ (interior of unit circle), as opposed to $\Re(s) < 0$ for continuous systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Routh–Hurwitz Criterion — Underlying Logic

### 2.1.1 The Characteristic Equation
For an $n$-th order LTI system, the characteristic equation is:

$$\begin{aligned}
a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + a_2 s^{n-2} + \dots + a_{n-1} s + a_n = 0
\end{aligned}$$

where $a_0 > 0$ (assumed without loss of generality).

### 2.1.2 Necessary Condition for Stability
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hurwitz Necessary Condition:** *All* coefficients of the characteristic polynomial must be **strictly positive** ($a_i > 0$ for $i = 0, 1, 2, \dots, n$). If *any* coefficient is zero or negative, the system is **unstable** by inspection.

### 2.1.3 Routh Array Construction — Step-by-Step Logic

| Step | Operation | Mathematical Operation |
|------|-----------|------------------------|
| 1 | Form the first row ($s^n$) from even-indexed coefficients | $a_0, \; a_2, \; a_4, \; \dots$ |
| 2 | Form the second row ($s^{n-1}$) from odd-indexed coefficients | $a_1, \; a_3, \; a_5, \; \dots$ |
| 3 | Compute the third row using the determinant formula | $b_i = \dfrac{a_1 \cdot a_{2i} - a_0 \cdot a_{2i+1}}{a_1}$ |
| 4 | Continue iteratively — each new row uses the two rows directly above it | $c_i = \dfrac{b_1 \cdot a_{2i+1} - a_1 \cdot b_{i+1}}{b_1}$ |
| 5 | Terminate with two rows: $s^1$ and $s^0$ | The last row is just $a_n$ |
| 6 | Inspect the first column | **All elements must be $> 0$** for stability |

### 2.1.4 The Number of Sign Changes
The number of **sign changes** in the first column of the Routh array equals the **number of roots in the right-half s-plane (RHP)**.

---

## 2.2 Special Cases in Routh Array

### 2.2.1 Case 1: Zero in the First Column (But Row Non-Zero)
> **Remedy:** Substitute a small positive number $\varepsilon$ ($\varepsilon \to 0^+$) in place of the zero, and proceed. Then evaluate the limit as $\varepsilon \to 0$.

### 2.2.2 Case 2: Entire Row of Zeros
> **Remedy:** This indicates the presence of **symmetrically placed roots** (e.g., $\pm j\omega$ pairs or $\pm \sigma$ pairs). Form the **Auxiliary Polynomial $A(s)$** from the row above, then:
> 1. Take the derivative: $\dfrac{dA(s)}{ds}$
> 2. Replace the row of zeros with the coefficients of $\dfrac{dA(s)}{ds}$
> 3. Continue the array construction.

### 2.2.3 Case 3: First Column Ends in Zero
> **Remedy:** Replace the zero with $\varepsilon$, then check sign changes as $\varepsilon \to 0^+$.

---

## 2.3 Jury's Test — Underlying Logic

### 2.3.1 General Form
The characteristic polynomial in $z$:

$$\begin{aligned}
F(z) = a_0 z^n + a_1 z^{n-1} + a_2 z^{n-2} + \dots + a_{n-1} z + a_n
\end{aligned}$$

with $a_0 > 0$.

### 2.3.2 Jury Array Construction

| Row | Construction Logic |
|-----|---------------------|
| Row 1 (Top) | $a_0, \; a_1, \; a_2, \; \dots, \; a_{n-1}, \; a_n$ |
| Row 2 (Bottom) | $a_n, \; a_{n-1}, \; \dots, \; a_1, \; a_0$ |
| Subsequent rows | Coefficients computed from the two rows directly above using a determinant-like formula |
| Last row | Two elements: $b_0, \; b_1$ |

The recursive determinant formula is:

$$\begin{aligned}
c_k = \begin{vmatrix} a_0 & a_{n-k} \\ a_n & a_k \end{vmatrix} = a_0 \cdot a_k - a_n \cdot a_{n-k}
\end{aligned}$$

### 2.3.3 Jury's Stability Conditions (Low-Order Quick Check)

For **$n = 2$** ($F(z) = a_0 z^2 + a_1 z + a_2$):

$$\begin{aligned}
&F(1) > 0 \\
&F(-1) > 0 \quad (\text{since } n \text{ is even}) \\
&a_2 < a_0
\end{aligned}$$

For **$n = 3$** ($F(z) = a_0 z^3 + a_1 z^2 + a_2 z + a_3$):

$$\begin{aligned}
&F(1) > 0 \\
&F(-1) < 0 \quad (\text{since } n \text{ is odd}) \\
&a_3 < a_0 \\
&\Delta = a_3 - a_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert \Delta \vert < a_0
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The general $n$-th order Jury test requires all **$n+1$ conditions** (analogous to the $n$ conditions of Routh–Hurwitz) — the array of determinants must all be positive.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Use of Routh–Hurwitz | Use of Jury's Test |
|------------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Aerospace Control** | Aircraft autopilot stability margins | Digital flight controller (sampled-data) |
| **Robotics** | DC motor arm stability | Sampled joint-position controller |
| **Power Systems** | SMIB generator swing stability | Digital load-frequency controller |
| **Biomedical** | Drug-delivery model stability | Digital infusion pump model |
| **DSP** | Analog filter design | FIR/IIR digital filter pole placement |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Routh Array — Complete Derivation of General Entry

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the Recursive Formula

Let the characteristic polynomial be $P(s) = a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \dots + a_n = 0$.

Consider a generic Routh array entry $b_i$ in the row below $a_0, a_2, a_4, \dots$ and $a_1, a_3, a_5, \dots$:

$$\begin{aligned}
b_i = \frac{\begin{vmatrix} a_1 & a_{2i+1} \\ a_0 & a_{2i} \end{vmatrix}}{a_1}
= \frac{a_1 \cdot a_{2i} - a_0 \cdot a_{2i+1}}{a_1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Textual Explanation:**
- The numerator is a $2 \times 2$ determinant formed by the elements directly above the current entry.
- The denominator is the **first element of the row directly above**.
- This determinant is computed for every column position in the new row.

### 3.1.2 General $n$-th Order Routh Array Template

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
s^n & a_0 & a_2 & a_4 & \cdots \\
s^{n-1} & a_1 & a_3 & a_5 & \cdots \\
s^{n-2} & b_1 & b_2 & b_3 & \cdots \\
s^{n-3} & c_1 & c_2 & c_3 & \cdots \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots \\
s^1 & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot \\
s^0 & a_n & 0 & 0 & 0
\end{array}
\end{aligned}$$

where:

$$\begin{aligned}
b_1 &= \frac{a_1 a_2 - a_0 a_3}{a_1} \\
b_2 &= \frac{a_1 a_4 - a_0 a_5}{a_1} \\
b_3 &= \frac{a_1 a_6 - a_0 a_7}{a_1} \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
c_1 &= \frac{b_1 a_3 - a_1 b_2}{b_1} \\
c_2 &= \frac{b_1 a_5 - a_1 b_3}{b_1} \\
&\;\;\vdots
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Routh–Hurwitz for a 4th-Order System

**Problem:** Determine the stability of the system whose characteristic equation is:

$$P(s) = s^4 + 2s^3 + 3s^2 + 4s + 5 = 0$$

### Step 1 — Verify Necessary Condition
All coefficients are positive: $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ — necessary condition is satisfied.

### Step 2 — Construct the Routh Array

**Row $s^4$:** $1, \; 3, \; 5$

**Row $s^3$:** $2, \; 4, \; 0$

**Row $s^2$:** Compute $b_1$ and $b_2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
b_1 &= \frac{(2)(3) - (1)(4)}{2} = \frac{6 - 4}{2} = \frac{2}{2} = 1 \\
b_2 &= \frac{(2)(5) - (1)(0)}{2} = \frac{10 - 0}{2} = 5
\end{aligned}$$

So Row $s^2$: $1, \; 5$

**Row $s^1$:** Compute $c_1$ and $c_2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
c_1 &= \frac{(1)(4) - (2)(5)}{1} = \frac{4 - 10}{1} = -6 \\
c_2 &= \frac{(1)(0) - (2)(0)}{1} = 0
\end{aligned}$$

So Row $s^1$: $-6, \; 0$

**Row $s^0$:** Compute $d_1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= \frac{(-6)(5) - (1)(0)}{-6} = \frac{-30}{-6} = 5
\end{aligned}$$

So Row $s^0$: $5$

### Step 3 — Final Routh Array

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{array}{c|ccc}
s^4 & 1 & 3 & 5 \\
s^3 & 2 & 4 & 0 \\
s^2 & 1 & 5 & 0 \\
s^1 & -6 & 0 & 0 \\
s^0 & 5 & 0 & 0
\end{array}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Inspect First Column
First column: $\{1, 2, 1, -6, 5\}$

**Sign changes:** $+\to+ \to+ \to-\to+$
- $1 \to 2$: no change
- $2 \to 1$: no change
- $1 \to -6$: **1 sign change** ✓
- $-6 \to 5$: **2nd sign change** ✓

**Conclusion:** The system is **unstable** with **2 roots in the right-half s-plane**.

---

## 3.3 Special Case Worked Example — Row of Zeros

**Problem:** Determine stability of $P(s) = s^5 + 2s^4 + 24s^3 + 48s^2 - 25s - 50 = 0$.

### Step 1 — Initial Array

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{array}{c|ccc}
s^5 & 1 & 24 & -25 \\
s^4 & 2 & 48 & -50 \\
s^3 & b_1 & b_2 & 0
\end{array}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
b_1 &= \frac{(2)(24) - (1)(48)}{2} = \frac{0}{2} = 0 \\
b_2 &= \frac{(2)(-25) - (1)(-50)}{2} = \frac{0}{2} = 0
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Row of Zeros Detected
The entire row $s^3$ is zero → form the auxiliary polynomial from row $s^4$:

$$\begin{aligned}
A(s) = 2s^4 + 48s^2 - 50
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Differentiate

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dA(s)}{ds} = 8s^3 + 96s
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Replace the Row of Zeros

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{array}{c|ccc}
s^5 & 1 & 24 & -25 \\
s^4 & 2 & 48 & -50 \\
s^3 & 8 & 96 & 0 \\
s^2 & c_1 & c_2 & 0 \\
s^1 & d_1 & 0 & 0 \\
s^0 & e_1 & 0 & 0
\end{array}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Continue Calculation

$$\begin{aligned}
c_1 &= \frac{(8)(48) - (2)(96)}{8} = \frac{384 - 192}{8} = 24 \\
c_2 &= \frac{(8)(-50) - (2)(0)}{8} = -50
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= \frac{(24)(96) - (8)(-50)}{24} = \frac{2304 + 400}{24} = \frac{2704}{24} \approx 112.67
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
e_1 &= \frac{(112.67)(-50) - (24)(0)}{112.67} = -50
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — First Column Inspection
$\{1, 2, 8, 24, 112.67, -50\}$ → 1 sign change ($+\to-$).

**Conclusion:** **Unstable**, with 1 RHP root. The roots of $A(s) = 0$ give $\omega = \pm j\sqrt{24}$ and $\pm 1$, confirming marginal stability elements.

---

## 3.4 Jury's Test — Complete Worked Example (3rd Order)

**Problem:** Determine the stability of the discrete system with characteristic polynomial:

$$F(z) = z^3 + 0.5 z^2 - 0.2 z + 0.1 = 0$$

Here: $a_0 = 1, \; a_1 = 0.5, \; a_2 = -0.2, \; a_3 = 0.1$.

### Step 1 — Apply Jury's Conditions for $n = 3$

**Condition 1:** $F(1) > 0$

$$\begin{aligned}
F(1) = 1 + 0.5 - 0.2 + 0.1 = 1.4 > 0 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Condition 2:** $F(-1) < 0$ (since $n=3$ is odd)

$$\begin{aligned}
F(-1) = -1 + 0.5 + 0.2 + 0.1 = -0.2 < 0 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Condition 3:** $\vert a_n \vert < a_0$

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert 0.1 \vert < 1 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Condition 4:** $\vert \Delta \vert < a_0$, where $\Delta = a_3 - a_0$

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta = 0.1 - 1 = -0.9 \\
\vert -0.9 \vert = 0.9 < 1 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Construct the Full Jury Array (Verification)

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
\text{Row} & & & & \\
1 & 1 & 0.5 & -0.2 & 0.1 \\
2 & 0.1 & -0.2 & 0.5 & 1 \\
3 & b_0 & b_1 & b_2 & \\
4 & b_2 & b_1 & b_0 & \\
5 & c_0 & c_1 & & \\
6 & c_1 & c_0 & &
\end{array}
\end{aligned}$$

Compute Row 3:

$$\begin{aligned}
b_0 &= \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 0.1 \\ 0.1 & 0.1 \end{vmatrix} = (1)(0.1) - (0.1)(0.1) = 0.09 \\
b_1 &= \begin{vmatrix} 1 & -0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.5 \end{vmatrix} = 0.5 - (-0.02) = 0.52 \\
b_2 &= \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 0.5 \\ 0.1 & -0.2 \end{vmatrix} = -0.2 - 0.05 = -0.25
\end{aligned}$$

Row 4: $-0.25, \; 0.52, \; 0.09$

Compute Row 5:

$$\begin{aligned}
c_0 &= \begin{vmatrix} 0.09 & -0.25 \\ -0.25 & 0.09 \end{vmatrix} = (0.09)^2 - (-0.25)^2 \\
&= 0.0081 - 0.0625 = -0.0544
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Stability Check
Since $c_0 = -0.0544 < 0$, the system is **unstable**.

**Conclusion:** Despite satisfying conditions 1–4, the determinant-based full array reveals a violation → **unstable** discrete system.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Routh–Hurwitz Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Characteristic Equation P(s) = 0"] --> B["Verify all coefficients are positive"]
    B --> C{"All a_i > 0?"}
    C -->|No| D["STOP: System UNSTABLE"]
    C -->|Yes| E["Construct Routh Array rows s^n and s^(n-1)"]
    E --> F["Compute subsequent rows via 2x2 determinant formula"]
    F --> G{"Row of zeros detected?"}
    G -->|Yes| H["Form Auxiliary Polynomial A(s) from row above, differentiate, replace zeros"]
    G -->|No| I{"Zero in first column only?"}
    I -->|Yes| J["Replace zero with epsilon, take limit as epsilon -> 0+"]
    I -->|No| K["Inspect first column of array"]
    H --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{"Any sign changes in first column?"}
    L -->|No| M["System is STABLE: all roots in LHP"]
    L -->|Yes| N["Count sign changes = Number of RHP roots"]
    N --> O["System is UNSTABLE: report RHP root count"]
```

## 4.2 Jury's Test Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: F(z) = a_0 z^n + ... + a_n = 0"] --> B["Verify a_0 > 0 and a_n < a_0"]
    B --> C{"Condition F(1) > 0?"}
    C -->|No| D["STOP: UNSTABLE"]
    C -->|Yes| E{"n is even?"}
    E -->|Yes| F{"F(-1) > 0?"}
    E -->|No| G{"F(-1) < 0?"}
    F -->|No| D
    G -->|No| D
    F -->|Yes| H["Build full Jury Array of determinants"]
    G -->|Yes| H
    H --> I["Compute all (n+1) determinant rows"]
    I --> J{"All first-column determinants > 0?"}
    J -->|No| D
    J -->|Yes| K["System is ASYMPTOTICALLY STABLE: all poles inside unit circle"]
```

## 4.3 Mapping: Routh-Hurwitz vs Jury's Test (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CT["Continuous-Time Domain"]
        R1["Characteristic Equation in s"] --> R2["Routh Array Construction"]
        R2 --> R3["Stability = All roots with Re s < 0"]
    end
    subgraph DT["Discrete-Time Domain"]
        J1["Characteristic Equation in z"] --> J2["Jury Array Construction"]
        J2 --> J3["Stability = All roots with |z| < 1"]
    end
    R1 -.Bilinear Transform z = (1+s)/(1-s).-> J1
    style CT fill:#E8F4FD
    style DT fill:#FFF4E6
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the necessary and sufficient conditions for stability of a continuous-time LTI system using the Routh–Hurwitz criterion.**

**Model Answer:**

> The characteristic equation of an $n$-th order LTI system is $P(s) = a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \dots + a_n = 0$.
>
> **Necessary condition:** All coefficients $a_i > 0$ for $i = 0, 1, 2, \dots, n$.
>
> **Sufficient condition:** When the Routh array is constructed, **all elements of the first column must be strictly positive** (no sign changes).
>
> The number of sign changes in the first column equals the number of roots of $P(s)$ lying in the right-half s-plane.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the role of the auxiliary polynomial in the Routh array construction?**

**Model Answer:**

> When an **entire row of zeros** appears in the Routh array, it indicates the presence of symmetrically placed roots (such as $\pm j\omega$ or $\pm \sigma$ pairs) in the characteristic equation.
>
> In such a case, an **auxiliary polynomial $A(s)$** is formed using the coefficients of the row immediately above the zero row. Then $A(s)$ is differentiated with respect to $s$, and the coefficients of $\dfrac{dA(s)}{ds}$ replace the row of zeros, allowing the array to be continued.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Construct the Routh array for the characteristic equation $P(s) = s^4 + 5s^3 + 4s^2 + 10s + 30 = 0$. Comment on the stability of the system.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Using the Routh–Hurwitz criterion, find the range of $K$ for which the system with characteristic equation $s^3 + 3s^2 + (K+2)s + 4K = 0$ is stable.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a):

**Step 1:** Identify coefficients: $a_0 = 1, a_1 = 5, a_2 = 4, a_3 = 10, a_4 = 30$.

All positive → necessary condition satisfied.

**Step 2:** Construct the Routh array:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{array}{c|ccc}
s^4 & 1 & 4 & 30 \\
s^3 & 5 & 10 & 0 \\
s^2 & b_1 & b_2 & 0 \\
s^1 & c_1 & 0 & 0 \\
s^0 & d_1 & 0 & 0
\end{array}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $b_1, b_2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
b_1 &= \frac{(5)(4) - (1)(10)}{5} = \frac{10}{5} = 2 \\
b_2 &= \frac{(5)(30) - (1)(0)}{5} = 30
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Compute $c_1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
c_1 = \frac{(2)(10) - (5)(30)}{2} = \frac{20 - 150}{2} = \frac{-130}{2} = -65
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Compute $d_1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
d_1 = \frac{(-65)(30) - (2)(0)}{-65} = 30
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** Final Array:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{array}{c|ccc}
s^4 & 1 & 4 & 30 \\
s^3 & 5 & 10 & 0 \\
s^2 & 2 & 30 & 0 \\
s^1 & -65 & 0 & 0 \\
s^0 & 30 & 0 & 0
\end{array}
\end{aligned}$$

**First column:** $\{1, 5, 2, -65, 30\}$ → sign change: $+\to-$ (at $2 \to -65$); $-\to+$ (at $-65 \to 30$) → **2 sign changes**.

**Conclusion:** The system is **unstable** with **2 poles in the right-half s-plane**.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (b):

**Step 1:** Coefficients: $a_0 = 1, a_1 = 3, a_2 = K+2, a_3 = 4K$.

**Step 2:** Necessary condition: All coefficients must be positive:

$$\begin{aligned}
K + 2 > 0 &\Rightarrow K > -2 \\
4K > 0 &\Rightarrow K > 0
\end{aligned}$$

So $K > 0$ is the base condition.

**Step 3:** Routh Array:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{array}{c|cc}
s^3 & 1 & K+2 \\
s^2 & 3 & 4K \\
s^1 & b_1 & 0 \\
s^0 & c_1 & 0
\end{array}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Compute $b_1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
b_1 = \frac{(3)(K+2) - (1)(4K)}{3} = \frac{3K + 6 - 4K}{3} = \frac{6 - K}{3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Compute $c_1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
c_1 = \frac{\left(\frac{6-K}{3}\right)(4K) - (3)(0)}{\frac{6-K}{3}} = 4K
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** Stability condition — all first column entries positive:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{6 - K}{3} > 0 \Rightarrow K < 6 \\
4K > 0 \Rightarrow K > 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Range of stability:** $\boxed{0 < K < 6}$.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Using Jury's stability test, determine whether the discrete-time system with characteristic equation $F(z) = z^3 - 0.5z^2 + 0.1z - 0.02 = 0$ is stable. Show the complete Jury array.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a unity-feedback discrete system with open-loop transfer function $G(z) = \dfrac{K(z+0.5)}{(z-1)(z-0.6)}$, find the range of $K$ for closed-loop stability using Jury's test.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a):

Given: $F(z) = z^3 - 0.5z^2 + 0.1z - 0.02$. Coefficients: $a_0 = 1, a_1 = -0.5, a_2 = 0.1, a_3 = -0.02$.

**Step 1 — Apply Jury's quick conditions for $n=3$:**

**Condition 1:** $F(1) > 0$

$$\begin{aligned}
F(1) = 1 - 0.5 + 0.1 - 0.02 = 0.58 > 0 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Condition 2:** $F(-1) < 0$ ($n$ is odd)

$$\begin{aligned}
F(-1) = -1 - 0.5 - 0.1 - 0.02 = -1.62 < 0 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Condition 3:** $\vert a_n \vert < a_0$

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert -0.02 \vert = 0.02 < 1 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Build full Jury array:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
\text{Row} & c_0 & c_1 & c_2 & c_3 \\
1 & 1 & -0.5 & 0.1 & -0.02 \\
2 & -0.02 & 0.1 & -0.5 & 1 \\
3 & b_0 & b_1 & b_2 & \\
4 & b_2 & b_1 & b_0 & \\
5 & c_0 & c_1 & & \\
6 & c_1 & c_0 & &
\end{array}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Row 3 entries:**

$$\begin{aligned}
b_0 &= \begin{vmatrix} 1 & -0.02 \\ -0.02 & -0.02 \end{vmatrix} = (1)(-0.02) - (-0.02)(-0.02) = -0.02 - 0.0004 = -0.0204 \\
b_1 &= \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 0.1 \\ -0.02 & -0.5 \end{vmatrix} = -0.5 - (-0.002) = -0.498 \\
b_2 &= \begin{vmatrix} 1 & -0.5 \\ -0.02 & 0.1 \end{vmatrix} = 0.1 - 0.01 = 0.09
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Row 4:** $0.09, \; -0.498, \; -0.0204$

**Step 5 — Compute Row 5:**

$$\begin{aligned}
c_0 &= \begin{vmatrix} -0.0204 & 0.09 \\ 0.09 & -0.0204 \end{vmatrix} = (-0.0204)^2 - (0.09)^2 \\
&= 0.00041616 - 0.0081 = -0.00768384
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Stability check:** Since $b_0 = -0.0204 < 0$, the system is **unstable**.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (b):

**Step 1 — Closed-loop characteristic equation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
1 + G(z) = 0 \Rightarrow (z-1)(z-0.6) + K(z+0.5) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Expand:**

$$\begin{aligned}
z^2 - 1.6z + 0.6 + Kz + 0.5K &= 0 \\
z^2 + (K - 1.6)z + (0.6 + 0.5K) &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

So: $a_0 = 1, \; a_1 = K - 1.6, \; a_2 = 0.6 + 0.5K$.

**Step 3 — Apply Jury's conditions for $n = 2$:**

**Condition 1:** $F(1) > 0$

$$\begin{aligned}
1 + (K - 1.6) + (0.6 + 0.5K) = 0 + 1.5K > 0 \Rightarrow K > 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Condition 2:** $F(-1) > 0$ (since $n=2$ is even)

$$\begin{aligned}
1 - (K - 1.6) + (0.6 + 0.5K) &= 1 - K + 1.6 + 0.6 + 0.5K \\
&= 3.2 - 0.5K > 0 \Rightarrow K < 6.4
\end{aligned}$$

**Condition 3:** $\vert a_n \vert < a_0$

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert 0.6 + 0.5K \vert < 1 \Rightarrow -1 < 0.6 + 0.5K < 1
\end{aligned}$$

Left inequality: $0.6 + 0.5K > -1 \Rightarrow K > -3.2$ (already satisfied if $K>0$).
Right inequality: $0.6 + 0.5K < 1 \Rightarrow 0.5K < 0.4 \Rightarrow K < 0.8$.

**Step 4 — Combine all conditions:**

$$\begin{aligned}
K > 0, \quad K < 6.4, \quad K < 0.8
\end{aligned}$$

**Range of stability:** $\boxed{0 < K < 0.8}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign-check oversight in Routh array:** Students often miss sign changes when a row transitions through **zero** (e.g., $+\to 0 \to -$). A zero in the first column **is** a sign change.
> 2. **Forgetting the auxiliary polynomial:** When a row of zeros appears, students substitute $\varepsilon$ in the first column only. The correct approach is to **differentiate $A(s)$** and replace the entire zero row.
> 3. **Condition sign for $F(-1)$ in Jury's test:** For **odd $n$**, the condition is $F(-1) < 0$; for **even $n$**, the condition is $F(-1) > 0$. Reversing this gives a false instability verdict.
> 4. **Jury's "quick conditions" are necessary, not sufficient:** For $n \ge 3$, the simple conditions only screen; the **full determinant array** is required for final verdict.
> 5. **Losing the second coefficient:** The Routh array row $s^{n-1}$ is built from the *odd-indexed* coefficients, not the entire set. Mis-ordering produces a wrong array.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Routh–Hurwitz** is applicable to **continuous-time** systems; checks if all roots lie in $\Re(s) < 0$.
- **Jury's Test** is applicable to **discrete-time** systems; checks if all roots lie in $\vert z \vert < 1$.
- The **first necessary condition** for both tests is: **all polynomial coefficients must be positive** (after normalizing $a_0 > 0$).
- In the **Routh array**, the number of sign changes in the first column = number of **RHP roots**.
- The Routh array uses a **$2 \times 2$ determinant** divided by the pivot from the row above to compute each new element.
- **Special case 1 (zero in first column):** Substitute $\varepsilon \to 0^+$.
- **Special case 2 (entire row of zeros):** Form $A(s)$ from the row above, differentiate, replace the zero row.
- **Jury's quick conditions for $n=2$:** $F(1) > 0$, $F(-1) > 0$, $\vert a_2 \vert < a_0$.
- **Jury's quick conditions for $n=3$:** $F(1) > 0$, $F(-1) < 0$, $\vert a_3 \vert < a_0$, $\vert a_3 - a_0 \vert < a_0$.
- The **full Jury array** (determinant-based) is the **necessary and sufficient** test, especially for $n \ge 3$.
- The **Bilinear transform** $z = \dfrac{1+s}{1-s}$ maps the $s$-plane LHP to the $z$-plane unit disc — it links the two tests.
- The **number of stability conditions** equals the system order $n$ (Routh) or $n+1$ (Jury full).
- For **marginal stability** detection, the auxiliary polynomial gives the **oscillation frequency** directly from its roots.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to State-Space Representation of Sampled Data Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT636 - ADVANCED CONTROL THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to State-Space Representation of Sampled Data Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In modern control theory, a **Sampled-Data System** is a hybrid system in which a **continuous-time (analog) plant** is controlled by a **digital controller**. The signal flow involves a continuous signal being sampled at discrete instants, processed digitally, and then reconstructed back to a continuous form for the plant. The **State-Space Representation** of such a system provides a complete, time-domain, internal mathematical description of the system's dynamics using first-order differential (continuous) or difference (discrete) equations in matrix form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> A sampled-data control system is one in which the signals at one or more points in the system are in the form of discrete-time pulses or digital words, while the plant itself is continuous. The state-space model offers a unified framework to handle multi-input, multi-output (MIMO), time-varying, and nonlinear systems — making it the modern standard for digital controller design.

### 1.1.1 Continuous-Time State-Space Model (The Plant)

The continuous LTI plant is governed by:

$$
\dot{x}(t) = A\,x(t) + B\,u(t)
$$

$$
y(t) = C\,x(t) + D\,u(t)
$$

Where the variables are defined as:

- $x(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ — state vector (internal memory)
- $u(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ — input vector
- $y(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ — output vector
- $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ — system (state) matrix
- $B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ — input matrix
- $C \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times n}$ — output matrix
- $D \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times m}$ — feedthrough (direct transmission) matrix

### 1.1.2 Discrete-Time State-Space Model (The Digital Core)

After sampling with period $T$, the equivalent discrete-time model is:

$$
x[(k+1)T] = G\,x(kT) + H\,u(kT)
$$

$$
y(kT) = C\,x(kT) + D\,u(kT)
$$

For compactness, we drop the explicit notation of $T$ and simply write $x(k+1)$, $x(k)$, $u(k)$, $y(k)$ — this is the **KTU-recommended shorthand** in exam answers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Digital Thermostat:**
> Imagine you have an old analog oven (the plant) whose temperature rises continuously, but you only check and adjust it every 5 minutes (sampling period $T = 5$ min). At each "check instant," you look at the thermometer (sample the sensor), do a quick calculation on a digital chip (digital processor), and then hold the heating element at a fixed power level until the next 5-minute mark (Zero-Order Hold). The continuous physics of heat flow inside the oven is still continuous, but the *control action* exists only at discrete instants.

- **Continuous plant ($A, B, C, D$)** → The physics of the oven (always true).
- **Sampling switch** → You snapping a photo of the thermometer.
- **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** → The "freeze" of the control signal between samples.
- **Discrete model ($G, H, C, D$)** → A reliable "snapshot-based" prediction of where the oven will be in 5 minutes, given today's snapshot.

The **state-space representation** in this analogy is essentially a *mini weather forecast model* built from snapshots: "If today I am in state $x(k)$, and I apply control $u(k)$, then 5 minutes from now I will be in state $x(k+1)$."

## 1.3 Why Use State-Space for Sampled-Data Systems?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Advantages Over Classical Transfer Functions:**
> 1. Handles **MIMO systems** naturally (no need for multiple SISO transfer functions).
> 2. Represents **internal variables** (states) — not just input-output behavior.
> 3. Directly amenable to **computer simulation and digital implementation**.
> 4. Forms the foundation for **modern controller design** (pole placement, LQR, Kalman filter, observer design).
> 5. Provides a unified time-domain framework for **continuous**, **discrete**, and **sampled-data** systems.

## 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Sampling Period $T$ (seconds)** — The interval between two consecutive samples; the inverse $f_s = 1/T$ is the **sampling frequency in Hz**.
- **Nyquist Rate $f_N = 2 f_{max}$** — The minimum sampling frequency to avoid aliasing (per Shannon's Sampling Theorem).
- **State Dimension $n$** — Number of independent energy-storage elements in the plant.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sampling Process and Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)

In a practical sampled-data system, the analog signal $u(t)$ is converted to a discrete sequence $u(kT)$ by an **ideal sampler**, and the discrete output of the controller is converted back to a continuous signal via a **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)**. The ZOH holds the value of $u(kT)$ constant over the interval $[kT, (k+1)T)$.

### 2.1.1 Analytical Foundation — Discretization of Continuous LTI Systems

Given a continuous system $\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$ with input held constant over each interval, the solution at sample instants is derived by integrating the continuous state equation from $t = kT$ to $t = (k+1)T$:

$$
x[(k+1)T] = e^{AT}\,x(kT) + \left[\int_{0}^{T} e^{A\tau}\,d\tau\right] B\,u(kT)
$$

This naturally yields the **discrete matrices**:

$$
G = e^{AT} \quad \text{(Discrete System Matrix)}
$$

$$
H = \left[\int_{0}^{T} e^{A\tau}\,d\tau\right] B \quad \text{(Discrete Input Matrix)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The matrix exponential $e^{AT}$ is computed using the **series expansion**:
> $$e^{AT} = I + AT + \frac{(AT)^2}{2!} + \frac{(AT)^3}{3!} + \cdots$$
> Alternatively, use the **inverse Laplace transform trick**: $e^{AT} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}$ evaluated at $T$.

### 2.1.2 Output Equation

Because the output is typically sampled instantaneously, the output equation remains unchanged across sampling:

$$
y(k) = C\,x(k) + D\,u(k)
$$

## 2.2 Structured Logic Steps — Building the Discrete Model

**Step 1 — Start with the continuous plant model** $(A, B, C, D)$.

**Step 2 — Identify the sampling period $T$** specified in the problem (e.g., $T = 0.1$ s).

**Step 3 — Compute the discrete system matrix** $G = e^{AT}$. For low-order systems, use the **Cayley-Hamilton Theorem** or **Laplace inverse method**.

**Step 4 — Compute the discrete input matrix** $H$ via $H = \int_{0}^{T} e^{A\tau}\,d\tau \cdot B$. A useful identity is:

$$
H = A^{-1}(G - I)\,B \quad \text{(when } A \text{ is nonsingular)}
$$

**Step 5 — Combine** to write $x(k+1) = Gx(k) + Hu(k)$, $y(k) = Cx(k) + Du(k)$.

**Step 6 — (Optional)** Derive the **pulse transfer function**:

$$
H(z) = C(zI - G)^{-1}H + D
$$

This is the z-domain equivalent of the transfer function, and it is the bridge between state-space and classical z-domain analysis.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Applicable To |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$ | Continuous state equation | Plant (analog) |
| 2 | $y(t) = Cx(t) + Du(t)$ | Continuous output equation | Plant (analog) |
| 3 | $x(k+1) = Gx(k) + Hu(k)$ | Discrete state equation | Digital controller / sampled model |
| 4 | $y(k) = Cx(k) + Du(k)$ | Discrete output equation | Sampled output |
| 5 | $G = e^{AT}$ | Discrete system matrix | Discretization with ZOH |
| 6 | $H = \int_{0}^{T} e^{A\tau}\,d\tau \cdot B$ | Discrete input matrix | Discretization with ZOH |
| 7 | $H = A^{-1}(G - I)B$ | Alternate form of $H$ | When $A$ is invertible |
| 8 | $H(z) = C(zI - G)^{-1}H + D$ | Pulse transfer function | z-domain analysis |
| 9 | $x(k) = G^{k}x(0) + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} G^{k-1-j}Hu(j)$ | General solution | Free + forced response |
| 10 | $y(k) = CG^{k}x(0) + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} CG^{k-1-j}Hu(j) + Du(k)$ | Output response | Time response |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Note on Notation:** Where the original mathematical expression uses "absolute value" notation (e.g., $\vert x \vert$), the table renders it as $\vert x \vert$ using LaTeX's vertical bar without breaking the markdown pipe.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Servo Drives & CNC Machines** — Microcontrollers implement state-feedback laws $u(k) = -Kx(k)$ designed via pole placement on the discrete model.
- **Aircraft Flight Control (Fly-by-Wire)** — Modern Airbus/Boeing systems use sampled-data state-space models at sampling rates of 50–400 Hz.
- **Robotics & Autonomous Vehicles** — Discrete state-space models enable Model Predictive Control (MPC) at every time step.
- **Power Electronics & Motor Control** — Field-Oriented Control (FOC) of 3-phase inverters relies on discretized state models of the motor at kHz sampling rates.
- **Smart Grid & Power Systems** — Discrete state estimation (Kalman filter) for real-time grid monitoring.

## 2.5 Connection Between Continuous and Discrete Poles

A continuous pole $s = p_i$ maps to a discrete pole $z_i = e^{p_i T}$. This **bilinear-like exponential mapping** preserves stability:

- Continuous LHP ($p_i < 0$) $\Longleftrightarrow$ Discrete unit circle interior ($\vert z_i \vert < 1$).
- Continuous imaginary axis ($p_i = j\omega$) $\Longleftrightarrow$ Discrete unit circle ($\vert z_i \vert = 1$).
- Continuous RHP ($p_i > 0$) $\Longleftrightarrow$ Discrete unit circle exterior ($\vert z_i \vert > 1$).
