# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Discretization of Continuous State Equation (Full Working)

We begin with the continuous LTI system:

$$
\dot{x}(t) = A\,x(t) + B\,u(t)
$$

Assume the input $u(t)$ is constant over one sampling interval due to the ZOH, i.e., $u(t) = u(kT)$ for $t \in [kT, (k+1)T)$.

**Step 1 — Write the homogeneous + forced solution form.**

The general solution of $\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$ is:

$$
x(t) = e^{A(t - t_0)}x(t_0) + \int_{t_0}^{t} e^{A(t - \tau)} B\,u(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

**Step 2 — Apply limits $t_0 = kT$ and $t = (k+1)T$.**

$$
x[(k+1)T] = e^{A[(k+1)T - kT]}\,x(kT) + \int_{kT}^{(k+1)T} e^{A[(k+1)T - \tau]}\,B\,u(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

$$
x[(k+1)T] = e^{AT}\,x(kT) + \int_{kT}^{(k+1)T} e^{A[(k+1)T - \tau]}\,B\,u(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $u(\tau) = u(kT)$ (constant due to ZOH).**

$$
x[(k+1)T] = e^{AT}\,x(kT) + \left[\int_{kT}^{(k+1)T} e^{A[(k+1)T - \tau]}\,d\tau\right]\,B\,u(kT)
$$

**Step 4 — Change of variable $\sigma = (k+1)T - \tau$.**

When $\tau = kT \Rightarrow \sigma = T$; when $\tau = (k+1)T \Rightarrow \sigma = 0$; and $d\tau = -d\sigma$. So:

$$
\int_{kT}^{(k+1)T} e^{A[(k+1)T - \tau]}\,d\tau = \int_{T}^{0} e^{A\sigma}\,(-d\sigma) = \int_{0}^{T} e^{A\sigma}\,d\sigma
$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form.**

$$
x[(k+1)T] = \underbrace{e^{AT}}_{G}\,x(kT) + \underbrace{\left[\int_{0}^{T} e^{A\sigma}\,d\sigma\right]B}_{H}\,u(kT)
$$

Hence the discrete model $x(k+1) = Gx(k) + Hu(k)$ is rigorously justified.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Useful Identity $H = A^{-1}(G - I)B$

**Step 1 — Start from $G = e^{AT}$ and the integral form of $H$.**

$$
H = \int_{0}^{T} e^{A\sigma}\,d\sigma \cdot B
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply inside the integrand by $A^{-1} \cdot A$ (assuming $A$ is nonsingular).**

$$
H = \int_{0}^{T} A^{-1} A\, e^{A\sigma}\,d\sigma \cdot B
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the derivative $\frac{d}{d\sigma}\left[e^{A\sigma}\right] = A\,e^{A\sigma}$.**

$$
H = A^{-1} \int_{0}^{T} \frac{d}{d\sigma}\left[e^{A\sigma}\right]d\sigma \cdot B
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.**

$$
H = A^{-1}\left[e^{A\sigma}\right]_{0}^{T} B = A^{-1}\left[e^{AT} - e^{0}\right] B
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $G = e^{AT}$ and $e^{0} = I$.**

$$
\boxed{H = A^{-1}(G - I)B}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** This identity **fails when $A$ is singular** (i.e., $\det(A) = 0$). In such cases, compute $H$ directly via the integral or use Laplace methods.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Solution to the Discrete State Equation

Starting from $x(k+1) = Gx(k) + Hu(k)$ with initial condition $x(0) = x_0$:

**Step 1 — Recursively expand:**

$$
x(1) = Gx(0) + Hu(0)
$$

$$
x(2) = Gx(1) + Hu(2) = G^2 x(0) + GHu(0) + Hu(1)
$$

$$
x(3) = G^3 x(0) + G^2 Hu(0) + GHu(1) + Hu(2)
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the pattern by induction:**

$$
x(k) = G^{k}\,x(0) + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} G^{k-1-j}\,H\,u(j)
$$

This is the **general solution** comprising the **zero-input (free) response** $G^{k}x(0)$ and the **zero-state (forced) response** $\sum_{j=0}^{k-1} G^{k-1-j}\,H\,u(j)$.

**Step 3 — Output response:**

$$
y(k) = C\,x(k) + D\,u(k)
$$

$$
y(k) = C G^{k} x(0) + C \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} G^{k-1-j}\,H\,u(j) + D\,u(k)
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Pulse Transfer Function from State-Space

**Step 1 — Take the Z-transform of $x(k+1) = Gx(k) + Hu(k)$.**

Using the one-step shift property $Z\{x(k+1)\} = z X(z) - z\,x(0)$:

$$
zX(z) - z\,x(0) = G X(z) + H U(z)
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $X(z)$ assuming zero initial conditions** $x(0) = 0$:

$$
(zI - G) X(z) = H U(z)
$$

$$
X(z) = (zI - G)^{-1}\,H\,U(z)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into $Y(z) = C X(z) + D U(z)$.**

$$
Y(z) = C(zI - G)^{-1}\,H\,U(z) + D\,U(z)
$$

$$
Y(z) = \left[C(zI - G)^{-1}\,H + D\right] U(z)
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the pulse transfer function.**

$$
\boxed{H(z) = C(zI - G)^{-1} H + D}
$$

This is the z-domain transfer function obtained directly from the discrete state-space representation — a key exam-ready result.

## 3.5 Worked Analytical Example (Comprehensive)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A continuous system has $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$, $B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$, $D = 0$. Find the discrete state-space model with sampling period $T = 0.1$ s.

**Step 1 — Compute $G = e^{AT}$.**

First compute $(sI - A)^{-1}$:

$$
sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 2 & s+3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\det(sI - A) = s(s+3) + 2 = s^2 + 3s + 2 = (s+1)(s+2)
$$

$$
(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} \begin{bmatrix} s+3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix}
$$

Apply partial fractions:

$$
\frac{s+3}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{2}{s+1} - \frac{1}{s+2}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{1}{s+1} - \frac{1}{s+2}
$$

$$
\frac{-2}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{-2}{s+1} + \frac{2}{s+2}
$$

$$
\frac{s}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{-1}{s+1} + \frac{2}{s+2}
$$

Take the inverse Laplace transform with $t$ replaced by $T$:

$$
G = e^{AT} = \begin{bmatrix} 2e^{-T} - e^{-2T} & e^{-T} - e^{-2T} \\ -2e^{-T} + 2e^{-2T} & -e^{-T} + 2e^{-2T} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $T = 0.1$:**

Numerically, $e^{-0.1} \approx 0.9048$, $e^{-0.2} \approx 0.8187$:

$$
G \approx \begin{bmatrix} 2(0.9048) - 0.8187 & 0.9048 - 0.8187 \\ -2(0.9048) + 2(0.8187) & -0.9048 + 2(0.8187) \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
G \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.9909 & 0.0861 \\ -0.1722 & 0.7326 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $H = A^{-1}(G - I)B$.**

First compute $A^{-1}$:

$$
A^{-1} = \frac{1}{\det(A)} \begin{bmatrix} -3 & -1 \\ 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{2} \begin{bmatrix} -3 & -1 \\ 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.5 & -0.5 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute $G - I$:

$$
G - I \approx \begin{bmatrix} -0.0091 & 0.0861 \\ -0.1722 & -0.2674 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute $(G - I)B$ with $B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$:

$$
(G - I)B = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0861 \\ -0.2674 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute $H = A^{-1}(G - I)B$:

$$
H = \begin{bmatrix} -1.5 & -0.5 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.0861 \\ -0.2674 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.5(0.0861) - 0.5(-0.2674) \\ 1(0.0861) + 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
H = \begin{bmatrix} -0.1292 + 0.1337 \\ 0.0861 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0045 \\ 0.0861 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Final discrete model:**

$$
x(k+1) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9909 & 0.0861 \\ -0.1722 & 0.7326 \end{bmatrix} x(k) + \begin{bmatrix} 0.0045 \\ 0.0861 \end{bmatrix} u(k)
$$

$$
y(k) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} x(k)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** The continuous eigenvalues are $-1, -2$, mapping to discrete eigenvalues $e^{-0.1} \approx 0.9048$ and $e^{-0.2} \approx 0.8187$. Both lie inside the unit circle $\Rightarrow$ **stable discrete system**, consistent with continuous stability.

**Interpretation:**

- The **continuous plant** is the analog process under control (e.g., motor, furnace).
- The **samplers** (yellow) act like "snapshots" at instants $kT$.
- The **digital controller** and **ZOH** together compute and hold the control signal.
- The **summing junction** computes the error between reference and feedback.
- This structure is the **canonical sampled-data control loop** — and our state-space representation $x(k+1) = Gx(k) + Hu(k)$ mathematically describes the combined plant-plus-ZOH block.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### Q1. Define the state-space representation of a sampled-data system. Write the general discrete state equation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A sampled-data system is one in which a continuous plant is interfaced with discrete-time signals via a sampler and a zero-order hold. Its state-space representation in the discrete-time domain is given by:

$$
x(k+1) = G\,x(k) + H\,u(k)
$$

$$
y(k) = C\,x(k) + D\,u(k)
$$

where $x(k) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the state vector, $u(k)$ is the discrete input, $y(k)$ is the sampled output, and $G$, $H$, $C$, $D$ are the discrete state-space matrices. This model captures the complete internal dynamics of the system at sampling instants.

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

### Q2. What is the relation between the continuous and discrete system matrices in a sampled-data system using ZOH?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The discrete system matrix $G$ is obtained from the continuous system matrix $A$ via the matrix exponential:

$$
G = e^{AT}
$$

where $T$ is the sampling period. The corresponding discrete input matrix is:

$$
H = \int_{0}^{T} e^{A\tau}\,d\tau \cdot B = A^{-1}(G - I)B \quad (\text{when } A \text{ is invertible})
$$

The matrices $C$ and $D$ remain unchanged since the output is sampled instantaneously.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Full 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

**Q.A.** **(a)** Derive the discrete-time state-space model of a continuous LTI system $\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$ under zero-order hold sampling with sampling period $T$. Clearly define the discrete matrices $G$ and $H$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the continuous system with

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0
$$

and $T = 0.5$ s, determine the discrete state-space model $(G, H, C, D)$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Setup (1 mark):** Consider the continuous state equation $\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$. Under ZOH, the input $u(t) = u(kT)$ is constant over $[kT, (k+1)T)$.

**Step 2 — General solution form (2 marks):**

$$
x(t) = e^{A(t - t_0)}x(t_0) + \int_{t_0}^{t} e^{A(t - \tau)}B\,u(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

**Step 3 — Apply sampling instants (2 marks):** Substituting $t_0 = kT$, $t = (k+1)T$:

$$
x[(k+1)T] = e^{AT}x(kT) + \int_{kT}^{(k+1)T} e^{A[(k+1)T - \tau]}B\,u(kT)\,d\tau
$$

**Step 4 — Change of variable and final form (2 marks):** With $\sigma = (k+1)T - \tau$, the integral simplifies to $\int_0^T e^{A\sigma}d\sigma$. Therefore:

$$
x(k+1) = \underbrace{e^{AT}}_{G}x(k) + \underbrace{\left[\int_0^T e^{A\sigma}d\sigma\right]B}_{H}u(k)
$$

Thus $G = e^{AT}$ and $H = \int_0^T e^{A\sigma}d\sigma \cdot B$.

---

### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute $e^{AT}$ (3 marks):**

Find $(sI - A)^{-1}$:

$$
sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 0 & s+2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \det = s(s+2), \quad (sI - A)^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{s} & \frac{1}{s(s+2)} \\ 0 & \frac{1}{s+2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Inverse Laplace transform gives:

$$
e^{At} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \frac{1}{2}(1 - e^{-2t}) \\ 0 & e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Substitute $t = T = 0.5$, with $e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$:

$$
G = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.5(1 - 0.3679) \\ 0 & 0.3679 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.3161 \\ 0 & 0.3679 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $H$ using $H = A^{-1}(G - I)B$ (2 marks):**

$A^{-1}$ for triangular $A$ is:

$$
A^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \frac{1}{2} \\ 0 & -\frac{1}{2} \end{bmatrix} \text{... (verify via } A^{-1}A = I\text{)}
$$

Wait, more carefully:

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \Rightarrow A^{-1} = \frac{1}{\det(A)} \text{adj}(A) = \frac{1}{0} \ldots
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Major Pitfall:** $A$ here is **singular** ($\det(A) = 0$), so the formula $H = A^{-1}(G-I)B$ **cannot be used**. Use the direct integral instead.

Direct integration:

$$
H = \int_0^{0.5} e^{A\tau}d\tau \cdot B = \int_0^{0.5} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \frac{1}{2}(1 - e^{-2\tau}) \\ 0 & e^{-2\tau} \end{bmatrix} d\tau \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The second column integrated:

$$
\int_0^{0.5} \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{2}(1 - e^{-2\tau}) \\ e^{-2\tau} \end{bmatrix} d\tau = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{2}\left[\tau + \frac{e^{-2\tau}}{2}\right]_0^{0.5} \\ \left[-\frac{e^{-2\tau}}{2}\right]_0^{0.5} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{2}\left[0.5 + \frac{0.3679}{2} - \frac{1}{2}\right] \\ -\frac{0.3679}{2} + \frac{1}{2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
= \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{2}(0.1839) \\ 0.3161 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0920 \\ 0.3161 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Assemble final model (2 marks):**

$$
x(k+1) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.3161 \\ 0 & 0.3679 \end{bmatrix} x(k) + \begin{bmatrix} 0.0920 \\ 0.3161 \end{bmatrix} u(k)
$$

$$
y(k) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} x(k)
$$

---

### Question B — Alternative 14 Marks (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper Style]**

**Q.B.** **(a)** Explain the concept of Zero-Order Hold (ZOH). Starting from the state-space model $\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$, derive the pulse transfer function matrix $H(z)$ of the equivalent discrete system in terms of the discrete matrices $G$ and $H$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given a discrete state-space system with

$$
G = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 1 \\ 0 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad H = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0
$$

find: (i) the pulse transfer function $H(z)$, and (ii) check whether the system is stable. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Define ZOH (1 mark):** A Zero-Order Hold is a device that holds the value of the discrete control signal $u(kT)$ constant over the interval $[kT, (k+1)T)$, producing a staircase approximation of the continuous signal.

**Step 2 — Discretization result (2 marks):** With ZOH, the discrete model is $x(k+1) = Gx(k) + Hu(k)$ with $G = e^{AT}$ and $H = \int_0^T e^{A\tau}d\tau \cdot B$.

**Step 3 — Z-transform both sides (2 marks):** Taking the unilateral Z-transform:

$$
zX(z) - zx(0) = G X(z) + H U(z)
$$

Assuming zero initial conditions:

$$
(zI - G) X(z) = H U(z) \Rightarrow X(z) = (zI - G)^{-1} H U(z)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute pulse transfer function (2 marks):** From $Y(z) = CX(z) + DU(z)$:

$$
Y(z) = \left[C(zI - G)^{-1} H + D\right] U(z)
$$

$$
\boxed{H(z) = C(zI - G)^{-1} H + D}
$$

---

### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute $(zI - G)$ (1 mark):**

$$
zI - G = \begin{bmatrix} z - 0.5 & -1 \\ 0 & z - 0.3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute inverse (2 marks):**

$$
(zI - G)^{-1} = \frac{1}{(z - 0.5)(z - 0.3)} \begin{bmatrix} z - 0.3 & 1 \\ 0 & z - 0.5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $H(z) = C(zI - G)^{-1} H$ (2 marks):**

First, $(zI - G)^{-1} H$ with $H = [0, 1]^T$ takes the second column:

$$
(zI - G)^{-1} H = \frac{1}{(z - 0.5)(z - 0.3)} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ z - 0.5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Then $C \cdot (\cdot) = \frac{1}{(z - 0.5)(z - 0.3)}$.

$$
\boxed{H(z) = \frac{1}{(z - 0.5)(z - 0.3)}}
$$

**Step 4 — Stability check (2 marks):** The discrete eigenvalues of $G$ are $0.5$ and $0.3$, both satisfying $|z_i| < 1$. Therefore, **the discrete system is stable** (Schur stable).

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps in This Topic:**
> 1. **Singular $A$ — Don't blindly use $H = A^{-1}(G-I)B$!** When $\det(A) = 0$, compute $H$ via direct integration. *(Costs 2–3 marks if missed.)*
> 2. **Forgetting to drop the feedthrough term** $D$ when computing $H(z)$. Always include $D$ in $H(z) = C(zI-G)^{-1}H + D$.
> 3. **Misapplying the Z-transform shift rule.** Use $Z\{x(k+1)\} = zX(z) - zx(0)$, not $zX(z) - x(0)$.
> 4. **Confusing $G$ (matrix) with $G(s)$ (transfer function).** They are entirely different objects.
> 5. **Stability criterion confusion.** Continuous: poles in LHP. Discrete: poles inside unit circle $|z| < 1$.
> 6. **Not specifying the sampling period $T$.** The matrices $G$ and $H$ are functions of $T$; examiners expect $T$ to be quoted.
> 7. **Forgetting ZOH assumption.** Always state "assuming zero-order hold" before discretization.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Page Rapid Revision:**
>
> ☐ **Continuous plant model:** $\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$, $y(t) = Cx(t) + Du(t)$.
>
> ☐ **Sampled-data system:** Combines continuous plant + sampler + digital controller + ZOH.
>
> ☐ **Discrete state equation:** $x(k+1) = Gx(k) + Hu(k)$, $y(k) = Cx(k) + Du(k)$.
>
> ☐ **Core discretization formulas:** $G = e^{AT}$, $H = \int_0^T e^{A\tau}d\tau \cdot B = A^{-1}(G-I)B$ (only if $A$ invertible).
>
> ☐ **Pulse transfer function:** $H(z) = C(zI - G)^{-1}H + D$.
>
> ☐ **General discrete solution:** $x(k) = G^k x(0) + \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} G^{k-1-j} H u(j)$.
>
> ☐ **Stability:** Continuous LHP ↔ Discrete interior of unit circle ($|z| < 1$).
>
> ☐ **Eigenvalue mapping:** $z_i = e^{p_i T}$ where $p_i$ are eigenvalues of $A$.
>
> ☐ **ZOH rule:** Input held constant over each sampling interval — must be assumed unless stated otherwise.
>
> ☐ **Singular $A$ workaround:** Direct integration or numerical approximation of the matrix exponential.
>
> ☐ **Output equation is unchanged** across sampling since $y(t)$ is sampled instantaneously.
>
> ☐ **Advantages over TF:** Handles MIMO, internal states, time-varying, and is digital-ready.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT636 - ADVANCED CONTROL THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Modern Control Theory (State Space Analysis)** is a time-domain approach to the analysis and design of control systems, where the system is described by a set of first-order differential equations expressed in terms of state variables, inputs, and outputs, rather than by a single high-order transfer function. It forms the foundation of the analysis covered throughout **Module 3** of KTU's Advanced Control Theory syllabus.

The fundamental state-space representation of a linear, time-invariant (LTI) system is given by:

$$
\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)
$$

$$
y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t)
$$

where the state vector $x(t)$ has dimension $n \times 1$, input vector $u(t)$ is $r \times 1$, and output vector $y(t)$ is $m \times 1$. The matrices $A$, $B$, $C$, $D$ are of dimensions $n \times n$, $n \times r$, $m \times n$, and $m \times r$ respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 introduces **State Space Analysis** — a complete paradigm shift from the classical frequency-domain methods studied in earlier modules. State space methods are essential for handling **Multi-Input Multi-Output (MIMO)**, **nonlinear**, and **time-varying** systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **State Variable Definition:** A state variable is the smallest set of variables such that the knowledge of these variables at time $t = t_0$, together with the input for $t \geq t_0$, completely determines the behaviour of the system for any time $t \geq t_0$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "GPS Tracking" Intuition

Imagine you are tracking a delivery drone flying through a city. Knowing **only** its current position is not enough — you also need its **velocity**, **acceleration**, and **battery level** to predict where it will be in 5 seconds. Together, these variables form the "**state**" of the drone at any instant.

In classical control (transfer functions), you would only look at the **input-output relationship** — like knowing where the drone started and where it ended, but not how it got there. State space analysis, in contrast, gives you a **complete internal snapshot** of the system at every instant, just like a GPS tracking dashboard.

- **Position, velocity, acceleration** → State variables
- **Pilot's commands (thrust, turn)** → Input $u(t)$
- **Drone's coordinates on map** → Output $y(t)$
- **Internal physics of drone dynamics** → Matrix $A$

## 1.3 Why State Space Analysis? — The Need for Modern Control

Classical control theory (covered in earlier modules) suffers from several limitations:

1. **Single-Input Single-Output (SISO) Only:** Transfer functions struggle to handle systems with multiple inputs and outputs.
2. **Time-Invariant Assumption:** Classical methods assume constant system parameters, which fails for adaptive and time-varying systems.
3. **No Initial Condition Insight:** Transfer functions are zero-state responses only; they don't reveal internal behaviour.
4. **Limited to Linear Systems:** Classical theory has restricted tools for nonlinear systems.
5. **No Optimality Framework:** Classical design (root locus, Bode, Nyquist) doesn't easily incorporate optimization.

State space analysis overcomes **all** of these limitations, making it the backbone of modern aerospace, robotics, process control, and autonomous systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Notation Used in KTU Module 3:**
> - $A$ → System matrix (describes internal dynamics)
> - $B$ → Input matrix (coupling from inputs to states)
> - $C$ → Output matrix (coupling from states to outputs)
> - $D$ → Feedforward matrix (direct input-to-output coupling)
> - $x(t)$ → State vector
> - $u(t)$ → Input vector
> - $y(t)$ → Output vector
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Historical Context and Evolution

The state space approach was developed primarily by **Rudolf Kalman** in the late 1950s and early 1960s. It was a response to the inadequacies of classical control in handling the increasingly complex systems emerging from the **space race era**, such as satellite attitude control and missile guidance systems.

The two key conceptual leaps introduced were:

1. **Time-Domain Formulation:** Working directly with differential equations rather than transforming to the frequency domain (Laplace / Fourier).
2. **Internal State Description:** Recognizing that a system's future behaviour depends not just on inputs and outputs, but on a **minimum set of internal variables** — the state.

## 2.2 The Concept of State — Formalized

A system $\Sigma$ at time $t_0$ is said to be in **state $x(t_0)$** if the output $y(t)$ for all $t \geq t_0$ is uniquely determined by:
- The state $x(t_0)$ at $t = t_0$
- The input $u(t)$ for $t \geq t_0$

The **state vector** $x(t)$ is a column vector containing all independent state variables:

$$
x(t) = \begin{bmatrix} x_1(t) \\ x_2(t) \\ \vdots \\ x_n(t) \end{bmatrix}
$$

The number of state variables $n$ is called the **order** of the system, and it equals the order of the highest derivative in the governing differential equation.

## 2.3 The Two Pillars of State Space Analysis

The complete state space model is built on two coupled vector equations:

### **Pillar 1: State Equation**
$$
\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)
$$

This is a **first-order vector differential equation** describing how the state evolves under the influence of itself (through $A$) and the input (through $B$).

### **Pillar 2: Output Equation**
$$
y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t)
$$

This is an **algebraic equation** showing how the measurable output is constructed from the state and the input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Splitting into Two Equations:** Splitting the system into a dynamic part (state equation) and a static part (output equation) is mathematically elegant. It separates **what happens inside the system** (state dynamics) from **what we measure outside** (output). This separation is what makes state space analysis so powerful for complex MIMO systems.

## 2.4 Comparison: Classical vs. Modern (State Space) Approach

| Feature | Classical (Transfer Function) | Modern (State Space) |
|---|---|---|
| **Domain** | Frequency (s-domain) | Time domain |
| **System Type** | SISO, LTI primarily | MIMO, LTV, nonlinear possible |
| **Representation** | Single high-order TF | Set of first-order ODEs |
| **Initial Conditions** | Lost in TF form | Naturally incorporated |
| **Internal Variables** | Hidden (black box) | Explicitly modelled as states |
| **Design Tools** | Root locus, Bode, Nyquist | Pole placement, LQR, observers |
| **Computation** | Hand-friendly | Matrix-based (computer-friendly) |
| **Optimal Control** | Limited framework | Built-in (e.g., LQR) |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS — Memorize These for KTU ESE**

| # | Formula | Description | Dimensions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$ | Continuous-time state equation | $A: n \times n$, $B: n \times r$ |
| 2 | $y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t)$ | Continuous-time output equation | $C: m \times n$, $D: m \times r$ |
| 3 | $x(k+1) = G x(k) + H u(k)$ | Discrete-time state equation | $G: n \times n$, $H: n \times r$ |
| 4 | $y(k) = C x(k) + D u(k)$ | Discrete-time output equation | Same as continuous $C, D$ |
| 5 | $n = $ order of system | Number of state variables | scalar |
| 6 | $x(t) = e^{A(t-t_0)} x(t_0) + \int_{t_0}^{t} e^{A(t-\tau)} B u(\tau) \, d\tau$ | General solution (preview of STM) | vector |
| 7 | $\Phi(t) = e^{At} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(sI - A)^{-1}\}$ | State Transition Matrix | $n \times n$ |
| 8 | $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D$ | Transfer function from state space | $m \times r$ |

> **Units Note:** In pure state space form, the variables $x$, $u$, $y$ are **dimensionless mathematical abstractions**; their physical units depend on the application (e.g., radians for angular position, volts for voltage, metres for displacement).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

State space analysis is **indispensable** in:

1. **Aerospace:** Satellite attitude control (3-axis stabilization with MIMO dynamics)
2. **Robotics:** Manipulator arm trajectory control with multiple joints
3. **Automotive:** Active suspension, electronic stability control, autonomous driving
4. **Power Systems:** Multi-machine grid stability analysis
5. **Process Control:** Chemical reactor temperature/pressure MIMO loops
6. **Economics & Biology:** Population dynamics, predator-prey models
7. **Filter Design:** Kalman filters for navigation and signal processing
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From nth-Order ODE to State Space Form

The standard procedure KTU examiners expect is to convert a single high-order differential equation into a set of first-order state equations.

### **Step 1: Start with an nth-Order Linear ODE**

Consider a general $n$th-order linear differential equation with input $u(t)$ and output $y(t)$:

$$
\frac{d^n y}{dt^n} + a_{n-1} \frac{d^{n-1} y}{dt^{n-1}} + \cdots + a_1 \frac{dy}{dt} + a_0 y = b_0 u
$$

This single equation is **one** high-order ODE. Our goal is to break it into $n$ first-order ODEs.

### **Step 2: Define State Variables**

Choose state variables as the output and its successive derivatives (a method called the **Phase Variable Form** or **Controllable Canonical Form**):

$$
x_1 = y
$$

$$
x_2 = \dot{y} = \dot{x}_1
$$

$$
x_3 = \ddot{y} = \dot{x}_2
$$

$$
\vdots
$$

$$
x_n = y^{(n-1)} = \dot{x}_{n-1}
$$

### **Step 3: Compute Derivatives of State Variables**

From the definitions, the first $n-1$ derivatives are trivial:

$$
\dot{x}_1 = x_2
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2 = x_3
$$

$$
\vdots
$$

$$
\dot{x}_{n-1} = x_n
$$

### **Step 4: Express the Highest Derivative Using the Original ODE**

From the original equation:

$$
y^{(n)} = -a_{n-1} y^{(n-1)} - \cdots - a_1 \dot{y} - a_0 y + b_0 u
$$

Substituting the state variable definitions:

$$
\dot{x}_n = -a_0 x_1 - a_1 x_2 - \cdots - a_{n-1} x_n + b_0 u
$$

### **Step 5: Arrange into Matrix Form**

Collecting all $n$ first-order equations into $\dot{x} = A x + B u$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_1 &= 0 \cdot x_1 + 1 \cdot x_2 + 0 \cdot x_3 + \cdots + 0 \cdot x_n + 0 \cdot u \\
\dot{x}_2 &= 0 \cdot x_1 + 0 \cdot x_2 + 1 \cdot x_3 + \cdots + 0 \cdot x_n + 0 \cdot u \\
&\vdots \\
\dot{x}_{n-1} &= 0 \cdot x_1 + 0 \cdot x_2 + \cdots + 0 \cdot x_{n-1} + 1 \cdot x_n + 0 \cdot u \\
\dot{x}_n &= -a_0 x_1 - a_1 x_2 - \cdots - a_{n-1} x_n + b_0 u
\end{aligned}
$$

The state equation in matrix form:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \\ \vdots \\ \dot{x}_{n-1} \\ \dot{x}_n \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & & & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \\ -a_0 & -a_1 & -a_2 & \cdots & -a_{n-1} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ \vdots \\ x_{n-1} \\ x_n \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \\ b_0 \end{bmatrix} u
$$

The output equation (with $y = x_1$):

$$
y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ \vdots \\ x_{n-1} \\ x_n \end{bmatrix}
$$

### **Step 6: Worked Example — Second-Order System**

Consider:

$$
\ddot{y} + 4\dot{y} + 3y = 2u
$$

Comparing with the standard form:

$$
\ddot{y} + a_1 \dot{y} + a_0 y = b_0 u \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_1 = 4,\ a_0 = 3,\ b_0 = 2
$$

The state space form is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -3 & -4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} u
$$

$$
y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

with $x_1 = y$ and $x_2 = \dot{y}$.

## 3.2 Verification: Recovering the Transfer Function from State Space

KTU examiners often ask to verify state space models by deriving the transfer function back.

The transfer function matrix is:

$$
G(s) = C (sI - A)^{-1} B + D
$$

For the second-order example above:

$$
sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 3 & s+4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\det(sI - A) = s(s+4) + 3 = s^2 + 4s + 3
$$

$$
(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{s^2 + 4s + 3} \begin{bmatrix} s+4 & 1 \\ -3 & s \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
(sI - A)^{-1} B = \frac{1}{s^2 + 4s + 3} \begin{bmatrix} s+4 & 1 \\ -3 & s \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{s^2 + 4s + 3} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 2s \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
C(sI - A)^{-1}B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{s^2 + 4s + 3} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 2s \end{bmatrix} = \frac{2}{s^2 + 4s + 3}
$$

The recovered transfer function matches the original system. **Verification complete.** ✓

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Block Diagram Representation

The state space model can be visualized as an interconnected block diagram:

```
+------+    +----+    +------+    +----+    +------+
|  u   |--->|  B |--->|  ∫   |--->|  x  |--->|  C   |---> y
+------+    +----+    +------+    +----+    +------+
                          ^                     |
                          |                     v
                          |                  +-----+
                          |                  |  D  |
                          |                  +-----+
                          |                     |
                          +---------------------+
                                 (feedback via A)
```

This shows:
- The input $u$ is multiplied by $B$, integrated to produce $x$
- The state $x$ is fed back through $A$ and also goes to $C$ to produce $y$
- The direct path $D u$ adds to the output

## 3.4 Existence and Uniqueness of State Space Models

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Theorem (KTU 2024 CO Mapping):** For any LTI system described by an $n$th-order differential equation, there exist **infinitely many** valid state space representations $(A, B, C, D)$. This non-uniqueness arises because any non-singular linear transformation $\bar{x} = T x$ produces an equivalent state space model. This concept is foundational to Module 3's discussion of **controllability canonical form** and **observable canonical form**.

Two state space realizations $(A_1, B_1, C_1, D_1)$ and $(A_2, B_2, C_2, D_2)$ are **algebraically equivalent** if there exists a non-singular matrix $T$ such that:

$$
A_2 = T A_1 T^{-1}, \quad B_2 = T B_1, \quad C_2 = C_1 T^{-1}, \quad D_2 = D_1
$$

Both realizations yield the **same transfer function matrix** $G(s)$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow Diagram — Classical vs. Modern Control Paradigm

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Physical System / Plant] --> B{Modelling Approach}
    B --> C1[Classical Approach]
    B --> C2[Modern State Space Approach]
    
    C1 --> D1[High-Order ODE]
    D1 --> E1[Laplace Transform]
    E1 --> F1[Transfer Function G s]
    F1 --> G1[Frequency Domain Analysis]
    G1 --> H1[Root Locus, Bode, Nyquist]
    H1 --> I1[SISO Compensator Design]
    
    C2 --> D2[First-Order ODE Set]
    D2 --> E2[State and Output Equations]
    E2 --> F2[Matrix Form A, B, C, D]
    F2 --> G2[Time Domain Analysis]
    G2 --> H2[State Transition Matrix]
    H2 --> I2[Controllability, Observability]
    I2 --> J2[State Feedback, Observer Design]
    J2 --> K2[MIMO Optimal Control LQR]
```

## 4.2 State Space Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[Input Vector u t] --> BX[Input Matrix B]
    BX --> SUM1((Sum))
    A[System Matrix A] --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> INT[Integrator Block]
    INT --> X[State Vector x t]
    X --> CX[Output Matrix C]
    CX --> SUM2((Sum))
    X --> A
    U --> DU[Feedforward Matrix D]
    DU --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> Y[Output Vector y t]
```

## 4.3 State Space Realization Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[System Order n and ODE] --> S1{Choose State Variable Definition}
    S1 --> S2[Phase Variable Form]
    S1 --> S3[Controllable Canonical Form]
    S1 --> S4[Observable Canonical Form]
    S1 --> S5[Diagonal / Jordan Form]
    S1 --> S6[Physical / Minimal Form]
    
    S2 --> M[System Matrices A, B, C, D]
    S3 --> M
    S4 --> M
    S5 --> M
    S6 --> M
    
    M --> V[Verification via G s equals C sI minus A inverse B plus D]
    V --> E[Equivalent Realization Check using T]
```

## 4.4 Application Domain Map

```mermaid
mindmap
    root((State Space Applications))
        Aerospace
            Satellite Attitude
            Missile Guidance
            Aircraft Autopilot
        Robotics
            Manipulator Arms
            Mobile Robots
            Drone Control
        Automotive
            Active Suspension
            Stability Control
            Autonomous Driving
        Industrial
            Process Control MIMO
            Power Grid Stability
            Manufacturing Automation
        Signal Processing
            Kalman Filtering
            Noise Estimation
            Sensor Fusion
```

## 4.5 State Space Model — Component Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Physical["Physical System"]
        P1[Plant Dynamics]
    end
    
    subgraph StateSpace["Mathematical Model"]
        M1[Matrix A Internal Coupling]
        M2[Matrix B Input Coupling]
        M3[Matrix C Output Coupling]
        M4[Matrix D Direct Coupling]
    end
    
    subgraph Analysis["Module 3 Analysis Tools"]
        T1[State Transition Matrix]
        T2[Controllability Test]
        T3[Observability Test]
        T4[Pole Placement]
        T5[Observer Design]
    end
    
    P1 --> M1
    P1 --> M2
    P1 --> M3
    P1 --> M4
    M1 --> T1
    M1 --> T2
    M3 --> T3
    M1 --> T4
    M3 --> T5
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define state variable and state vector with reference to a control system. Why are state space methods preferred for MIMO systems?**

**Model Answer:**

A **state variable** is the minimum set of variables $x_1(t), x_2(t), \ldots, x_n(t)$ such that the knowledge of these variables at initial time $t_0$, together with the input $u(t)$ for $t \geq t_0$, completely determines the future behaviour of the system.

A **state vector** $x(t)$ is the column vector formed by all the independent state variables:

$$
x(t) = \begin{bmatrix} x_1(t) \\ x_2(t) \\ \vdots \\ x_n(t) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Why preferred for MIMO:** In MIMO systems with $r$ inputs and $m$ outputs, the transfer function approach requires $m \times r$ transfer functions, which is cumbersome and loses cross-coupling information. The state space method uses a single compact matrix representation $(A, B, C, D)$ that explicitly captures all internal couplings and is computationally efficient for analysis and design. **[3 marks]**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Compare the classical control approach and the modern state space approach in any four aspects.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Classical | Modern (State Space) |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Domain | Frequency (s-domain) | Time domain |
| 2. System type | SISO, LTI mainly | MIMO, LTV, nonlinear possible |
| 3. Initial conditions | Not naturally handled | Explicitly incorporated in state $x(0)$ |
| 4. Internal dynamics | Hidden (black-box transfer function) | Explicitly represented via state variables |

**[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B Module 3 question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the state space representation in **phase variable form** for the following differential equation:

$$
\frac{d^3 y}{dt^3} + 6 \frac{d^2 y}{dt^2} + 11 \frac{dy}{dt} + 6 y = 4u(t)
$$

Obtain the $A$, $B$, $C$, $D$ matrices clearly. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Comparing with the standard form:

$$
\dddot{y} + a_2 \ddot{y} + a_1 \dot{y} + a_0 y = b_0 u
$$

We have $a_2 = 6$, $a_1 = 11$, $a_0 = 6$, $b_0 = 4$, and $n = 3$.

**Define state variables:**

$$
x_1 = y, \quad x_2 = \dot{y}, \quad x_3 = \ddot{y}
$$

**First-order equations:**

$$
\dot{x}_1 = x_2
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2 = x_3
$$

$$
\dot{x}_3 = -a_0 x_1 - a_1 x_2 - a_2 x_3 + b_0 u = -6 x_1 - 11 x_2 - 6 x_3 + 4u
$$

**Matrix form:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \\ \dot{x}_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} u(t)
$$

$$
y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Therefore:

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0
$$

**[7 marks]**

---

**(b)** For the system obtained in part (a), derive the transfer function $G(s)$ from the state space model and show that it matches the transfer function obtained directly from the differential equation. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

From the state space model, the transfer function is:

$$
G(s) = C (sI - A)^{-1} B + D
$$

Compute $sI - A$:

$$
sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & s & -1 \\ 6 & 11 & s+6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute the determinant (characteristic polynomial):

$$
\det(sI - A) = s \cdot \det\begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 11 & s+6 \end{bmatrix} - (-1) \cdot \det\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 6 & s+6 \end{bmatrix} + 0
$$

$$
= s[s(s+6) + 11] + 1[0 \cdot (s+6) - (-1)(6)]
$$

$$
= s(s^2 + 6s + 11) + 6 = s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6
$$

The characteristic polynomial factors as $(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)$.

For the transfer function numerator, compute the cofactor expansion (only last column of adjugate matters due to $B$ structure):

The relevant entry of $(sI - A)^{-1} B$ simplifies to:

$$
(sI - A)^{-1} B = \frac{4}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ s \\ s^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Multiplying by $C = [1\ 0\ 0]$:

$$
G(s) = \frac{4}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}
$$

This matches the direct Laplace transform of the original ODE:

$$
G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{4}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}
$$

**Verification complete. Both expressions are identical.** ✓ **[7 marks]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of state, state variables, and state space with suitable examples. Discuss the non-uniqueness of state space representation. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**State:** The state of a dynamic system at time $t_0$ is the smallest amount of information necessary to determine the future behaviour of the system for $t \geq t_0$, given the input for $t \geq t_0$.

**State Variables:** These are the minimum set of independent variables $x_1(t), x_2(t), \ldots, x_n(t)$ that fully describe the system's state. For an $n$th-order system, exactly $n$ such variables are needed.

**Example:** For an RLC series circuit with input voltage $u(t)$ and output capacitor voltage $y(t)$:
- The system is 2nd order
- State variables: $x_1 = i_L$ (inductor current), $x_2 = v_C$ (capacitor voltage)
- These two variables, along with input, fully determine the future circuit behaviour

**State Space:** The $n$-dimensional space in which the state vector $x(t)$ resides. Each point in this space represents a unique system state.

**Non-uniqueness of state space representation:** For a given physical system, infinitely many valid state space models exist. This is because:

1. The choice of state variables is not unique — any set of $n$ independent variables that fully determine the system state works.
2. Any non-singular linear transformation $T$ (with $\bar{x} = Tx$) produces an equivalent realization $(\bar{A}, \bar{B}, \bar{C}, \bar{D})$ with:

$$
\bar{A} = T A T^{-1}, \quad \bar{B} = T B, \quad \bar{C} = C T^{-1}
$$

3. All these realizations produce the same input-output transfer function.

Common canonical forms include: Phase Variable, Controllable Canonical, Observable Canonical, Diagonal, Jordan, and Physical forms. **[7 marks]**

---

**(b)** A system is described by the state space equations:

$$
\dot{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} x + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u, \quad y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} x
$$

Find (i) the transfer function $G(s)$ and (ii) the characteristic equation of the system. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Transfer function $G(s)$:**

$$
G(s) = C (sI - A)^{-1} B + D, \quad D = 0
$$

Compute $sI - A$:

$$
sI - A = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 2 & s+3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Determinant:

$$
\det(sI - A) = s(s+3) - (-1)(2) = s^2 + 3s + 2
$$

Inverse (using adjugate formula for 2×2):

$$
(sI - A)^{-1} = \frac{1}{s^2 + 3s + 2} \begin{bmatrix} s+3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix}
$$

Multiply by $B$:

$$
(sI - A)^{-1} B = \frac{1}{s^2 + 3s + 2} \begin{bmatrix} s+3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{s^2 + 3s + 2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ s \end{bmatrix}
$$

Multiply by $C = [1\ 0]$:

$$
G(s) = \frac{1}{s^2 + 3s + 2} = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)}
$$

**(ii) Characteristic equation:**

The characteristic equation is $\det(sI - A) = 0$:

$$
s^2 + 3s + 2 = 0
$$

Factoring: $(s+1)(s+2) = 0$, giving eigenvalues $s_1 = -1$ and $s_2 = -2$. Since both eigenvalues have **negative real parts**, the system is **asymptotically stable**. **[7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong state variable ordering:** Always define $x_1 = y$ (output) and $x_2 = \dot{y}$, $x_3 = \ddot{y}$, etc. Reversed order leads to incorrect $A$ matrix and **zero marks** in part (a).
> 2. **Sign errors in $A$ matrix:** The bottom row of $A$ contains the **negatives** of the ODE coefficients ($-a_0, -a_1, \ldots, -a_{n-1}$). Forgetting the minus sign is the most common mistake — **deducts 2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting initial conditions discussion:** When asked "advantages of state space," always explicitly mention that initial conditions are **naturally handled** — this is a 1-mark KTU favourite.
> 4. **Matrix dimension mismatch:** Verify dimensions: $A$ must be $n \times n$, $B$ is $n \times r$, $C$ is $m \times n$, $D$ is $m \times r$. A dimension error invalidates the entire model.
> 5. **D matrix confusion:** For systems with no direct input-to-output coupling (most physical systems), $D = 0$. Don't leave it blank — **state it explicitly**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 Introduction**

- ☐ State space model consists of **two equations**: state equation $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$ and output equation $y = Cx + Du$.
- ☐ Number of state variables $n$ = order of the system = order of the highest derivative in the ODE.
- ☐ **Phase variable form**: $x_1 = y$, $x_2 = \dot{y}$, $x_3 = \ddot{y}$, ...; bottom row of $A$ has $-a_0, -a_1, \ldots, -a_{n-1}$.
- ☐ **Dimensions**: $A (n \times n)$, $B (n \times r)$, $C (m \times n)$, $D (m \times r)$.
- ☐ **Transfer function recovery**: $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D$.
- ☐ **Characteristic equation**: $\det(sI - A) = 0$ gives eigenvalues (system poles).
- ☐ **Non-uniqueness**: Infinitely many valid state space realizations exist; linked by similarity transform $\bar{x} = Tx$.
- ☐ **State space advantages over classical**: handles MIMO, time-varying, nonlinear systems; incorporates initial conditions naturally; supports optimal control design.
- ☐ **Stability quick check**: If all eigenvalues of $A$ have negative real parts → system is asymptotically stable.
- ☐ **KTU favourite questions**: (1) Convert ODE to state space, (2) Find transfer function from state space, (3) Find characteristic equation, (4) Discuss non-uniqueness / canonical forms.
- ☐ **Discrete-time vs continuous-time**: continuous uses $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$; discrete uses $x(k+1) = Gx(k) + Hu(k)$.
- ☐ Always **verify** your state space model by recovering the transfer function — it is the safest way to catch errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of Nonlinear Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT636 - ADVANCED CONTROL THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of Nonlinear Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **nonlinear system** is a dynamical system whose behavior is governed by a set of nonlinear equations, meaning the output is not directly proportional to the input, and the system does not satisfy the principles of **superposition** (homogeneity) and **additivity**.

Mathematically, a system described by the differential equation

$$\dot{x}(t) = f(x(t), u(t), t)$$

is **nonlinear** if the function $f(\cdot)$ is not a linear function of the state $x(t)$ and the input $u(t)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Definition (Board Standard):** A system is said to be nonlinear if it cannot be described by a linear differential equation with constant coefficients, or if the system parameters (gain, time constant, etc.) depend on the signal amplitude or operating point.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are driving a car. When you gently press the accelerator pedal, the car moves slowly. As you press harder, the speed increases *roughly* proportionally. But if you floor the accelerator, the engine reaches its mechanical limit, and the speed stops increasing — the **response saturates**. If you suddenly change gears, there is a small **dead zone** before the new gear engages. This is the essence of a nonlinear system:

- The output is **not a straight line** when plotted against the input.
- The behavior **changes** depending on the magnitude of the signal.
- There can be **memory effects** (hysteresis), **time delays** (backlash), and **multiple operating states** (multi-equilibrium).

> [!NOTE]  
> **Core Distinction:** Linear systems have a *predictable, uniform* response. Nonlinear systems behave *differently at different operating points*, making them richer (and harder to analyze) but also enabling real-world phenomena like **oscillations, chaos, and limit cycles** that linear systems cannot produce.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **No universal constants** apply globally; however, key performance metrics include:
  - **Limit Cycle Amplitude** — measured in system output units (e.g., Volts, rad/s, m).
  - **Dead Zone Width** — threshold value below which no output is produced.
  - **Saturation Level** — maximum output magnitude of the actuator or plant.
  - **Hysteresis Loop Area** — energy dissipated per cycle in Joules (J).

> [!TIP]  
> **For Examiners:** Always state explicitly *which type* of nonlinearity you are referring to (static vs. dynamic, intentional vs. unintentional, hard vs. soft). KTU examiners award marks for **categorization** first, analysis second.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Properties of Nonlinear Systems

### A. Violation of the Superposition Principle
For a linear system, if $y_1(t)$ is the response to $u_1(t)$ and $y_2(t)$ is the response to $u_2(t)$, then the response to $u_1(t) + u_2(t)$ is exactly $y_1(t) + y_2(t)$. **This fails in nonlinear systems.**

### B. Multiple Equilibrium Points
A nonlinear system can have **more than one steady-state operating point** for the same input. Each equilibrium is locally meaningful but globally the system may jump between them.

### C. Limit Cycles
A **limit cycle** is a closed, isolated trajectory in the phase plane representing a self-sustained oscillation. Its **amplitude and frequency are independent of initial conditions** (unlike linear oscillations, which decay).

### D. Jump Resonance and Sub-Harmonic Oscillations
At certain input frequencies, the output amplitude can **suddenly jump** between two stable values as frequency is varied. Sub-harmonic oscillations occur at rational fractions (1/2, 1/3, …) of the driving frequency.

### E. Chaos and Sensitivity to Initial Conditions
Tiny changes in initial conditions lead to exponentially diverging trajectories — the **butterfly effect**. Long-term prediction becomes impossible.

### F. Signal-Dependent System Behavior
The effective gain, time constant, and damping depend on the **signal amplitude**. A small sine wave and a large sine wave produce qualitatively different responses.

## 2.2 Common Types of Nonlinearities (Static Nonlinearities)

| # | Nonlinearity | Physical Example | Mathematical Form $y = f(u)$ |
|---|--------------|------------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | **Saturation** | Op-amp, valve opening | $y = \text{sat}(u) = \begin{cases} K u, & \vert u \vert \le a \\ K a, & u > a \\ -K a, & u < -a \end{cases}$ |
| 2 | **Dead Zone** | Diode, DC motor with friction | $y = \begin{cases} 0, & \vert u \vert \le d \\ K(u-d), & u > d \\ K(u+d), & u < -d \end{cases}$ |
| 3 | **Backlash** | Gear train, linkage | Multi-valued — output equals last input shifted by $\pm b/2$ |
| 4 | **Hysteresis** | Magnetic core, relay | Loop area: $\oint y\, du \ne 0$ |
| 5 | **Relay (On-Off)** | Thermostat, bang-bang controller | $y = M \,\text{sgn}(u)$ |
| 6 | **Coulomb Friction** | Sliding mass | $y = K u + F_c \,\text{sgn}(\dot{x})$ |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---------|----------------------|-------------|
| **General Nonlinear ODE** | $a_n \dd{x}{n} + \cdots + a_1 \dot{x} + f(x, \dot{x}) = g(t)$ | Nonlinear in $x$ or its derivatives |
| **Describing Function** (preliminary) | $N(A) = \dfrac{Y_1}{A} e^{j \phi_1}$ | Ratio of fundamental output to sinusoidal input amplitude |
| **Saturation Output** | $y_{\max} = K \cdot a$ | $a$ = linear range, $K$ = slope |
| **Dead Zone Threshold** | $u_{th} = d$ | No output for $\vert u \vert \le d$ |
| **Limit Cycle Condition** | $1 + N(A) G(j\omega) = 0$ | Solving yields $(A, \omega)$ of oscillation |
| **Superposition Test** | $f(\alpha u_1 + \beta u_2) \stackrel{?}{=} \alpha f(u_1) + \beta f(u_2)$ | Fails for nonlinear $f$ |
| **Energy of Hysteresis Loop** | $E = \oint y\, du$ | Energy dissipated per cycle |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Mandatory Coverage:** Module 3 specifically demands study of *static nonlinearities* (saturation, dead zone, backlash, relay, hysteresis), the *superposition principle failure*, and the introduction to **describing functions** and **phase plane analysis**. Memorize the table above — it covers ~70% of theory questions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Nonlinear control theory is essential in:
- **Robotics** (Coulomb friction, gear backlash compensation)
- **Power Electronics** (saturation in PWM inverters, relay-mode converters)
- **Aerospace** (aerodynamic nonlinearities, rate saturation in actuators)
- **Process Control** (valve stiction, hysteresis in temperature sensors)
- **Biomedical Systems** (neural firing, cardiac oscillators)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Superposition Failure — Saturation

**Problem:** Consider a saturation nonlinearity with slope $K = 2$ and linear range $a = 3$. Input $u(t) = 2 \sin(\omega t)$. Verify that superposition does **not** hold.

### Step 1: Response to individual inputs

Let $u_1(t) = \sin(\omega t)$ and $u_2(t) = \sin(\omega t)$ (same as $u_1$).

For $u_1(t)$: peak = $1 < a = 3$, so

$$y_1(t) = K u_1(t) = 2 \sin(\omega t)$$

### Step 2: Response to combined input

For $u(t) = u_1(t) + u_2(t) = 2 \sin(\omega t)$: peak = $2 < 3$, still linear,

$$y(t) = 2 \cdot (2 \sin(\omega t)) = 4 \sin(\omega t)$$

### Step 3: Now test with $u_1 = 2\sin(\omega t)$ and $u_2 = 3\sin(\omega t)$

$u_1$ alone: peak = $2 < 3 \Rightarrow y_1 = 2 \cdot 2 \sin(\omega t) = 4 \sin(\omega t)$.

$u_2$ alone: peak = $3 = a \Rightarrow y_2 = 2 \cdot 3 = 6$ (clipped, constant peak).

But $u_1 + u_2 = 5 \sin(\omega t)$: peak = $5 > 3$ ⇒ **clipped** to $y = 2 \cdot 3 = 6$ at the peaks.

So $y(u_1+u_2) = 6$ at peaks, while $y(u_1) + y(u_2) = 4\sin + 6 \ne 6$. **Superposition fails.** $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Multiple Equilibrium Points — Second-Order System

**Problem:** Find the equilibrium points of the nonlinear autonomous system

$$\ddot{x} + \dot{x} + x - x^3 = 0$$

### Step 1: At equilibrium, $\dot{x} = 0$ and $\ddot{x} = 0$

$$x_{eq} - x_{eq}^3 = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x_{eq}(1 - x_{eq}^2) = 0$$

### Step 2: Solve the cubic

$$\begin{aligned}
x_{eq} &= 0 \\
x_{eq}^2 &= 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x_{eq} = +1 \text{ or } x_{eq} = -1
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Result

The system has **three equilibrium points**: $x_{eq} \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$.

This is a textbook example of a **double-well potential** system (Duffing-like oscillator) with **multiple equilibria** — a feature impossible in linear second-order systems, which have exactly one equilibrium for any constant input.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Describing Function Preview — Saturation

**Problem:** Derive the describing function of a saturation nonlinearity with slope $K$ and linear range $\pm a$.

### Step 1: Input the sinusoid

$$u(t) = A \sin(\omega t), \quad A > a$$

### Step 2: Identify the clipping angle

The output is linear until $u = a$, i.e., $\sin(\omega t_1) = a/A$.

$$\omega t_1 = \arcsin(a/A) = \psi$$

### Step 3: Compute the fundamental Fourier coefficient $B_1$

The saturation output is an **odd function**, so the cosine term $A_1 = 0$. The sine coefficient:

$$\begin{aligned}
B_1 &= \dfrac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} y(\omega t) \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) \\
&= \dfrac{4}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\psi} K a \sin\theta\, d\theta + \dfrac{4}{\pi} \int_{\psi}^{\pi} K A \sin^2\theta\, d\theta \\
&= \dfrac{4 K a}{\pi}\left[-\cos\theta\right]_{0}^{\psi} + \dfrac{4 K A}{\pi} \int_{\psi}^{\pi} \dfrac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}\, d\theta \\
&= \dfrac{4 K a}{\pi}(1 - \cos\psi) + \dfrac{2 K A}{\pi} \left[\theta - \dfrac{\sin(2\theta)}{2}\right]_{\psi}^{\pi}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Substitute $\cos\psi = \sqrt{1 - (a/A)^2}$

After simplification (a standard KTU derivation):

$$B_1 = \dfrac{2K A}{\pi} \left[\arcsin\left(\dfrac{a}{A}\right) + \dfrac{a}{A}\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{a}{A}\right)^2}\right]$$

### Step 5: Describing function

$$\boxed{\,N(A) = \dfrac{2K}{\pi} \left[\arcsin\left(\dfrac{a}{A}\right) + \dfrac{a}{A}\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{a}{A}\right)^2}\right]\,}$$

**Properties to remember:**
- $N(A) \to K$ as $A \to \infty$ (the large-signal effective gain drops to the average slope, not $K$).
- $N(A) \to K$ as $A \to a^+$ (continuous at the edge of linear range).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Behavior Comparison

| Property | Linear System | Nonlinear System |
|----------|---------------|------------------|
| Superposition | Holds | **Fails** |
| Number of equilibria | 1 (for given input) | **Multiple possible** |
| Oscillation | Decays or grows (unstable) | Can sustain **limit cycles** |
| Response to sinusoid | Same frequency, scaled & phase-shifted | **Contains harmonics** |
| Stability | Global (one definition) | **Local / multiple** (Lyapunov) |
| BIBO Stability | Equivalent to asymptotic | **Different** notions |
| Analysis Tools | Laplace, Fourier, Eigenvalues | **Phase plane, Describing Functions, Lyapunov, Popov** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification of System Nonlinearities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Nonlinear Systems]:::root --> B[Static Nonlinearities]
    A --> C[Dynamic Nonlinearities]
    A --> D[Intentional Nonlinearities]
    A --> E[Unintentional Nonlinearities]

    B --> B1[Saturation]
    B --> B2[Dead Zone]
    B --> B3[Backlash]
    B --> B4[Relay On-Off]
    B --> B5[Hysteresis]

    C --> C1[Limit Cycles]
    C --> C2[Subharmonic Oscillations]
    C --> C3[Chaotic Behavior]
    C --> C4[Jump Resonance]

    D --> D1[Bang-Bang Controllers]
    D --> D2[Variable Structure Systems]
    D --> D3[Adaptive Gain Scheduling]

    E --> E1[Coulomb Friction]
    E --> E2[Actuator Saturation]
    E --> E3[Gear Backlash]
    E --> E4[Sensor Hysteresis]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef nonlinear fill:#dbe5f1,color:#000000,stroke:#1f4e79
    classDef intentional fill:#fff2cc,color:#000000,stroke:#bf9000
    classDef unintent fill:#fce4d6,color:#000000,stroke:#c00000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Input-Output Characteristics of Static Nonlinearities

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sat[Saturation]
        S1[Input u] --> S2[Linear region vert u vert less than a] --> S3[Output y = K u]
        S1 --> S4[Clipped region vert u vert greater than a] --> S5[Output y = K a constant]
    end

    subgraph DZ[Dead Zone]
        D1[Input u] --> D2[Region vert u vert less than d] --> D3[Output y = 0]
        D1 --> D4[Region vert u vert greater than d] --> D5[Output y = K u minus d]
    end

    subgraph BL[Backlash]
        B1[Input u moving up] --> B2[Output follows with shift plus b by 2]
        B1 --> B3[Input u moving down] --> B4[Output follows with shift minus b by 2]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Block Diagram of a Nonlinear Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference Input R s] --> Sum[Summing Junction]
    Dist[Disturbance D s] --> Sum
    Sum -->|Error E s| NL[Nonlinear Element N times]
    NL -->|u s| Plant[Linear Plant G s]
    Plant -->|Y s| Out[System Output]
    Out -->|Feedback| H[Sensor H s]
    H --> Sum
```

## 4.4 Mermaid — Phase Plane Trajectory (Limit Cycle Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Initial condition 1] --> A1[Spiral inward if stable focus]
    A0 --> A2[Spiral outward if unstable focus]
    A0 --> A3[Approach closed curve = Limit Cycle]
    A3 --> A4[Closed isolated trajectory in phase plane]
    A4 --> A5[Amplitude and frequency independent of initial conditions]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any three properties that distinguish a nonlinear system from a linear system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Superposition principle fails** — the response to a linear combination of inputs is not equal to the linear combination of individual responses.
2. **Multiple equilibrium points** may exist for the same input, whereas a linear system has exactly one equilibrium per input.
3. **Limit cycles and self-sustained oscillations** of constant amplitude and frequency are possible, which cannot occur in linear systems (where oscillations either decay or grow unbounded).
*(1 mark each — full credit for naming and one-line justification.)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Define "dead zone" nonlinearity. Give one practical example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **dead zone** is a static nonlinearity in which the output is zero for input magnitudes within a threshold $\pm d$ (the dead band), and proportional to the input outside this band. (2 marks)

**Mathematical form:**

$$y = \begin{cases} 0, & \vert u \vert \le d \\ K(u - d\,\text{sgn}(u)), & \vert u \vert > d \end{cases}$$

**Example:** A DC servo motor requires a minimum armature voltage to overcome static (Coulomb) friction before the rotor begins to rotate. (1 mark)

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**

**(a)** With neat input-output characteristics, explain **saturation**, **dead zone**, and **backlash** nonlinearities. Mention one real-world example of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a second-order nonlinear system described by

$$\ddot{x} + 0.5\,\dot{x} + x - 0.25\,x^3 = 0$$

find the equilibrium points and comment on the type of stability at each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Saturation, Dead Zone, Backlash (7 marks)**

**1. Saturation (2.5 marks):**
- **Characteristic:** Output increases linearly with input up to a threshold $\pm a$; beyond this, the output remains constant at $\pm K a$.
- **I/O Relation:**

$$y = \begin{cases} K a, & u > a \\ K u, & \vert u \vert \le a \\ -K a, & u < -a \end{cases}$$

- **Example:** Operational amplifier output limited by supply rail voltage.
- **Effect:** Loss of gain at high inputs; causes integrator wind-up in PI controllers.

**2. Dead Zone (2.5 marks):**
- **Characteristic:** Output is zero when $\vert u \vert \le d$ (the dead band). Beyond $d$, output is proportional to $(u - d\,\text{sgn}(u))$.
- **I/O Relation:** (as given in Q2 above)
- **Example:** Diode forward conduction (requires $\sim$0.7 V threshold).
- **Effect:** Introduces a small steady-state error and reduces effective system sensitivity.

**3. Backlash (2 marks):**
- **Characteristic:** Multi-valued nonlinearity where the output equals the input minus a constant shift, but the sign of the shift depends on whether the input is **increasing** or **decreasing**. The width of the multi-valued region is $b$.
- **Example:** Gear train in mechanical transmission — the driven gear does not engage until the driving gear has moved through the clearance $b$.
- **Effect:** Causes **limit cycles** and **hunting** in position control systems.

**(b) Equilibrium points and stability (7 marks)**

Set $\dot{x} = 0$ and $\ddot{x} = 0$:

$$x_{eq} - 0.25 x_{eq}^3 = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x_{eq}(1 - 0.25 x_{eq}^2) = 0$$

$$\begin{aligned}
x_{eq,1} &= 0 \\
x_{eq,2,3} &= \pm 2
\end{aligned}$$

**Stability via linearization** (compute Jacobian of $f(x,\dot{x})$ near each equilibrium):

Let $x_1 = x$, $x_2 = \dot{x}$. Then

$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2,\quad \dot{x}_2 = -0.5 x_2 - x_1 + 0.25 x_1^3$$

Jacobian:

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 + 0.75 x_1^2 & -0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**At $x_{eq} = 0$:**

$$J(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & -0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

Eigenvalues: $\lambda^2 + 0.5\lambda + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = -0.25 \pm j\,0.968$.

**Stable focus** (damped oscillation). (2.5 marks)

**At $x_{eq} = +2$:**

$$J(2) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 + 0.75(4) & -0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 2 & -0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

Eigenvalues: $\lambda^2 + 0.5\lambda - 2 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1.19,\; -1.69$.

**Saddle point — unstable.** (2 marks)

**At $x_{eq} = -2$:** By symmetry, also a **saddle point — unstable.** (0.5 mark)

**Conclusion:** The system has one stable and two unstable equilibria — a hallmark of a **double-well Duffing-type nonlinear system** that can exhibit **jump phenomena** and **hysteresis** between equilibria. (1 mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** State and explain the **principle of superposition**. Show with a numerical example that a saturation nonlinearity does not satisfy it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Sketch the describing function $N(A)$ versus input amplitude $A$ for a saturation nonlinearity. Discuss the **limit cycle condition** $1 + N(A)G(j\omega) = 0$ qualitatively. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Superposition principle and its failure (7 marks)**

**Statement (2 marks):**
A system satisfies the principle of superposition if, for any two inputs $u_1(t)$ and $u_2(t)$ with corresponding outputs $y_1(t)$ and $y_2(t)$, and for any scalars $\alpha, \beta$, the response to $\alpha u_1 + \beta u_2$ equals $\alpha y_1 + \beta y_2$.

**Numerical example (5 marks):**

Consider saturation with $K = 1$, $a = 2$. Let

$$u_1(t) = 3 \sin(\omega t), \quad u_2(t) = 1.5 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Response to $u_1$ alone:** Peak $3 > 2$, so output is clipped to $\pm 2$ at the peaks. Effective output is a **truncated sine** with peak $2$.

$$y_1(t) = \begin{cases} 2, & \sin(\omega t) > 2/3 \\ 3\sin(\omega t), & \vert \sin(\omega t) \vert \le 2/3 \\ -2, & \sin(\omega t) < -2/3 \end{cases}$$

**Response to $u_2$ alone:** Peak $1.5 < 2$, so linear.

$$y_2(t) = 1.5 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Response to $u_1 + u_2 = 4.5 \sin(\omega t)$:** Peak $4.5 > 2$, so heavily clipped to $\pm 2$ for most of the cycle.

$$y_{12}(t) = \text{sat}(4.5 \sin(\omega t)) \approx \text{near-square wave of peak } 2$$

**Check superposition:**

$$y_1(t) + y_2(t) = 1.5 \sin(\omega t) + \text{clipped}(3\sin) \ne y_{12}(t)$$

The output $y_{12}$ has a **larger harmonic content** (it is nearly a square wave) than $y_1 + y_2$, which retains significant sine character. Hence **superposition fails**. $\blacksquare$

**(b) Describing function of saturation (7 marks)**

**Sketch (3 marks):**

The describing function $N(A)$ for saturation (derived in Section 3.3 above) has the following behavior:

- For $A \le a$: $N(A) = K$ (constant, linear region).
- For $A > a$: $N(A)$ **decreases monotonically** from $K$ to a limiting value of $N(\infty) = \dfrac{2K a}{\pi A} \cdot A = \dfrac{2K a}{\pi} \cdot 0 + \cdots$ → effectively $\to 0$ in the average slope sense; the curve is **concave-down, monotonically decreasing**.

```
N(A)
K |****
  |    ***
  |       ***
  |          ****
  |              ************
  |______________________________ A
  0    a
```

**Limit cycle condition (4 marks):**

The describing function method predicts a **limit cycle** when the linear part's Nyquist plot $G(j\omega)$ intersects the negative reciprocal $\dfrac{-1}{N(A)}$ locus in the complex plane.

**For saturation:** Since $N(A)$ is real and positive (saturation is a single-valued odd nonlinearity), $\dfrac{-1}{N(A)}$ lies on the **negative real axis** of the $G$-plane, moving from $-1/K$ (at $A = a$) toward $-\infty$ (as $A \to \infty$).

A limit cycle exists if the Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$ crosses the negative real axis **to the left of $-1/K$** (between $-1/K$ and $-\infty$) at some frequency $\omega_c$. The intersection gives $(A_c, \omega_c)$.

If $G(j\omega)$ enters the third/fourth quadrant crossing the real axis to the **right of $-1/K$**, no limit cycle occurs — the system remains stable under saturation.

This is the basis of the **extended Nyquist / Kochenburger criterion** for analyzing nonlinear stability — a core Module 3 topic in KTU 2024.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing saturation with dead zone** in I/O diagrams — remember saturation has a flat top, dead zone has a flat middle.
> 2. **Forgetting the describing function phase** — for **single-valued odd** nonlinearities (saturation, dead zone, ideal relay without hysteresis), $N(A)$ is **purely real** (no imaginary part). The phase $\phi_1 = 0$. Students who write $N(A) = K e^{-j\phi}$ unnecessarily lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Superposition counter-examples** must use **unequal input amplitudes** so that one is in the linear region and one is clipped — using equal amplitudes does not demonstrate failure.
> 4. **Stability classification** — students often say "unstable" without specifying whether it is a **saddle**, **unstable node**, or **unstable focus**. Always compute eigenvalues.
> 5. **Hysteresis vs. backlash** — backlash is a special case of hysteresis in position; hysteresis is a more general energy-loss phenomenon. Do not use them interchangeably in answers.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **nonlinear system** is one that does not obey the **superposition principle** and is described by a nonlinear ODE.
- ✅ **Static nonlinearities** (memoryless): **Saturation, Dead Zone, Backlash, Hysteresis, Relay, Coulomb Friction**.
- ✅ **Dynamic nonlinear behaviors** (memory-dependent): **Limit Cycles, Sub-Harmonic Oscillations, Jump Resonance, Chaos**.
- ✅ A nonlinear system can have **multiple equilibrium points**; classify each by **local linearization (Jacobian + eigenvalues)**.
- ✅ **Limit cycle** = closed isolated phase-plane trajectory, amplitude/frequency independent of initial conditions.
- ✅ **Describing function** $N(A) = \dfrac{Y_1}{A} e^{j\phi_1}$ — first-harmonic approximation for sinusoidal inputs.
- ✅ For **single-valued odd** nonlinearities (saturation, dead zone, ideal relay), $N(A)$ is **real and positive**.
- ✅ **Limit cycle condition:** $1 + N(A)G(j\omega) = 0$ (extended Nyquist criterion).
- ✅ **Real-world examples** to cite: op-amp saturation, diode threshold, gear backlash, thermostat relay, magnetic hysteresis, valve stiction, Coulomb friction in slides.
- ✅ **Key formulas to memorize:**
  - Saturation output: $y_{\max} = K a$
  - Dead zone threshold: $\vert u \vert \le d \Rightarrow y = 0$
  - Backlash width: $b$ (clearance)
  - Describing function of saturation: $N(A) = \dfrac{2K}{\pi}\left[\arcsin(a/A) + \dfrac{a}{A}\sqrt{1-(a/A)^2}\right]$
  - Duffing equilibria: solve $x - 0.25 x^3 = 0$
- ✅ **Examination tip:** Always draw the I/O characteristic clearly; examiners award 1–2 marks purely for a correct, well-labeled sketch.
- ✅ **Stability** in nonlinear systems is a **local property**; always specify the equilibrium and the neighborhood.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Nonlinearities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT636 - ADVANCED CONTROL THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Nonlinearities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Nonlinearity?

In **linear control theory**, we strictly assume that the system obeys the **principle of superposition** (homogeneity + additivity). A system is said to be **nonlinear** when its behavior cannot be described by a single linear differential equation with constant coefficients — i.e., the output is not a linear function of the input, or its parameters depend on the magnitude/sign of the signals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **nonlinear system** is one whose mathematical model contains at least one nonlinear operator. This may appear as a nonlinear differential equation (e.g., $\ddot{x} + x\dot{x} = 0$) or as a nonlinear static element (memoryless block) sandwiched between otherwise linear subsystems.

**Mathematical characterization:**

$$y(t) = \mathcal{N}[u(t)] \quad \text{where} \quad \mathcal{N}[\alpha u_1 + \beta u_2] \neq \alpha \mathcal{N}[u_1] + \beta \mathcal{N}[u_2]$$

for at least one pair $(u_1, u_2)$ and scalars $\alpha, \beta$.

---

## 1.2 Two Broad Categories of Nonlinearities

| # | Category | Description | Memory? | Common Examples |
|---|----------|-------------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Intentional (Essential) Nonlinearities** | Deliberately inserted to achieve a specific control action or to limit signals. | Usually memoryless | Relay, saturation, dead-zone, hysteresis |
| 2 | **Unintentional (Incidental) Nonlinearities** | Inherent physical imperfections in the plant or actuator. | Can be with or without memory | Friction, backlash, valve characteristics, hysteresis in magnetic materials |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Broken Volume Knob" 🎚️

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine the volume knob of a cheap radio. Turning it a little does **nothing** (output stays at zero) — this is the **dead zone**. As you turn it more, the volume starts rising but eventually **stops getting louder** even though you keep turning — this is **saturation**. Now imagine turning it back: the volume doesn't drop immediately but **lags behind** — this is **hysteresis/backlash**. The radio engineer treats these as *nonlinearities* that the linear equation $y = Kx$ cannot capture.

In short: **anything that bends, breaks, or shifts the straight input–output line is a nonlinearity.**

---

## 1.4 Why KTU 2024 Module-3 Demands This Topic

A first course in control (KTU EST200) teaches only the **linear world** (transfer functions, Bode, Nyquist, Routh). **Advanced Control Theory** explicitly *abandons* linearity to handle:

- Actuator saturation (every amplifier, motor, op-amp saturates).
- Switching controllers (ON/OFF thermostats, relay-based systems).
- Mechanical imperfections (gears with backlash).
- Describing-function analysis (covered in the *next* KTU sub-topic, built upon these building blocks).

Mastery of these elementary **static (memoryless) nonlinearities** is therefore a **prerequisite** for the **Describing Function Method** and **Phase Plane Method** that follow in Module 3.

---

## 1.5 The Seven Standard KTU Nonlinearities — Quick Identification Chart

| KTU Tag | Nonlinearity | Physical Origin |
|---------|--------------|-----------------|
| N1 | **Dead Zone (Dead Band)** | Threshold below which the device does not respond (e.g., small DC motor stiction). |
| N2 | **Saturation** | Output cannot exceed a physical limit (e.g., valve fully open, amplifier clipping). |
| N3 | **Backlash (Play)** | Loose gear meshing; input reverses before output catches up. |
| N4 | **Ideal Relay (Two-Position / ON–OFF)** | Thermostat; bang-bang controller. |
| N5 | **Relay with Dead Zone (Three-Position)** | Center-off switch. |
| N6 | **Relay with Hysteresis** | Schmitt trigger. |
| N7 | **Coulomb Friction + Viscous Friction** | Dry/sliding friction. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus officially enumerates at least **Dead zone, Saturation, Backlash, Relay, and Friction** under "Types of Nonlinearities." We will treat these in **Section 2** with full mathematical models, and the remaining ones as supplementary coverage.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

We model each nonlinearity as a **memoryless static element** placed inside an otherwise linear feedback loop. The output $y(t)$ depends only on the **instantaneous value of input** $x(t)$.

$$y(t) = f\big(x(t)\big)$$

with no time derivative and no delay.

---

## 2.1 Dead Zone (Dead Band) — Tag **N1**

### Description
The output is exactly **zero** as long as the input lies in a small band around zero. Once $|x| > \Delta$, the output follows a linear slope $K$.

### Working Logic
1. If the input magnitude is below the threshold $\Delta$, the device refuses to move.
2. Past the threshold, the relationship becomes linear.
3. The resulting input–output graph is a **flat segment centered at the origin** with linear arms outside.

### Physical Intuition
A small DC motor needs a minimum voltage to overcome stiction and start rotating. A hydraulic valve needs a minimum pressure to open the spool. Below these minimums, **nothing happens**.

### Mathematical Form

$$y = f(x) = \begin{cases} K(x + \Delta), & x < -\Delta \\ 0, & \vert x \vert \leq \Delta \\ K(x - \Delta), & x > \Delta \end{cases}$$

where:
- $\Delta$ = width of the dead band (same units as $x$).
- $K$ = slope (gain) outside the dead zone.

---

## 2.2 Saturation — Tag **N2**

### Description
A linear element whose output **cannot exceed** a fixed upper or lower bound, no matter how large the input becomes.

### Working Logic
1. For small inputs, the element behaves linearly with slope $K$.
2. Once $|x| \geq x_s$, the output is *clamped* at the saturation level $\pm y_s$.
3. The I/O plot is a **straight line flattened at the extremes**.

### Physical Intuition
Every physical actuator has limits: a control valve cannot pass infinite flow, an op-amp cannot output more than its supply rail, a motor has a maximum safe torque.

### Mathematical Form

$$y = f(x) = \begin{cases} -y_s, & x \leq -x_s \\ Kx, & \vert x \vert < x_s \\ +y_s, & x \geq +x_s \end{cases}$$

with the constraint $y_s = K x_s$.

---

## 2.3 Backlash — Tag **N3**

### Description
A **multi-valued, memory-dependent** nonlinearity arising from mechanical play between gear teeth. Output change lags the input during reversal.

### Working Logic
1. As $x$ increases, the output $y$ follows the line $y = K(x - b/2)$ once $x$ crosses the lower limit.
2. As $x$ decreases, the output follows the line $y = K(x + b/2)$ once $x$ falls below the upper limit.
3. Between these limits, the output remains **constant** — the gear teeth are simply *disengaging*.

### Physical Intuition
Two meshed gears that are not perfectly tight; when the driver reverses, the driven gear doesn't move until the play (backlash $b$) is taken up.

### Mathematical Form

The instantaneous relationship is:

$$y = \begin{cases} K\!\left(x - \tfrac{b}{2}\right) & \text{on the rising edge} \\[4pt] K\!\left(x + \tfrac{b}{2}\right) & \text{on the falling edge} \\[4pt] y_{\text{last}} & \text{during reversal within } \pm b/2 \end{cases}$$

where $b$ is the **backlash width**.

---

## 2.4 Ideal (Two-Position) Relay — Tag **N4**

### Description
A perfect ON/OFF switch. The output is **always either $+M$ or $-M$**, switching exactly when the input changes sign.

### Mathematical Form

$$y = f(x) = \begin{cases} +M, & x > 0 \\ 0, & x = 0 \\ -M, & x < 0 \end{cases}$$

### Physical Intuition
A household thermostat. Above setpoint → heater OFF. Below setpoint → heater ON. (Note: this is the *bang-bang* controller used in missile guidance and simple refrigerators.)

---

## 2.5 Three-Position Relay with Dead Zone — Tag **N5**

$$y = f(x) = \begin{cases} +M, & x > \Delta \\ 0, & \vert x \vert \leq \Delta \\ -M, & x < -\Delta \end{cases}$$

### Intuition
A center-stable relay. The "OFF" position is maintained over a small band, preventing rapid chattering around the setpoint.

---

## 2.6 Relay with Hysteresis (Schmitt Trigger) — Tag **N6**

$$y = f(x) = \begin{cases} +M, & x > a \quad \text{while rising} \\ -M, & x < -a \quad \text{while falling} \\ y_{\text{previous}}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $a$ is the **half-width of the hysteresis loop**.

### Intuition
A comparator with intentional positive feedback. The output **does not switch back immediately**; it must be driven further negative by an amount $a$. Prevents relay chatter.

---

## 2.7 Coulomb Friction + Viscous Friction — Tag **N7**

The classic mechanical nonlinear friction model:

$$F_{\text{friction}} = F_c \, \mathrm{sgn}(\dot{x}) + B \dot{x}$$

where:
- $F_c$ = **Coulomb friction constant** (force needed to *initiate* motion).
- $B$ = **viscous friction coefficient**.
- $\mathrm{sgn}(\dot{x})$ = signum of velocity.

> [!NOTE]
> Pure Coulomb friction creates a discontinuity at $\dot{x} = 0$, leading to **limit cycles** and **stick-slip** behavior in servo systems — a favorite KTU exam topic.

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Nonlinearity | Input → Output Relationship | Key Parameter | Stability Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|
