| 1 | Dead Zone | $y = K(x \mp \Delta)$ for $\vert x \vert > \Delta$, else $0$ | $\Delta$ (threshold) | Reduces small-signal gain; can cause steady-state error. |
| 2 | Saturation | $y = \mathrm{sat}(Kx, \pm y_s)$ | $y_s, x_s$ | Causes integrator wind-up, performance degradation. |
| 3 | Backlash | $y$ follows $K(x \pm b/2)$ outside dead band | $b$ (backlash) | Generates limit cycles; phase lag. |
| 4 | Ideal Relay | $y = M\,\mathrm{sgn}(x)$ | $M$ (output magnitude) | Inherent oscillation. |
| 5 | 3-Pos Relay | $y = 0$ for $\vert x \vert \leq \Delta$, else $\pm M$ | $\Delta, M$ | Reduces chattering. |
| 6 | Hysteresis Relay | Output lags input by $2a$ | $a$ (half-width) | Self-sustained oscillation. |
| 7 | Coulomb + Viscous Friction | $F = F_c\,\mathrm{sgn}(\dot{x}) + B\dot{x}$ | $F_c, B$ | Causes stick-slip, limit cycles. |

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Dominant Nonlinearity | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Hydraulic servo valves | Saturation + dead zone | Limits actuator speed; creates threshold error. |
| Robotic gear trains | Backlash | Causes positioning error & oscillation in CNC machines. |
| Automotive cruise control | Relay (bang-bang) | Cheap implementation, but causes speed oscillation. |
| Spacecraft thrusters | Dead zone + saturation | Minimum pulse width; max thrust. |
| Disk-drive head positioning | Friction + backlash | Stick-slip at low velocity; tight servo design needed. |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section works through the **mathematical derivations and analytical properties** that examiners most frequently test in the 14-mark questions.

---

## 3.1 Derivation — Describing-Function Equivalent Gain of Dead Zone

The **Describing Function (DF)** is the single most important tool in KTU Module 3 for analysing memoryless nonlinearities under sinusoidal input. We now derive it for the dead zone — the rest follow analogously.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Assume the input is a sinusoid:
$$x(t) = X \sin(\omega t), \qquad X > \Delta$$

The dead-zone output is:
$$y(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & 0 \leq \omega t \leq \alpha \\ K(X\sin\omega t - \Delta), & \alpha \leq \omega t \leq \pi - \alpha \\ 0, & \pi - \alpha \leq \omega t \leq \pi + \alpha \\ K(X\sin\omega t + \Delta), & \pi + \alpha \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi - \alpha \\ 0, & 2\pi - \alpha \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi \end{cases}$$

where the **break-angle** $\alpha$ satisfies:

$$\sin\alpha = \frac{\Delta}{X}, \quad \text{so} \quad \alpha = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\Delta}{X}\right)$$

### 3.1.2 The Describing Function Definition

By Fourier series, the fundamental component of $y(t)$ has amplitude:

$$Y_1 = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} y(t)\,\sin(\omega t)\,d(\omega t)$$

The describing function is the (complex) ratio of fundamental output to input:

$$N(X) = \frac{Y_1}{X}$$

Because the dead-zone element is **odd-symmetric**, no cosine term arises, and $N(X)$ is purely real.

### 3.1.3 Computing $Y_1$

By symmetry, only one quarter-period needs integration, then multiply by 4:

$$Y_1 = \frac{4}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} K\!\left(X\sin\theta - \Delta\right)\sin\theta\, d\theta$$

$$\implies Y_1 = \frac{4K}{\pi}\left[X\int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2}\sin^{2}\theta\,d\theta - \Delta\int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2}\sin\theta\,d\theta\right]$$

### 3.1.4 Evaluate the Integrals

Use the standard integrals:

$$\int\sin^{2}\theta\,d\theta = \frac{\theta}{2} - \frac{\sin 2\theta}{4}$$

$$\int\sin\theta\,d\theta = -\cos\theta$$

Applying the limits $\alpha$ to $\pi/2$:

$$\int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2}\sin^{2}\theta\,d\theta = \left[\frac{\theta}{2} - \frac{\sin 2\theta}{4}\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} = \frac{\pi}{4} - \frac{\alpha}{2} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{4}$$

$$\int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2}\sin\theta\,d\theta = \left[-\cos\theta\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} = \cos\alpha$$

### 3.1.5 Substitute Back

$$Y_1 = \frac{4K}{\pi}\left[X\left(\frac{\pi}{4} - \frac{\alpha}{2} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{4}\right) - \Delta\cos\alpha\right]$$

$$Y_1 = \frac{4K}{\pi}\left[\frac{\pi X}{4} - \frac{X\alpha}{2} + \frac{X\sin 2\alpha}{4} - \Delta\cos\alpha\right]$$

### 3.1.6 Substitute $\sin 2\alpha = 2\sin\alpha\cos\alpha = 2\frac{\Delta}{X}\cos\alpha$

$$\frac{X\sin 2\alpha}{4} = \frac{X}{4}\cdot 2\cdot\frac{\Delta}{X}\cos\alpha = \frac{\Delta\cos\alpha}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$Y_1 = \frac{4K}{\pi}\left[\frac{\pi X}{4} - \frac{X\alpha}{2} + \frac{\Delta\cos\alpha}{2} - \Delta\cos\alpha\right]$$

$$Y_1 = \frac{4K}{\pi}\left[\frac{\pi X}{4} - \frac{X\alpha}{2} - \frac{\Delta\cos\alpha}{2}\right]$$

### 3.1.7 Final Result for the Describing Function

$$N(X) = \frac{Y_1}{X} = K - \frac{2K}{\pi}\!\left[\alpha + \frac{\Delta\cos\alpha}{X}\right]$$

Or, with $\alpha = \sin^{-1}(\Delta/X)$:

$$\boxed{\,N_{\text{dead-zone}}(X) = K - \frac{2K}{\pi}\!\left[\sin^{-1}\!\frac{\Delta}{X} + \frac{\Delta}{X}\sqrt{1-\frac{\Delta^{2}}{X^{2}}}\right]\,}$$

### 3.1.8 Physical Interpretation

- When $X \to \infty$: $\alpha \to 0$, so $N(X) \to K$ (linear region far from threshold).
- When $X \to \Delta^{+}$: $\alpha \to \pi/2$, so $N(X) \to 0$ (no fundamental component at threshold).

This is the **KTU-canonical result** that students must memorize for the 14-mark DF analysis question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Describing Function of Ideal Relay

The output of an ideal relay is a **square wave of amplitude $M$** (for input $x = X\sin\omega t$ with $X > 0$). The Fourier series of a square wave gives the fundamental amplitude:

$$Y_1 = \frac{4M}{\pi}$$

Since the input is $X\sin\omega t$, the describing function is:

$$\boxed{\,N_{\text{ideal relay}}(X) = \frac{4M}{\pi X}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice $N(X)$ for the ideal relay is **independent of $X$ in shape** — it varies only as $1/X$. This is why the relay is the *canonical* nonlinearity for limit-cycle analysis: it has only one free parameter to plot on the Nichols chart.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Describing Function of Saturation

For a saturating element with $y_s = K x_s$ and input $X > x_s$, the break angle satisfies $\beta = \sin^{-1}(x_s/X)$. By a derivation symmetric to the dead-zone case, the result is:

$$N_{\text{sat}}(X) = \frac{2K}{\pi}\!\left[\sin^{-1}\!\frac{x_s}{X} + \frac{x_s}{X}\sqrt{1-\frac{x_s^{2}}{X^{2}}}\right]$$

with the limits:
- $X \leq x_s$: $N(X) = K$ (still in the linear region).
- $X \to \infty$: $N(X) \to 0$ (output pinned at $y_s$ while input grows).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symmetry Note:** $N_{\text{sat}}(X) = K - N_{\text{dead-zone}}(X)$ when the parameters $\Delta = x_s$ are matched. This is a direct consequence of the algebraic identity between the two nonlinearities. Examiners love to test this.

---

## 3.4 Analytic Matrix — Describing Function of All Seven Nonlinearities

| # | Nonlinearity | $N(X)$ Formula | Range of Validity | Limit as $X \to \infty$ |
|---|--------------|---------------|------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Dead Zone | $K - \frac{2K}{\pi}\left[\sin^{-1}\!\frac{\Delta}{X} + \frac{\Delta}{X}\sqrt{1-\Delta^{2}/X^{2}}\right]$ | $X \geq \Delta$ | $K$ |
| 2 | Saturation | $\frac{2K}{\pi}\left[\sin^{-1}\!\frac{x_s}{X} + \frac{x_s}{X}\sqrt{1-x_s^{2}/X^{2}}\right]$ | $X \geq x_s$ | $0$ |
| 3 | Backlash (with $b$, $K$) | $\frac{K}{\pi}\!\left[\frac{\pi}{2} + \sin^{-1}\!\left(1 - \frac{2b}{X}\right) + 2\left(1 - \frac{2b}{X}\right)\sqrt{\frac{b}{X}\left(1-\frac{b}{X}\right)}\right]$ | $X \geq b$ | $K$ |
| 4 | Ideal Relay | $\dfrac{4M}{\pi X}$ | $X > 0$ | $0$ |
| 5 | 3-Pos Relay w/ Dead Zone | $\dfrac{4M}{\pi X}\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{\Delta}{X}\right)^{2}}$ for $X \geq \Delta$ | $X \geq \Delta$ | $0$ |
| 6 | Relay + Hysteresis | $\dfrac{4M}{\pi X}\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{a}{X}\right)^{2}} - j\dfrac{4Ma}{\pi X^{2}}$ | $X \geq a$ | $0$ (real part), $0$ (imag part) |
| 7 | Coulomb + Viscous Friction | $B + \dfrac{4F_c}{\pi X}$ | $X > 0$ | $B$ |

> [!WARNING]
> The hysteresis relay (N6) is the **only** commonly tested nonlinearity with a **non-zero imaginary part** in its describing function. This is the KTU favourite trick: students forget the $-j$ term and lose 2 marks.

---

## 3.5 Derivation — Limit-Cycle Condition (Preview of Next Sub-Topic)

For a unity-feedback system with nonlinearity $N(X)$ inside a linear plant $G(j\omega)$, the **closed-loop characteristic equation** is:

$$1 + N(X)\,G(j\omega) = 0$$

This splits into two simultaneous equations (necessary conditions for a self-sustained oscillation of amplitude $X$ and frequency $\omega$):

$$\text{Real part:}\quad 1 + N(X)\,\mathrm{Re}[G(j\omega)] = 0$$

$$\text{Imag part:}\quad N(X)\,\mathrm{Im}[G(j\omega)] = 0$$

These are solved graphically on the **Nichols chart** (plot $-1/N(X)$ locus and $G(j\omega)$ locus; their intersection gives $(X^*, \omega^*)$ — the limit cycle).

> [!TIP]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus splits this into two consecutive sub-topics. The above 3-line result is *the* bridge. **Memorize it.**

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

We use Mermaid diagrams to convey the *structure* of the seven standard nonlinearities. (Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguard, node IDs are alphanumeric and labels are raw uppercase text.)

---

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Nonlinearity Inside a Linear Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[INPUT R] --> SUMM[JUNCTION SUMM]
    SUMM --> NL[NONLINEARITY N]
    NL --> PLANT[LINEAR PLANT G]
    PLANT --> OUTPUT[OUTPUT Y]
    OUTPUT --> FB[FEEDBACK H]
    FB --> SUMM
```

This is the **canonical nonlinear feedback structure** assumed throughout Module 3.

---

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Classifying the Seven Nonlinearities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[NONLINEAR ELEMENT] --> Q1{Is there MEMORY?}
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Is the I-O CURVE SINGLE-VALUED?}
    Q1 -- YES --> MEM[WITH MEMORY BLOCK]
    Q2 -- YES --> Q3{Does the output SATURATE at extremes?}
    Q2 -- NO --> HYST[Hysteresis or Backlash]
    Q3 -- YES --> SAT[Saturation N2]
    Q3 -- NO --> Q4{Is there a DEAD BAND around origin?}
    Q4 -- YES --> DEAD[Dead Zone N1]
    Q4 -- NO --> Q5{Is output ONLY plus or minus M?}
    Q5 -- YES --> REL[Relay N4 N5 N6]
    Q5 -- NO --> MISC[Other Memoryless N]
    MEM --> Q6{Does output LAG on reversal?}
    Q6 -- YES --> BACK[Backlash N3]
    Q6 -- NO --> COUL[Coulomb Friction N7]
```

---

## 4.3 Input–Output Sketches (Rendered as Conceptual Block Diagrams)

### 4.3.1 Dead Zone

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DZ[DEAD ZONE N1]
        IN[Input X] --> COMP[Compare with plus minus Delta]
        COMP --> MUL[Multiply by K]
        MUL --> OUT[Output Y]
    end
```

### 4.3.2 Saturation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SAT[SATURATION N2]
        IN[Input X] --> LIN[Linear Gain K]
        LIN --> CLAMP[Clamp to plus minus Ys]
        CLAMP --> OUT[Output Y]
    end
```

### 4.3.3 Backlash

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BL[BACKLASH N3]
        IN[Input X] --> DETECT[Detect Direction of Motion]
        DETECT --> SHIFT[Shift by plus or minus b over 2]
        SHIFT --> GAIN[Multiply by K]
        GAIN --> HOLD[Hold last value during reversal]
        HOLD --> OUT[Output Y]
    end
```

### 4.3.4 Ideal Relay & 3-Position Relay

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REL[RELAYS N4 N5 N6]
        IN[Input X] --> SIGN[Sign of X]
        SIGN --> SW[Switch Output]
        SW -- X greater than Delta --> POS[Output plus M]
        SW -- X less than minus Delta --> NEG[Output minus M]
        SW -- middle band --> ZERO[Output ZERO]
    end
```

### 4.3.5 Coulomb + Viscous Friction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FR[FRICTION N7]
        VIN[Velocity dot X] --> SPLIT[Split Path]
        SPLIT --> SGN[Signum Function]
        SGN --> MUL1[Multiply by Fc]
        SPLIT --> GAIN[Multiply by B]
        GAIN --> MUL2[Scaled Velocity]
        MUL1 --> ADD[Add Both]
        MUL2 --> ADD
        ADD --> FOUT[Friction Force F]
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Closed-Loop Block Diagram with All Nonlinearity Slots

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Input R] --> B[Subtractor Node]
    B --> C[Controller C s]
    C --> D{Nonlinearity Slot}
    D -- N1 --> D1[Dead Zone]
    D -- N2 --> D2[Saturation]
    D -- N3 --> D3[Backlash]
    D -- N4 --> D4[Ideal Relay]
    D -- N5 --> D5[Three Pos Relay]
    D -- N6 --> D6[Hysteresis Relay]
    D -- N7 --> D7[Friction]
    D1 --> E[Plant G s]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    D7 --> E
    E --> F[Output Y]
    F --> G[Feedback H s]
    G --> B
```

This figure is the **only** block diagram examiners expect in 14-mark limit-cycle questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]
**Define a memoryless nonlinearity. List any three memoryless nonlinear elements.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **memoryless nonlinearity** is a static element whose output at any instant depends only on the present value of the input and not on its past history. Mathematically $y(t) = f(x(t))$.
Three examples (any 3): **1. Dead zone, 2. Saturation, 3. Ideal (ON–OFF) relay, 4. Three-position relay with dead zone.** *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing and brief mention.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]
**Distinguish between intentional and incidental nonlinearities with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Intentional nonlinearities** are deliberately introduced into the control loop to obtain a desired control action or to limit signals. *Example:* relay used in a thermostat; saturation deliberately added for safety.
- **Incidental nonlinearities** are inherent physical imperfections in plant components. *Example:* gear backlash in a mechanical transmission; Coulomb friction in a motor bearing.
*(1.5 marks each.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat input–output sketch, explain the **dead-zone** and **saturation** nonlinearities. Give one practical example of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **describing function** of an ideal (ON–OFF) relay whose input is $x(t) = X\sin\omega t$ and output is $\pm M$. Hence state the **limit-cycle condition** for a unity-feedback system containing the relay in cascade with a linear plant $G(s)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

A **dead-zone** element produces zero output as long as the input magnitude lies within $\pm\Delta$. Outside this band the output follows $K(x \mp \Delta)$.

$$\text{I/O:}\quad y = \begin{cases} 0, & \vert x \vert \leq \Delta \\ K(x - \Delta), & x > \Delta \\ K(x + \Delta), & x < -\Delta \end{cases}$$

*Sketch:* a flat segment of width $2\Delta$ around the origin with linear arms of slope $K$.
*Example:* small DC motor stiction — a minimum armature voltage is required before rotation begins. *(3.5 marks)*

A **saturation** element is linear in the range $\vert x \vert \leq x_s$ but the output is clamped to $\pm y_s$ for larger inputs.

$$y = \begin{cases} Kx, & \vert x \vert \leq x_s \\ \pm y_s, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

*Sketch:* a straight line of slope $K$ whose ends flatten at $\pm y_s$.
*Example:* a control valve that is fully open — flow cannot increase further. *(3.5 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (b)

Let the relay input be $x(t) = X\sin\omega t$ (with $X > 0$). The output is a **square wave** of amplitude $M$:

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} +M, & \sin\omega t > 0 \\ -M, & \sin\omega t < 0 \end{cases}$$

The Fourier series of a square wave of amplitude $M$ contains only odd harmonics. The fundamental (sine) component has amplitude:

$$Y_1 = \frac{4M}{\pi}$$

Therefore the **describing function** is:

$$N(X) = \frac{Y_1}{X} = \frac{4M}{\pi X}$$

*(4 marks)*

For a unity-feedback loop with linear plant $G(s)$ and the relay inside, the closed-loop characteristic equation is:

$$1 + N(X)\,G(j\omega) = 0 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad G(j\omega) = -\frac{1}{N(X)} = -\frac{\pi X}{4M}$$

A **limit cycle** exists if the Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$ intersects the **negative real axis** at a point whose magnitude is $-\pi X/4M$ for some positive $X$. *(3 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Main Paper]*

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **backlash** nonlinearity. Why is it classified as a *non-memoryless* element? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The output of a saturation element is given by

$$y = \begin{cases} -y_s, & x \leq -x_s \\ Kx, & \vert x \vert \leq x_s \\ +y_s, & x \geq +x_s \end{cases}$$

For an input $x(t) = X\sin\omega t$ with $X > x_s$, derive the **describing function** $N(X)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Backlash** arises from the mechanical play $b$ between two meshed gear teeth. When the input direction reverses, the output remains constant until the play is taken up.

*I/O Sketch (qualitative):* a parallelogram-shaped loop traced out as $x$ oscillates; during reversal, the output is **flat** over a span $\pm b/2$ in $x$.

*Mathematical description:*

$$y = \begin{cases} K\!\left(x - \tfrac{b}{2}\right) & \text{if } x \text{ is increasing and } x \geq x_{\text{prev}} \\ K\!\left(x + \tfrac{b}{2}\right) & \text{if } x \text{ is decreasing and } x \leq x_{\text{prev}} \\ y_{\text{last}} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Why non-memoryless?** The output at any instant depends on the **past history** of the input (specifically, the *direction* of motion and the last value), not just the present value. Hence $y(t) \neq f(x(t))$ — it is $y(t) = f(x(t), \dot{x}(t), y(t^-))$. *(3.5 marks for sketch + description; 3.5 marks for memory explanation.)*

#### Model Solution — (b)

When the input $x(t) = X\sin\omega t$ exceeds $x_s$ in magnitude, the output is a **truncated sine wave**: a pure sinusoid $Kx$ in the middle, flat at $\pm y_s$ at the extremes. The break angle $\beta$ is:

$$\sin\beta = \frac{x_s}{X} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \beta = \sin^{-1}\!\frac{x_s}{X}$$

By Fourier analysis, the fundamental output amplitude is:

$$Y_1 = \frac{2}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi} y(\theta)\sin\theta\, d\theta$$

Exploiting symmetry and breaking the integral into three regions:

$$Y_1 = \frac{2}{\pi}\!\left[\int_{0}^{\beta}y_s\sin\theta\,d\theta + \int_{\beta}^{\pi-\beta} KX\sin^{2}\theta\,d\theta + \int_{\pi-\beta}^{\pi}y_s\sin\theta\,d\theta\right]$$

Working through these standard integrals:

$$\int_{0}^{\beta}\sin\theta\,d\theta = 1-\cos\beta$$

$$\int_{\beta}^{\pi-\beta}\sin^{2}\theta\,d\theta = \frac{\pi}{2} - \beta - \frac{\sin 2\beta}{2} = \frac{\pi}{2} - \beta - \sin\beta\cos\beta$$

Combining, and using $y_s = Kx_s$ and $\sin\beta = x_s/X$, we obtain:

$$Y_1 = \frac{2KX}{\pi}\!\left[\frac{\pi}{2} - \beta + \sin\beta\cos\beta\right] = \frac{2K}{\pi}\!\left[\frac{\pi X}{2} - X\beta + X\sin\beta\cos\beta\right]$$

Replacing $X\sin\beta\cos\beta = x_s\cos\beta = x_s\sqrt{1-x_s^{2}/X^{2}}$ and dividing by $X$:

$$\boxed{\,N_{\text{sat}}(X) = \frac{2K}{\pi}\!\left[\sin^{-1}\!\frac{x_s}{X} + \frac{x_s}{X}\sqrt{1-\frac{x_s^{2}}{X^{2}}}\right]\,}$$

*Check:* when $X \to x_s^+$, $\beta \to \pi/2$, $N \to K$. When $X \to \infty$, $\beta \to 0$ and $N \to 0$. ✔ *(Full 7 marks for the complete derivation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the symmetry factor** of 4 (or 2) in the Fourier integral — losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing the break-angle definition** for dead zone ($\alpha = \sin^{-1}\Delta/X$) vs. saturation ($\beta = \sin^{-1}x_s/X$) — losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Dropping the imaginary part** of the hysteresis-relay describing function — losing 2 marks in the 14-mark question.
> 4. **Writing $y(t) = f(x(t))$ for backlash** — this is wrong because backlash *is* memory-dependent. The examiner will cut 1 mark.
> 5. **Mixing up Coulomb and viscous friction** — the Coulomb term is multiplied by $\mathrm{sgn}(\dot{x})$, not by $\dot{x}$.
> 6. **Not stating the limit-cycle condition** $G(j\omega) = -1/N(X)$ explicitly when it is asked for.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Definition.** A nonlinearity violates the superposition principle; it is *memoryless* if $y = f(x)$ alone, *with memory* otherwise.
- **Seven canonical KTU nonlinearities:** Dead Zone (N1), Saturation (N2), Backlash (N3), Ideal Relay (N4), 3-Position Relay (N5), Hysteresis Relay (N6), Coulomb + Viscous Friction (N7).
- **Odd-symmetry ⇒ no DC, no cosine term** in the describing function.
- **DF of ideal relay:** $N(X) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi X}$ — purely real, $1/X$ shape.
- **DF of saturation:** $N(X) = \dfrac{2K}{\pi}\!\left[\sin^{-1}\!\dfrac{x_s}{X} + \dfrac{x_s}{X}\sqrt{1-\dfrac{x_s^2}{X^2}}\right]$ — equals $K$ at $X=x_s$, tends to $0$ as $X\to\infty$.
- **DF of dead zone:** $N_{\text{DZ}}(X) = K - N_{\text{sat}}(X)$ when $\Delta = x_s$ — *complementary* result, examinable as a one-mark identity.
- **DF of hysteresis relay** is the *only* one with a non-zero imaginary part $-j\dfrac{4Ma}{\pi X^2}$.
- **Backlash** is the canonical **with-memory** nonlinearity — the output depends on direction of motion.
- **Limit-cycle condition:** intersection of $G(j\omega)$ locus with $-1/N(X)$ locus on the **Nichols chart**.
- **Coulomb friction** produces a discontinuity at $\dot{x}=0$ → **stick-slip** behaviour.
- **Saturation + integrator in a loop** = **integrator wind-up** — students should remember this for the next KTU module on compensation.
- **Default approach** to analyse a memoryless nonlinear system in Module 3 = **Describing Function Method** (covered in the next sub-topic, but built entirely on this foundation).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis through Harmonic Linearisation – Determination of Describing Function of Nonlinearities (Relay, Dead Zone and Saturation Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT636 - ADVANCED CONTROL THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analysis through Harmonic Linearisation – Determination of Describing Function of Nonlinearities (Relay, Dead Zone and Saturation Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Harmonic Linearisation — Formal Definition

**Harmonic Linearisation (Describing Function Method)** is an approximate frequency-domain analysis technique used to study nonlinear control systems. The fundamental principle states that:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** When a sinusoidal signal $x(t) = X\sin(\omega t)$ is applied to a nonlinear element, the output $y(t)$ is generally non-sinusoidal. However, if the linear part of the system has **low-pass filtering characteristics**, the higher harmonics generated by the nonlinearity are sufficiently attenuated, leaving only the **fundamental component** to circulate in the loop. The nonlinearity is then replaced by an equivalent linear "gain" called the **Describing Function (DF)**.

The **Describing Function** is formally defined as the complex ratio of the fundamental Fourier component of the output to the input sinusoid:

$$N(X, \omega) = \frac{Y_1}{X} e^{j\phi_1} = \frac{1}{X}\left[\sqrt{A_1^2 + B_1^2}\right] \angle \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{A_1}{B_1}\right)$$

where the Fourier coefficients are:

$$A_1 = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} y(t)\sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t), \quad B_1 = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} y(t)\cos(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

For **memoryless, odd-symmetric, single-valued** nonlinearities (no hysteresis), the output contains only odd harmonics and is anti-symmetric. This forces $A_1 = 0$, reducing the describing function to a **real, amplitude-dependent gain**:

$$N(X) = \frac{B_1}{X} = \frac{4}{\pi X}\int_{0}^{\pi/2} y(t)\sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Volume Distortion" Metaphor

Imagine a guitarist playing through a **distortion pedal** (the nonlinearity) connected to an **amplifier with a heavy low-pass tone knob** (the linear plant). The pedal chops the clean sine wave into a blocky, harmonic-rich square-like waveform. However, the amplifier's tone knob suppresses everything above the fundamental note. To the listener, it sounds almost like a clean tone — but slightly louder and slightly different in color.

The **Describing Function** is the engineer's mathematical "loudness + color" rating of the pedal, expressed as a single complex number that depends on how hard the guitarist strums (the **input amplitude $X$**). It allows us to analyze the entire system using familiar linear Bode/Nyquist tools, despite the pedal being nonlinear.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The three nonlinearities you must master are the **Ideal Relay**, **Relay with Dead Zone**, **Dead Zone**, and **Saturation**. Each must be derived by explicitly setting up the Fourier integral and evaluating the fundamental harmonic component.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Input amplitude $X$** — Peak value of applied sinusoid, units depend on the physical signal (Volts, Nm, m, etc.)
- **Output saturation level $M$** — Relay magnitude (Volts, Nm, etc.)
- **Dead zone half-width $\Delta$** — Threshold below which the output is zero
- **Linear slope $k$** — Gain of the linear portion (output units / input units)
- **Saturation limit $a$** — Input magnitude at which saturation begins
- **Fundamental frequency $\omega$** — Rad/s, assumed preserved in the linear element

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The General DF Computation Procedure

The describing function for any memoryless nonlinearity is computed via this four-step algorithm:

1. **Apply a trial sinusoid** $x(t) = X\sin(\omega t)$ at the input.
2. **Sketch the output waveform** $y(t)$ — identify the switching/transit angles $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \ldots$
3. **Express $y(t)$ piecewise** as a function of $\omega t$ over the interval $[0, 2\pi]$.
4. **Evaluate the fundamental Fourier coefficient** $B_1 = \frac{4}{\pi X}\int_{0}^{\pi/2} y(t)\sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$ and form $N(X) = B_1 / X$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $B_1$ alone suffices:** For odd-symmetric nonlinearities, the output has half-wave symmetry: $y(t + \pi) = -y(t)$. This forces all even Fourier coefficients to vanish. With no hysteresis, there is no phase shift between input and fundamental output, eliminating $A_1$ and making the DF purely real.

### 2.2 Switching-Angle Convention

For nonlinearities involving a **dead zone $\Delta$** or **saturation limit $a$**, the input $x = X\sin(\omega t)$ crosses the threshold at:

$$\alpha = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\Delta}{X}\right) \quad \text{(dead zone threshold angle)}$$

$$\alpha = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{a}{X}\right) \quad \text{(saturation threshold angle)}$$

The angle $\alpha$ defines the boundary between the **linear region** and the **nonlinear region** in one quarter-cycle. The function $\cos\alpha$ appears repeatedly in derivations:

$$\cos\alpha = \cos\!\left[\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\Delta}{X}\right)\right] = \sqrt{1 - \frac{\Delta^2}{X^2}} = \frac{\sqrt{X^2 - \Delta^2}}{X}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Describing Functions of Standard Nonlinearities

| **Nonlinearity** | **Input–Output Relation** | **Switching Angle** | **Describing Function $N(X)$** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ideal Relay** | $y = +M$ for $x>0$; $y = -M$ for $x<0$ | None (instantaneous) | $N(X) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi X}$ | DF $\to \infty$ as $X \to 0$ |
| **Relay with Dead Zone** | $y=0$ for $\vert x\vert < \Delta$; $y=\pm M$ otherwise | $\alpha = \sin^{-1}(\Delta/X)$ | $N(X) = \dfrac{4M}{\pi X^2}\sqrt{X^2 - \Delta^2}$ | Valid only for $X \geq \Delta$ |
| **Dead Zone** | $y=0$ for $\vert x\vert < \Delta$; $y=k(x \mp \Delta)$ otherwise | $\alpha = \sin^{-1}(\Delta/X)$ | $N(X) = k - \dfrac{2k}{\pi}\!\left[\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2}\sin(2\alpha)\right]$ | $N \to k$ as $X \to \infty$; $N = 0$ at $X = \Delta$ |
| **Saturation** | $y=kx$ for $\vert x\vert < a$; $y=\pm ka$ for $\vert x\vert > a$ | $\alpha = \sin^{-1}(a/X)$ | $N(X) = \dfrac{2k}{\pi}\!\left[\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2}\sin(2\alpha)\right]$ | $N \to k$ as $X \to a$; $N \to 0$ as $X \to \infty$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Describing functions are extensively used in:

- **Aerospace**: Predicting limit-cycle oscillations in flight control actuators with rate saturation.
- **Robotics**: Analysing stick-slip friction in joints.
- **Power Electronics**: Studying self-oscillation in relay-controlled switching regulators and PWM inverters.
- **Process Control**: Detecting unwanted cycling in on-off valve controllers.
- **Automotive**: Analysing anti-lock brake system (ABS) modulator valve nonlinearities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The DF technique is the **bridge between linear classical control and nonlinear reality**. Once a DF is obtained, the entire Bode plot, Nyquist plot, and Routh-Hurwitz machinery become applicable to nonlinear systems — a powerful conceptual unification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Ideal Relay (ON-OFF Controller)

**Setup:** Input $x(t) = X\sin(\omega t)$. Output:

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} +M, & 0 < \omega t < \pi \\ -M, & \pi < \omega t < 2\pi \end{cases}$$

The output is a **square wave** of amplitude $M$. By half-wave symmetry, only the fundamental $B_1$ is computed.

**Step 1: Set up the integral over one half-cycle (use symmetry factor of 4).**

$$B_1 = \frac{4}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi/2} y(t)\sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \frac{4}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi/2} M\sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 2: Evaluate the integral.**

$$B_1 = \frac{4M}{\pi}\Big[-\cos(\omega t)\Big]_{0}^{\pi/2} = \frac{4M}{\pi}\Big[-\cos(\pi/2) + \cos(0)\Big]$$

**Step 3: Substitute the cosine values.**

$$B_1 = \frac{4M}{\pi}\Big[-(0) + 1\Big] = \frac{4M}{\pi}$$

**Step 4: Form the describing function.**

$$N(X) = \frac{B_1}{X} = \frac{4M}{\pi X}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** The DF of the ideal relay is **inversely proportional to $X$**. As the input amplitude shrinks, the equivalent gain becomes enormous, which is precisely why on-off controllers exhibit relay-type high-gain behaviour near the setpoint.

---

### 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Relay with Dead Zone

**Setup:** Input $x(t) = X\sin(\omega t)$ with $X \geq \Delta$. Output:

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & 0 < \omega t < \alpha \\ +M, & \alpha < \omega t < \pi - \alpha \\ 0, & \pi - \alpha < \omega t < \pi \\ -M, & \pi + \alpha < \omega t < 2\pi - \alpha \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The switching angle satisfies $X\sin\alpha = \Delta$, hence $\alpha = \sin^{-1}(\Delta/X)$.

**Step 1: Set up the integral using the quarter-wave symmetry factor.**

$$B_1 = \frac{4}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi/2} y(t)\sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \frac{4}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} M\sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

(The integrand is zero from $0$ to $\alpha$ since $y = 0$ there.)

**Step 2: Evaluate the definite integral.**

$$B_1 = \frac{4M}{\pi}\Big[-\cos(\omega t)\Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} = \frac{4M}{\pi}\Big[-\cos(\pi/2) + \cos\alpha\Big] = \frac{4M}{\pi}\cos\alpha$$

**Step 3: Substitute $\cos\alpha = \sqrt{1 - (\Delta/X)^2}$.**

$$B_1 = \frac{4M}{\pi}\sqrt{1 - \frac{\Delta^2}{X^2}} = \frac{4M}{\pi X}\sqrt{X^2 - \Delta^2}$$

**Step 4: Form the describing function.**

$$N(X) = \frac{B_1}{X} = \frac{4M}{\pi X^2}\sqrt{X^2 - \Delta^2}$$

**Step 5: Limiting cases (sanity check).**

- As $\Delta \to 0$: $N(X) \to \frac{4M}{\pi X}$ — matches the ideal relay. ✓
- As $X \to \Delta$: $N(X) \to 0$ — physically consistent, the relay just barely activates. ✓
- As $X \to \infty$: $N(X) \to 0$ — large signals saturate the equivalent gain. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric meaning:** $\cos\alpha$ is the projection of the unit input phasor onto the active switching window, scaled by the relay magnitude $M$.

---

### 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Dead Zone Nonlinearity

**Setup:** Input $x(t) = X\sin(\omega t)$ with $X \geq \Delta$. Output:

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & 0 \leq \omega t \leq \alpha \\ k(X\sin\omega t - \Delta), & \alpha \leq \omega t \leq \pi - \alpha \\ 0, & \pi - \alpha \leq \omega t \leq \pi \end{cases}$$

(plus symmetric negative cycle), with $\alpha = \sin^{-1}(\Delta/X)$ and $k$ being the linear slope.

**Step 1: Set up the quarter-cycle integral.**

$$B_1 = \frac{4}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi/2} y(t)\sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \frac{4}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} k(X\sin\omega t - \Delta)\sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 2: Split into two integrals.**

$$B_1 = \frac{4kX}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2}\sin^2(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) - \frac{4k\Delta}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2}\sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the first integral using $\sin^2\theta = (1 - \cos 2\theta)/2$.**

$$\int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2}\sin^2(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[\omega t - \frac{\sin 2\omega t}{2}\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi/2}$$

$$= \frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\frac{\pi}{2} - 0\right) - \left(\alpha - \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)\right] = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{\pi}{2} - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the second integral.**

$$\int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2}\sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \Big[-\cos(\omega t)\Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} = -\cos(\pi/2) + \cos\alpha = \cos\alpha$$

**Step 5: Combine and apply the constraint $\Delta = X\sin\alpha$.**

Note that $2\Delta\cos\alpha = 2X\sin\alpha\cos\alpha = X\sin 2\alpha$. Substituting:

$$B_1 = \frac{4kX}{\pi} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{\pi}{2} - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right] - \frac{4k\Delta}{\pi}\cos\alpha$$

$$= \frac{2kX}{\pi}\left[\frac{\pi}{2} - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right] - \frac{4kX\sin\alpha\cos\alpha}{\pi}$$

$$= kX - \frac{2kX\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{kX\sin 2\alpha}{\pi} - \frac{2kX\sin 2\alpha}{\pi}$$

$$= kX - \frac{2kX\alpha}{\pi} - \frac{kX\sin 2\alpha}{\pi}$$

**Step 6: Form the describing function by dividing by $X$.**

$$N(X) = \frac{B_1}{X} = k - \frac{2k\alpha}{\pi} - \frac{k\sin 2\alpha}{\pi} = k - \frac{2k}{\pi}\left[\alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]$$

**Step 7: Limiting cases (sanity check).**

- As $X \to \Delta$: $\alpha \to \pi/2$, so $\sin 2\alpha \to 0$ and $N(X) \to k - \frac{2k}{\pi}(\pi/2) = 0$. ✓
- As $X \to \infty$: $\alpha \to 0$, so $\sin 2\alpha \approx 2\alpha$ and $N(X) \to k - \frac{2k}{\pi}(0 + 0) = k$. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The DF of a dead-zone element approaches the linear gain $k$ at large amplitudes (where the dead zone is negligible) and vanishes at the threshold (where the input is "swallowed" by the dead zone). This makes dead zone a **variable attenuator** that suppresses small signals.

---

### 3.4 DERIVATION 4 — Saturation Nonlinearity

**Setup:** Input $x(t) = X\sin(\omega t)$ with $X \geq a$. Output:

$$y(t) = \begin{cases} kX\sin\omega t, & 0 \leq \omega t \leq \alpha \\ +ka, & \alpha \leq \omega t \leq \pi - \alpha \\ kX\sin\omega t, & \pi - \alpha \leq \omega t \leq \pi \end{cases}$$

(plus symmetric negative cycle), with $\alpha = \sin^{-1}(a/X)$.

**Step 1: Set up the quarter-cycle integral.**

$$B_1 = \frac{4}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi/2} y(t)\sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \frac{4}{\pi}\left[\int_{0}^{\alpha} kX\sin^2(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) + \int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2} ka\sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)\right]$$

**Step 2: Evaluate the first integral.**

$$\int_{0}^{\alpha}\sin^2(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[\alpha - \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the second integral.**

$$\int_{\alpha}^{\pi/2}\sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \cos\alpha$$

**Step 4: Combine and apply the constraint $a = X\sin\alpha$.**

$$B_1 = \frac{4kX}{\pi} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left[\alpha - \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right] + \frac{4ka}{\pi}\cos\alpha$$

$$= \frac{2kX\alpha}{\pi} - \frac{kX\sin 2\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{4kX\sin\alpha\cos\alpha}{\pi}$$

$$= \frac{2kX\alpha}{\pi} - \frac{kX\sin 2\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{2kX\sin 2\alpha}{\pi}$$

$$= \frac{2kX\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{kX\sin 2\alpha}{\pi}$$

**Step 5: Form the describing function.**

$$N(X) = \frac{B_1}{X} = \frac{2k\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{k\sin 2\alpha}{\pi} = \frac{2k}{\pi}\left[\alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]$$

**Step 6: Limiting cases (sanity check).**

- As $X \to a$: $\alpha \to \pi/2$, $\sin 2\alpha \to 0$, $N(X) \to \frac{2k}{\pi}(\pi/2) = k$. ✓ (Linear region)
- As $X \to \infty$: $\alpha \to 0$, $\sin 2\alpha \approx 2\alpha$, $N(X) \to 0$. ✓ (Hard saturation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Dual relationship with Dead Zone:** Observe that $N_{\text{sat}}(X) = k - N_{\text{dead zone}}(X)$. The saturation DF and the dead-zone DF sum exactly to the linear gain $k$. This is the famous **complementarity** between the two nonlinearities — a frequent KTU viva question.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Nonlinearity** | **DF Expression** | **Gain at $X \to 0$** | **Gain at $X \to \infty$** | **Type of Gain Variation** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ideal Relay | $\dfrac{4M}{\pi X}$ | $\infty$ | $0$ | Monotonically decreasing |
| Relay + Dead Zone | $\dfrac{4M\sqrt{X^2 - \Delta^2}}{\pi X^2}$ | $0$ (at $X=\Delta$) | $0$ | Peaks at intermediate $X$ |
| Dead Zone | $k - \dfrac{2k}{\pi}\!\left[\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\alpha\right]$ | $0$ (at $X=\Delta$) | $k$ | Monotonically increasing |
| Saturation | $\dfrac{2k}{\pi}\!\left[\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\alpha\right]$ | $k$ (at $X=a$) | $0$ | Monotonically decreasing |
