<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Phase Plane Analysis** of nonlinear autonomous systems of the form:

$$\dot{x}_1 = f_1(x_1, x_2)$$
$$\dot{x}_2 = f_2(x_1, x_2)$$

a **Singular Point** (also called an **Equilibrium Point**, **Critical Point**, or **Fixed Point**) is a point $(x_{1e}, x_{2e})$ in the state space where the time derivatives of all state variables vanish simultaneously:

$$\dot{x}_1 = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \dot{x}_2 = 0$$

Mathematically:

$$f_1(x_{1e}, x_{2e}) = 0$$
$$f_2(x_{1e}, x_{2e}) = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Singular points are the most critical topological features of the phase portrait. Their classification (Node, Saddle, Focus, Center) completely characterizes the local qualitative behavior of the nonlinear system in their neighborhood.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are **standing on a hill landscape**. The "state" is your exact position on this landscape, and the "dynamics" dictate how you would roll, slide, or move based on the slope at your location.

- **Top of a hill** → Unstable equilibrium. The slightest push sends you rolling away.
- **Bottom of a valley** → Stable equilibrium. If pushed slightly, you return to the bottom.
- **Mountain pass** → Saddle point. Stable in one direction, unstable in the perpendicular direction.
- **Whirlpool center** → Stable focus. You spiral inward.
- **Eye of a hurricane** → Center. You orbit forever without escaping.

In control systems, the singular point is that special location where the system "rests" if undisturbed, and its **classification** tells us what happens when the system is slightly disturbed from this rest position.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

- **Eigenvalues** ($\lambda_1, \lambda_2$): Roots of the characteristic equation of the Jacobian matrix.
- **Trace** ($\Delta$): $\text{tr}(A) = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2$
- **Determinant** ($\Delta$): $\det(A) = \lambda_1 \cdot \lambda_2$
- **Discriminant** ($\Delta$): $\Delta = (\text{tr}(A))^2 - 4\det(A)$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Foundation — Linearization via Jacobian

For a nonlinear system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})$, the **Jacobian Matrix** evaluated at the singular point $(x_{1e}, x_{2e})$ is:

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} & \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_2} \\ \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_1} & \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_2} \end{bmatrix}_{x_{1e},x_{2e}} = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$$

The **linearized system** near the singular point becomes:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}} \approx J \cdot \mathbf{x}$$

The **characteristic equation** is:

$$\lambda^2 - (\text{tr}\, J)\lambda + \det(J) = 0$$

### 2.2 Why and How of Classification

The classification logic proceeds as follows:
1. Compute the **trace** $T = a + d$ and **determinant** $\Delta = ad - bc$.
2. Form the discriminant $\Delta_d = T^2 - 4\Delta$.
3. Based on the **signs and magnitudes** of $T$, $\Delta$, and $\Delta_d$, classify the singular point.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Classification Table

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorization Tip for KTU Exam:** Draw a 2D plane with axes $T = \text{tr}(A)$ and $\Delta = \det(A)$. The famous **parabola** $\Delta = T^2/4$ divides the regions. Use this as your mental map.

| S.No | Eigenvalues ($\lambda_1, \lambda_2$) | Discriminant $\Delta_d$ | Type of Singular Point | Stability |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|
| 1 | Real, distinct, both negative ($\lambda_1, \lambda_2 < 0$) | $\Delta_d > 0, T<0, \Delta>0$ | **Stable Node** | Asymptotically Stable |
| 2 | Real, distinct, both positive ($\lambda_1, \lambda_2 > 0$) | $\Delta_d > 0, T>0, \Delta>0$ | **Unstable Node** | Unstable |
| 3 | Real, distinct, opposite signs ($\lambda_1 \lambda_2 < 0$) | $\Delta_d > 0, \Delta<0$ | **Saddle Point** | Always Unstable |
| 4 | Complex with negative real part ($\alpha \pm j\beta, \alpha<0$) | $\Delta_d < 0, T<0, \Delta>0$ | **Stable Focus (Spiral)** | Asymptotically Stable |
| 5 | Complex with positive real part ($\alpha \pm j\beta, \alpha>0$) | $\Delta_d < 0, T>0, \Delta>0$ | **Unstable Focus (Spiral)** | Unstable |
| 6 | Purely imaginary ($\pm j\beta$) | $\Delta_d < 0, T=0, \Delta>0$ | **Center (Vortex)** | Marginally Stable |
| 7 | Real, equal, negative ($\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 < 0$) | $\Delta_d = 0, T<0$ | **Stable Proper/Star Node** | Asymptotically Stable |
| 8 | Real, equal, positive ($\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 > 0$) | $\Delta_d = 0, T>0$ | **Unstable Proper/Star Node** | Unstable |
| 9 | Real, equal, one independent eigenvector | $\Delta_d = 0$ | **Improper Node** | Depends on signs |
| 10 | One zero, one negative | $\Delta_d > 0$ | **Degenerate Node** | Unstable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Borderline Cases:**
> - If $\Delta > 0$ and $T = 0$ → **Center** (conservative systems, undamped oscillators).
> - If $\Delta < 0$ → **Saddle Point** (always, regardless of $T$).
> - If $\Delta = 0$ → At least one eigenvalue is zero (non-isolated singular points or degenerate case).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics**: Stability of manipulator joints at rest positions (saddle vs. node).
- **Aircraft Dynamics**: Dutch roll mode identification (focus/center).
- **Power Systems**: Transient stability of generators after disturbances (saddle-node bifurcation).
- **Pendulum Control**: Energy dissipation maps node vs. focus behavior.
- **Chemical Reactors**: Multiple steady-state operation (saddle-point separatrices define safe operating regions).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Characteristic Equation

Starting with the linearized system matrix:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$$

The characteristic polynomial is given by:

$$\det(\lambda I - A) = 0$$

Expanding the determinant:

$$\det \begin{bmatrix} \lambda - a & -b \\ -c & \lambda - d \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

Performing the 2×2 determinant expansion:

$$(\lambda - a)(\lambda - d) - (-b)(-c) = 0$$

$$(\lambda - a)(\lambda - d) - bc = 0$$

$$\lambda^2 - (a+d)\lambda + ad - bc = 0$$

Identifying the trace and determinant:

$$\boxed{\lambda^2 - T\lambda + \Delta = 0}$$

where $T = a + d$ and $\Delta = ad - bc$.

### 3.2 Solving for Eigenvalues (Quadratic Formula)

Using the standard quadratic formula on the characteristic equation:

$$\lambda = \frac{T \pm \sqrt{T^2 - 4\Delta}}{2}$$

Let the discriminant be $\Delta_d = T^2 - 4\Delta$.

**Case 1:** $\Delta_d > 0$ → Eigenvalues are **real and distinct**.

$$\lambda_1 = \frac{T + \sqrt{\Delta_d}}{2}, \quad \lambda_2 = \frac{T - \sqrt{\Delta_d}}{2}$$

**Case 2:** $\Delta_d = 0$ → Eigenvalues are **real and repeated**.

$$\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \frac{T}{2}$$

**Case 3:** $\Delta_d < 0$ → Eigenvalues are **complex conjugates**.

$$\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{T}{2} \pm j\frac{\sqrt{\vert \Delta_d \vert}}{2} = \alpha \pm j\beta$$

### 3.3 Detailed KTU Worked Example

**Problem:** Consider the nonlinear system:
$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2$$
$$\dot{x}_2 = -x_1 - x_2 - x_1^2$$

**Step 1:** Find singular points by setting $\dot{x}_1 = \dot{x}_2 = 0$.

From $\dot{x}_1 = 0$: $x_2 = 0$.
Substituting into $\dot{x}_2 = 0$: $-x_1 - 0 - x_1^2 = 0 \Rightarrow x_1(1 + x_1) = 0$.

Thus, $x_1 = 0$ or $x_1 = -1$.

The singular points are:

$$\boxed{(0, 0) \quad \text{and} \quad (-1, 0)}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the Jacobian matrix.

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} & \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_2} \\ \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_1} & \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 - 2x_1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate Jacobian at $(0, 0)$.

$$A_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Trace: $T_1 = 0 + (-1) = -1$.
Determinant: $\Delta_1 = (0)(-1) - (1)(-1) = 1$.
Discriminant: $\Delta_{d,1} = (-1)^2 - 4(1) = -3 < 0$.

Since $T < 0$, $\Delta > 0$, and $\Delta_d < 0$, the singular point $(0, 0)$ is a **Stable Focus (Spiral Point)**.

**Step 4:** Evaluate Jacobian at $(-1, 0)$.

$$A_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 - 2(-1) & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Trace: $T_2 = 0 + (-1) = -1$.
Determinant: $\Delta_2 = (0)(-1) - (1)(1) = -1 < 0$.

Since $\Delta < 0$, the singular point $(-1, 0)$ is a **Saddle Point** (always unstable).

**Step 5:** Eigenvalues of $A_2$ explicitly.

$$\lambda = \frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{(-1)^2 - 4(-1)}}{2} = \frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2}$$

$$\lambda_1 = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 0.618 > 0$$

$$\lambda_2 = \frac{-1 - \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx -1.618 < 0$$

Opposite signs → confirmed **Saddle Point**.

### 3.4 Energy Perspective — Center vs. Focus

For an undamped linear oscillator:

$$\ddot{x} + \omega^2 x = 0 \Rightarrow \text{State form: } \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\omega^2 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Here, $T = 0$, $\Delta = \omega^2 > 0$, so it is a **Center**. The total energy is conserved.

Adding damping $\dot{x}$ changes the $(2,2)$ entry to $-2\zeta\omega$, making $T < 0$, transforming the center into a **Stable Focus**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Compute Jacobian A at Singular Point] --> Calc[Calculate Trace T and Determinant Delta]
    Calc --> CheckD{Delta Sign?}
    CheckD -- Delta less than 0 --> Saddle[Saddle Point - Always Unstable]
    CheckD -- Delta greater than 0 --> CheckDisc{Discriminant T squared minus 4 Delta}
    CheckD -- Delta equals 0 --> Degenerate[Degenerate or Non-isolated case]
    CheckDisc -- T squared minus 4 Delta greater than 0 --> RealCase[Real Eigenvalues]
    CheckDisc -- T squared minus 4 Delta less than 0 --> ComplexCase[Complex Eigenvalues]
    CheckDisc -- T squared minus 4 Delta equals 0 --> EqualCase[Equal Real Eigenvalues]
    RealCase --> NodeCheck{Sign of T}
    NodeCheck -- T less than 0 --> StableNode[Stable Node]
    NodeCheck -- T greater than 0 --> UnstableNode[Unstable Node]
    ComplexCase --> FocusCheck{Sign of T}
    FocusCheck -- T less than 0 --> StableFocus[Stable Focus Spiral]
    FocusCheck -- T greater than 0 --> UnstableFocus[Unstable Focus Spiral]
    FocusCheck -- T equals 0 --> Center[Center Vortex Marginally Stable]
    EqualCase --> EqualCheck{Sign of T}
    EqualCheck -- T less than 0 --> StarStable[Stable Star Node]
    EqualCheck -- T greater than 0 --> StarUnstable[Unstable Star Node]
```

### 4.2 Parametric Plane Map (Trace vs Determinant)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Quadrant_Analysis
        direction TB
        TopLeft["Delta less than 0: Saddle Point - Entire T-axis region below the parabola is SADDLE"]
        TopRight["Delta greater than 0 and T greater than 0: Unstable Region - Unstable Node if real, Unstable Focus if complex"]
        BotRight["Delta greater than 0 and T less than 0: Stable Region - Stable Node if real, Stable Focus if complex"]
        TopAxis["T-axis Delta equals 0: Degenerate boundary"]
        Parabola["Parabolic curve Delta equals T squared divided by 4: Separates real from complex eigenvalues"]
    end
```

### 4.3 Phase Portrait Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PP[Phase Portrait Behavior] --> SP{Singular Point Type}
    SP -- Stable Node --> SN[All trajectories converge to point - no rotation - monotonic decay]
    SP -- Unstable Node --> USN[All trajectories diverge from point - monotonic growth]
    SP -- Saddle Point --> SD[Trajectories approach along stable manifold and depart along unstable manifold]
    SP -- Stable Focus --> SF[Spiral inward toward point - damped oscillation]
    SP -- Unstable Focus --> UF[Spiral outward from point - growing oscillation]
    SP -- Center --> CT[Closed elliptical orbits - sustained oscillation]
    SP -- Star Node --> STN[Radial straight line trajectories]
```

### 4.4 Block Architecture — Singular Point Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InputBlock[Nonlinear System Equations] --> JacobianBlock[Step 1 Compute Jacobian J]
    JacobianBlock --> EquilibriumBlock[Step 2 Solve for Singular Points]
    EquilibriumBlock --> LinearizeBlock[Step 3 Evaluate J at Each Point]
    LinearizeBlock --> CharEqBlock[Step 4 Form Characteristic Equation]
    CharEqBlock --> EigenBlock[Step 5 Solve for Eigenvalues]
    EigenBlock --> ClassifyBlock[Step 6 Apply Classification Rules]
    ClassifyBlock --> StabilityBlock[Step 7 Determine Local Stability]
    StabilityBlock --> OutputBlock[Step 8 Sketch Phase Portrait and Report]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define the term "singular point" in the context of phase plane analysis. Mention any two classifications of singular points.*

**Model Answer:**
A **singular point** (also called an equilibrium point) of a second-order autonomous system is a point in the phase plane where the system can remain at rest indefinitely, i.e., where $\dot{x}_1 = f_1(x_1, x_2) = 0$ and $\dot{x}_2 = f_2(x_1, x_2) = 0$ simultaneously.

**Two classifications:**
1. **Stable Node** — Both eigenvalues are real, negative, and distinct. Trajectories converge to the singular point without oscillation.
2. **Saddle Point** — Eigenvalues are real with opposite signs. Always unstable.

*(Valid alternatives: Stable Focus, Center, Unstable Node, Unstable Focus, etc.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*For the linear system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x}$ with $A = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 1 \\ 0 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$, classify the singular point at the origin.*

**Model Answer:**
Compute trace and determinant:

$$T = -2 + (-3) = -5$$

$$\Delta = (-2)(-3) - (1)(0) = 6$$

$$\Delta_d = T^2 - 4\Delta = 25 - 24 = 1 > 0$$

Since $\Delta > 0$, $T < 0$, and $\Delta_d > 0$, the eigenvalues are real, distinct, and both negative (eigenvalues are $-2$ and $-3$).

**Classification: Stable Node.**

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - July 2024]`

**(a)** *For the nonlinear system:*
$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2$$
$$\dot{x}_2 = -x_1 + x_1^3 - x_2$$
*find all singular points, linearize the system about each, and classify them using eigenvalue analysis.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Sketch the phase portrait near each singular point and comment on the global stability of the system.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step 1: Finding Singular Points**

Setting $\dot{x}_1 = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = 0$.
Setting $\dot{x}_2 = 0 \Rightarrow -x_1 + x_1^3 - x_2 = 0 \Rightarrow -x_1 + x_1^3 = 0 \Rightarrow x_1(x_1^2 - 1) = 0$.

Solutions: $x_1 = 0, +1, -1$.

**Three singular points:** $(0, 0), (+1, 0), (-1, 0)$.

**Step 2: Jacobian Matrix**

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 + 3x_1^2 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: At $(0, 0)$:**

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$T = -1, \Delta = 1, \Delta_d = 1 - 4 = -3 < 0$.

Since $T < 0, \Delta > 0, \Delta_d < 0$ → **Stable Focus (Spiral Point)**.

**Step 4: At $(1, 0)$:**

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 + 3 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 2 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$T = -1, \Delta = -2 < 0$ → **Saddle Point** (always unstable).

**Step 5: At $(-1, 0)$:**

By symmetry: $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 2 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$ (since $(-1)^2 = 1$).

$T = -1, \Delta = -2 < 0$ → **Saddle Point** (always unstable).

**(b) Sketch and Global Stability:**

```
       x2
        |    SPIRAL IN
        |   /
   *    |  /    * (1,0) Saddle
(-1,0)  | /     /
Saddle  |/_____/________ x1
       /|
      / |
     /  |  SPIRAL IN
        |
        |  
```
- Trajectories spiral inward to $(0,0)$ from a finite region around the origin.
- $(+1, 0)$ and $(-1, 0)$ are saddle points whose stable manifolds form the **separatrices** dividing basins.
- The **basin of attraction of $(0,0)$** is bounded by these separatrices.
- Globally, the system has a **bounded stable region** around origin; large perturbations cause divergence.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** *Consider the Van der Pol equation:*
$$\ddot{x} - \mu(1 - x^2)\dot{x} + x = 0$$
*Write it in state-space form, find singular points, and classify them for $\mu = 0.5$ and $\mu = 2$.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Explain with a phase portrait sketch the difference between a stable focus and a stable node. Comment on what parameter $\mu$ controls in the system.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step 1: State-Space Form**

Let $x_1 = x, x_2 = \dot{x}$:

$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2$$
$$\dot{x}_2 = -x_1 + \mu(1 - x_1^2)x_2$$

**Step 2: Singular Points**

$x_2 = 0$, and $-x_1 + \mu(1 - x_1^2)(0) = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 = 0$.

**Only singular point: $(0, 0)$.**

**Step 3: Jacobian**

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 - 2\mu x_1 x_2 & \mu(1 - x_1^2) \end{bmatrix}$$

At $(0, 0)$:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & \mu \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: For $\mu = 0.5$:**

$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$

$T = 0.5, \Delta = 1, \Delta_d = 0.25 - 4 = -3.75 < 0$.

Since $T > 0, \Delta > 0, \Delta_d < 0$ → **Unstable Focus**.

Eigenvalues: $\lambda = \frac{0.5 \pm \sqrt{-3.75}}{2} = 0.25 \pm j0.968$.

**Step 5: For $\mu = 2$:**

$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$

$T = 2, \Delta = 1, \Delta_d = 4 - 4 = 0$.

Eigenvalues: $\lambda = \frac{2}{2} = 1$ (repeated, positive).

$\Delta_d = 0, T > 0$ → **Unstable Star/Proper Node**.

**(b) Phase Portrait Distinction:**

| Feature | Stable Focus | Stable Node |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Eigenvalues | Complex with negative real part | Real, negative, distinct |
| Trajectory shape | Spirals inward | Curves monotonically inward |
| Rotation | Yes (oscillation) | No rotation |
| Linear systems analogy | $\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega\dot{x} + \omega^2 x = 0$ with $\zeta < 1$ | $\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega\dot{x} + \omega^2 x = 0$ with $\zeta > 1$ |

**Physical interpretation of $\mu$:**

- $\mu$ is the **nonlinear damping coefficient**.
- Small $\mu$ → light damping → trajectories spiral (focus).
- Large $\mu$ → heavy damping → trajectories approach monotonically (node).
- The Van der Pol oscillator exhibits a **limit cycle** (sustained oscillation) for $\mu > 0$, which the linearization cannot capture; nonlinear analysis is essential.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Most common error:** Forgetting to compute $\Delta_d = T^2 - 4\Delta$ before classifying. Students often jump to "stable node" if $T < 0$ and $\Delta > 0$ — this is WRONG if $\Delta_d < 0$ (it's actually a stable focus).
> 2. **Sign mistake:** Mixing up saddle and node. **Memory aid:** If $\Delta < 0$, eigenvalues have opposite signs → **Saddle**, always.
> 3. **Nonlinear reminder:** Classification is **local** — only valid in a small neighborhood. Global behavior may differ (limit cycles, multiple basins).
> 4. **Pure imaginary:** When $T = 0$ and $\Delta > 0$, it is a **Center**, NOT a stable focus. Do not be tricked.
> 5. **Valuation tip (2 marks lost per error):** Always show the **eigenvalue calculation explicitly**; do not just state the type.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Definition:** Singular point = equilibrium where all derivatives vanish simultaneously.
- ✅ **Linearization:** Use Jacobian matrix evaluated at the singular point.
- ✅ **Three classifiers:** Trace $T$, Determinant $\Delta$, Discriminant $\Delta_d = T^2 - 4\Delta$.
- ✅ **Mnemonic Map:** Parabola $\Delta = T^2/4$ on the $T$-$\Delta$ plane separates complex (inside) from real (outside) eigenvalues.
- ✅ **Stability Rule:** $T < 0$ AND $\Delta > 0$ implies asymptotic stability (node or focus).
- ✅ **Saddle Point:** Always $\Delta < 0$, always unstable — instant classification.
- ✅ **Center:** $T = 0$ exactly, $\Delta > 0$ — conservative systems (no damping).
- ✅ **Equal eigenvalues:** $\Delta_d = 0$; check $T$ sign for star/proper node.
- ✅ **Local vs Global:** Classification is local. Phase portraits can have limit cycles and separatrices globally.
- ✅ **Van der Pol, Pendulum, Relay systems** are classic KTU favorites using this analysis.
- ✅ **Bifurcation Note:** When trace/determinant cross critical thresholds (e.g., $\Delta = 0$), qualitative behavior changes — this is a **bifurcation**.
- ✅ **Always check** both singular points in symmetric systems ($-1, 0$ and $+1, 0$ often appear).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition of Stability: Asymptotic Stability and Instability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT636 - ADVANCED CONTROL THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Definition of Stability: Asymptotic Stability and Instability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to System Stability

In the study of nonlinear and linear control systems, **Stability** is the most fundamental property an engineer must guarantee before deploying a system in the real world. An unstable system is, by definition, useless — no matter how accurate or fast it is. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020)**, the formal definitions introduced by the Russian mathematician **Aleksandr Mikhailovich Lyapunov (1892)** form the bedrock of Module 4.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of PEEIT636 explicitly begins with the *Definition of Stability*. The student is expected to write the exact Lyapunov $\epsilon$-$\delta$ definitions verbatim in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE). Memorize the precise mathematical statements.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definition — Stability in the Sense of Lyapunov

Consider an unforced (autonomous) nonlinear system described by the state equation:

$$
\dot{x}(t) = f(x(t), t)
$$

with an initial condition $x(t_0) = x_0$, and let $x_e = 0$ be an equilibrium state (i.e., $f(x_e, t) = 0$ for all $t$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Lyapunov's Definition of Stability (Internal / Lyapunov Stability):**
>
> The equilibrium state $x_e = 0$ is said to be **stable** (in the sense of Lyapunov) if, for every real number $\epsilon > 0$, there exists a real number $\delta(\epsilon, t_0) > 0$ such that every solution $x(t)$ starting from within the ball $\Vert x(t_0) \Vert < \delta$ remains inside the ball $\Vert x(t) \Vert < \epsilon$ for all $t \geq t_0$.
>
> In symbols:
> $$ \forall\, \epsilon > 0,\; \exists\, \delta > 0 \;\; \text{such that} \;\; \Vert x(0) \Vert < \delta \;\Rightarrow\; \Vert x(t) \Vert < \epsilon,\;\; \forall t \geq 0 $$

**Interpretation in plain English:** If you start the system *close enough* to the equilibrium, you can guarantee that the trajectory will *stay close* to that equilibrium forever. It does **not** require the trajectory to return to the equilibrium — it only requires that it doesn't wander far away.

---

## 1.3 Formal Definition — Asymptotic Stability

> [!NOTE]
> **Lyapunov's Definition of Asymptotic Stability:**
>
> An equilibrium state $x_e = 0$ is **asymptotically stable** if:
> 1. It is stable in the sense of Lyapunov (as defined above), **AND**
> 2. There exists a $\delta > 0$ such that $\Vert x(0) \Vert < \delta$ implies
> $$ \lim_{t \to \infty} \Vert x(t) \Vert = 0 $$
> In other words, every trajectory starting within the ball of radius $\delta$ converges to the origin as $t \to \infty$.

**Plain English:** The system must *not only* stay near the equilibrium, it must actually *return* to it as time progresses. Asymptotic stability is the property required in **almost every practical control design** (missile guidance, robotic arms, aircraft autopilots, etc.).

A stronger version often asked in KTU exams:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Asymptotic Stability in the Large (Global Asymptotic Stability):** If asymptotic stability holds for *all* initial states (i.e., $\delta \to \infty$), then the equilibrium is called **globally asymptotically stable**. This is the gold standard of stability and is required when the initial condition of the plant is unknown.

---

## 1.4 Formal Definition — Instability

> [!NOTE]
> **Lyapunov's Definition of Instability:**
>
> An equilibrium state $x_e = 0$ is **unstable** if it is *not stable*. Equivalently, there exists some $\epsilon > 0$ for which no $\delta > 0$ can be found that keeps the trajectory bounded within $\epsilon$. In symbols:
> $$ \exists\, \epsilon > 0,\; \forall\, \delta > 0,\;\; \text{there exists a solution with } \Vert x(0) \Vert < \delta \text{ but } \Vert x(t) \Vert \geq \epsilon \text{ for some } t \geq 0 $$

---

## 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — The Three Hills

Imagine a ball placed on a surface. The shape of the surface and the friction determine stability.

| Surface Type | Physical Behavior | Control-Theory Name |
|---|---|---|
| **Flat horizontal floor** | Ball displaced → stays put | **Stable** (Lyapunov Stable) |
| **Valley / Bowl** | Ball displaced → rolls back to bottom | **Asymptotically Stable** |
| **Hilltop / Inverted dome** | Ball displaced → rolls away forever | **Unstable** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "Define stability" in 3 marks, draw the three curves (valley, flat, hill) and label them. Examiners reward diagrams generously. Pair it with the $\epsilon$-$\delta$ statement for full marks.

---

## 1.6 Graphical Phase-Plane Picture

For a 2nd-order system $\dot{x} = Ax$, the equilibrium at the origin behaves as follows:

- **Asymptotically stable** → All trajectories spiral **inward** (or converge directly) to the origin.
- **Stable (Lyapunov only)** → Trajectories form **closed orbits** (limit cycles) around the origin.
- **Unstable** → Trajectories spiral **outward** away from the origin.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breaking Down the $\epsilon$-$\delta$ Logic

The $\epsilon$-$\delta$ definition is logically structured. KTU examiners often test whether students can write this in the *correct order*.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Choose a tolerance** $\epsilon$ (how close to equilibrium you want to stay, e.g., $\epsilon = 0.1$).
2. **Prove there exists a starting radius** $\delta$ (how close you must begin) such that the condition is met.
3. The $\delta$ is allowed to **depend on** $\epsilon$ — written $\delta(\epsilon)$.
4. For *time-varying* (non-autonomous) systems, $\delta$ may also depend on the initial time $t_0$, written $\delta(\epsilon, t_0)$.
5. If $\delta$ does *not* depend on $t_0$ → the system is **uniformly stable**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Two Conditions of Asymptotic Stability — KTU Hot Question:**
> Examiners love to ask: *"Is asymptotic stability and Lyapunov stability the same?"* The answer is **NO**. Asymptotic stability is a *strictly stronger* condition. It requires *both* (a) Lyapunov stability *and* (b) convergence to zero. A Lyapunov stable limit cycle is *not* asymptotically stable.

---

## 2.2 Stability for Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) Systems

For the linear system $\dot{x} = Ax$, the stability classification can be determined purely from the **eigenvalues** $\lambda_i$ of the matrix $A$. This is a high-yield topic for KTU numerical problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Linear System Stability Rules (Eigenvalue Test):**
>
> Let $\lambda_i = \sigma_i + j\omega_i$ be the eigenvalues of $A$.
> - **Asymptotically Stable** ⇔ $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) = \sigma_i < 0$ for **all** $i$.
> - **Marginally / Lyapunov Stable** ⇔ $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) \leq 0$ for all $i$ AND any eigenvalue with $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) = 0$ is **simple** (algebraic multiplicity = 1, geometric multiplicity = 1).
> - **Unstable** ⇔ At least one eigenvalue has $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) > 0$, OR a zero-real-part eigenvalue is **repeated**.

The free response of a linear system is:

$$
x(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i v_i e^{\lambda_i t}
$$

where $v_i$ are eigenvectors and $c_i$ are constants determined by initial conditions. The exponential term $e^{\lambda_i t}$ governs convergence/divergence.

- If $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$ → $e^{\lambda_i t} \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$ → decay.
- If $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) > 0$ → $e^{\lambda_i t} \to \infty$ → explosion (unstable).
- If $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) = 0$ (simple) → $e^{j\omega t}$ → bounded oscillation (marginally stable).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Bookmark this table — these formulas are asked every year in KTU ESE.

| # | Concept | Mathematical Statement | Condition / Key Insight |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lyapunov Stability (Autonomous) | $\forall \epsilon>0,\;\exists \delta>0 : \Vert x(0)\Vert<\delta \Rightarrow \Vert x(t)\Vert<\epsilon,\; \forall t\geq 0$ | Trajectory stays in $\epsilon$-ball |
| 2 | Asymptotic Stability | Lyapunov stable $\;+\; \lim_{t\to\infty} \Vert x(t)\Vert = 0$ | Trajectory returns to origin |
| 3 | Global Asymptotic Stability | Asymptotic stability holds for **all** $x(0) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Holds for any initial condition |
| 4 | Instability | $\exists \epsilon>0,\;\forall \delta>0,\;\exists x(0):\Vert x(0)\Vert<\delta$ but $\Vert x(t)\Vert\geq \epsilon$ for some $t$ | Trajectory escapes every neighborhood |
| 5 | Eigenvalue Test (Asymptotic) | $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0,\;\forall i$ | Strictly left-half plane |
| 6 | Eigenvalue Test (Marginal) | $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) \leq 0$ and zero-part simple | Imaginary axis, no Jordan blocks of size $>1$ |
| 7 | Eigenvalue Test (Unstable) | $\exists i : \text{Re}(\lambda_i) > 0$ or repeated zero eigenvalue | At least one root in RHP |
| 8 | Linear Free Response | $x(t) = \sum c_i v_i e^{\lambda_i t}$ | Eigen-decomposition of $A$ |
| 9 | Exponential Bound | $\Vert x(t) \Vert \leq K \Vert x(0) \Vert e^{-\alpha t}$ for $\alpha>0$ | Consequence of asymptotic stability |
| 10 | Uniform Stability | $\delta$ independent of $t_0$ | Required for time-varying systems |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Aircraft Autopilot:** Asymptotic stability is mandatory. A neutrally stable (oscillatory) aircraft would oscillate forever and run out of fuel.
- **Inverted Pendulum on a Cart:** The upright equilibrium is *unstable* — even a tiny disturbance causes the pendulum to fall. This is the canonical example of a system that **needs** active control to become asymptotically stable.
- **Power Systems:** Synchronous generators must be asymptotically stable after a fault; otherwise, the grid collapses (e.g., the 2003 Northeast blackout).
- **Chemical Reactors:** Instability in a CSTR (Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor) can lead to thermal runaway and explosions.
- **Robotic Manipulators:** A robot arm holding a payload must be globally asymptotically stable at its commanded pose, regardless of the starting joint angles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stability Criterion from the State Transition Matrix

For a linear time-invariant system $\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t)$, the solution is given by the **state transition matrix** (matrix exponential):

$$
x(t) = e^{A(t-t_0)} x(t_0) = \Phi(t-t_0)\, x(t_0)
$$

We want to derive the condition for **asymptotic stability** rigorously.

**Step 1.** Assume $A$ is diagonalizable (or can be put in Jordan form). Write $A = V \Lambda V^{-1}$, where $\Lambda = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \dots, \lambda_n)$ and $V$ is the eigenvector matrix.

**Step 2.** The matrix exponential becomes:

$$
e^{At} = V e^{\Lambda t} V^{-1} = V \, \text{diag}\!\left(e^{\lambda_1 t},\, e^{\lambda_2 t},\, \dots,\, e^{\lambda_n t}\right) V^{-1}
$$

**Step 3.** Therefore, the state solution is:

$$
x(t) = V \, \text{diag}\!\left(e^{\lambda_1 t},\, e^{\lambda_2 t},\, \dots,\, e^{\lambda_n t}\right) V^{-1} x(0)
$$

**Step 4.** Take the limit as $t \to \infty$:

$$
\lim_{t \to \infty} x(t) = V \cdot \lim_{t \to \infty} \text{diag}\!\left(e^{\lambda_1 t},\, \dots,\, e^{\lambda_n t}\right) \cdot V^{-1} x(0)
$$

**Step 5.** For the limit to equal the zero vector (origin) for *any* initial condition $x(0) \in \mathbb{R}^n$, we need:

$$
\lim_{t \to \infty} e^{\lambda_i t} = 0 \quad \text{for every } i = 1, 2, \dots, n
$$

**Step 6.** This limit is zero if and only if:

$$
\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0 \quad \text{for all } i
$$

**Conclusion (Q.E.D.):** A linear system $\dot{x} = Ax$ is asymptotically stable **if and only if** every eigenvalue of $A$ has a strictly negative real part. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Classifying Stability from Eigenvalues

> **Problem (KTU Style):** Determine the stability of the equilibrium at the origin for the system $\dot{x} = Ax$ where
> $$ A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} $$

**Step 1: Compute the characteristic polynomial** $\det(\lambda I - A) = 0$.

$$
\det\!\begin{bmatrix} \lambda & -1 \\ 2 & \lambda+3 \end{bmatrix} = \lambda(\lambda+3) - (-1)(2) = \lambda^2 + 3\lambda + 2
$$

**Step 2: Solve the characteristic equation.**

$$
\lambda^2 + 3\lambda + 2 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; (\lambda+1)(\lambda+2) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda_1 = -1,\;\; \lambda_2 = -2
$$

**Step 3: Classify the equilibrium.**

Both eigenvalues satisfy $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$. Therefore, the origin is **asymptotically stable** (in fact, globally asymptotically stable since this is a linear system with a unique equilibrium).

**Step 4: State the trajectory behavior.**

Since eigenvalues are real, distinct, and negative, the origin is a **stable node**. Trajectories approach the origin along exponential curves without oscillation.

---

## 3.3 Counter-Example — Marginal Stability

> **Problem:** Classify stability for $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ (simple harmonic oscillator).

**Step 1: Characteristic equation.**

$$
\det(\lambda I - A) = \lambda^2 + 1 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda = \pm j
$$

**Step 2: Classification.**

Eigenvalues are purely imaginary with $\text{Re}(\lambda) = 0$. They are simple (algebraic multiplicity 1). Hence, the origin is **Lyapunov stable (marginally stable)** but **NOT asymptotically stable** (trajectories are closed orbits of radius $\Vert x(0) \Vert$, never converging to zero).

---

## 3.4 Counter-Example — Instability from a Repeated Zero Eigenvalue

> **Problem:** Classify stability for $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 1: Characteristic equation.**

$$
\det(\lambda I - A) = \lambda^2 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda_{1,2} = 0 \;\; \text{(repeated)}
$$

**Step 2: Classification.**

Although $\text{Re}(\lambda) = 0$, the eigenvalue is **repeated**. The system has a Jordan block of size 2. The solution contains a term $tv$, which grows unboundedly. Hence the system is **unstable**.

**Verification by direct simulation:** The system gives $\dot{x}_1 = x_2$, $\dot{x}_2 = 0$, so $x_2(t) = x_2(0) = \text{const}$, and $x_1(t) = x_1(0) + x_2(0) t$. As $t \to \infty$, $x_1(t) \to \infty$ if $x_2(0) \neq 0$. The trajectory escapes every neighborhood of the origin. Confirmed: **unstable**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flowchart for LTI Systems

$$
\text{Given } A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n} \Rightarrow \text{Compute eigenvalues } \{\lambda_i\}
$$

$$
\Downarrow
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Any } \text{Re}(\lambda_i) > 0 ? \;\;\;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \textbf{UNSTABLE (Stop)} \\
&\text{Else, all } \text{Re}(\lambda_i) \leq 0 \Rightarrow \text{Check eigenvalues with } \text{Re}(\lambda_i) = 0 \\
&\quad \text{Any zero-real-part eigenvalue with algebraic mult.} > 1 ? \;\;\;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \textbf{UNSTABLE} \\
&\quad \text{All zero-real-part eigenvalues are simple} \;\;\;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \textbf{MARGINALLY STABLE (Lyapunov stable only)} \\
&\text{All } \text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0 \;\;\;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \textbf{ASYMPTOTICALLY STABLE}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of Stability Classification

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the *decision logic* a control engineer (or an examiner's model answer) follows when classifying the stability of an equilibrium point.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Equilibrium xE = 0]) --> B{System Type?}
    B -- Linear dot x = A x --> C[Compute Eigenvalues lambda i of A]
    B -- Non linear dot x = f x t --> D[Use Lyapunov Second Method V x]

    C --> E{All Re lambda less than 0?}
    E -- Yes --> F[GLOBALLY ASYMPTOTICALLY STABLE]
    E -- No --> G{Any Re lambda greater than 0?}
    G -- Yes --> H[UNSTABLE]
    G -- No --> I{Zero real part eigenvalues?}
    I -- None --> F
    I -- Simple root --> J[MARGINALLY STABLE Lyapunov Stable Only]
    I -- Repeated root --> H

    D --> K{dV dt negative definite?}
    K -- Yes --> F
    K -- Only negative semi definite plus invariance --> J
    K -- Positive definite --> H
```

---

## 4.2 Ball-on-Surface Analogy (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StableType [STABLE LYAPUNOV]
        direction TB
        S1[Flat floor]
        S2[Ball displaced, stays put]
    end

    subgraph AsympType [ASYMPTOTICALLY STABLE]
        direction TB
        A1[Valley or bowl shape]
        A2[Ball returns to bottom]
    end

    subgraph UnstableType [UNSTABLE]
        direction TB
        U1[Hilltop or inverted dome]
        U2[Ball rolls away forever]
    end

    StableType --> AsympType
    AsympType --> UnstableType
```

---

## 4.3 Phase Portrait — Three Canonical 2nd-Order Cases

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph StableNode [STABLE NODE lambda 1,2 less than 0]
        SN1[Trajectories approach origin monotonically]
    end

    subgraph StableSpiral [STABLE FOCUS complex lambda with neg real part]
        SS1[Trajectories spiral inward]
    end

    subgraph Center [CENTER pure imaginary lambda]
        C1[Closed orbits around origin]
    end

    subgraph UnstableSpiral [UNSTABLE FOCUS complex lambda with pos real part]
        US1[Trajectories spiral outward]
    end

    subgraph SaddlePoint [SADDLE real lambda of opposite signs]
        SP1[Trajectories diverge along unstable manifold]
    end

    StableNode --> StableSpiral --> Center --> UnstableSpiral --> SaddlePoint
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip (5-Mark Question):** When asked to "discuss stability," reproduce any of the three diagrams above. A 30-second sketch adds **2–3 extra marks** under the KTU marking scheme's "presentation" criterion.

---

## 4.4 Mapping of $\epsilon$-$\delta$ Definition (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E[Choose Epsilon greater than 0] --> D[Find Delta greater than 0 depending on Epsilon]
    D --> IC[Initial state inside Delta ball]
    IC --> EV[Trajectory stays inside Epsilon ball for all t greater than or equal to 0]
    EV --> AS{Does trajectory return to origin?}
    AS -- No --> LS[Lyapunov Stable]
    AS -- Yes --> ASY[Asymptotically Stable]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the following terms with reference to Lyapunov stability of nonlinear systems:**
(i) Stability in the sense of Lyapunov, (ii) Asymptotic stability, (iii) Instability.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Stability:** The equilibrium $x_e = 0$ is said to be *stable* if for every $\epsilon > 0$, there exists a $\delta(\epsilon) > 0$ such that $\Vert x(0) \Vert < \delta \Rightarrow \Vert x(t) \Vert < \epsilon$ for all $t \geq 0$. The trajectory must stay close to the equilibrium if it starts close.

**(ii) Asymptotic Stability:** An equilibrium is *asymptotically stable* if (a) it is stable in the sense of Lyapunov, **and** (b) $\lim_{t \to \infty} \Vert x(t) \Vert = 0$ for all initial conditions within a small neighbourhood $\delta$. The trajectory must return to the origin as time progresses.

**(iii) Instability:** An equilibrium is *unstable* if it is not stable, i.e., there exists an $\epsilon > 0$ such that no $\delta > 0$ can keep the trajectory inside the $\epsilon$-ball for all time.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**State and explain the conditions for asymptotic stability of a linear time-invariant system $\dot{x} = Ax$ in terms of the eigenvalues of $A$.**

**Model Answer:**

Let $\lambda_i = \sigma_i + j\omega_i$ denote the eigenvalues of $A$. The equilibrium at the origin of $\dot{x} = Ax$ is **asymptotically stable** if and only if:

$$
\text{Re}(\lambda_i) = \sigma_i < 0 \quad \text{for every } i = 1, 2, \dots, n
$$

Equivalently, all eigenvalues must lie strictly in the open left half of the complex plane (LHP). This is a necessary and sufficient condition. The system is *Lyapunov (marginally) stable* if $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) \leq 0$ with any zero-real-part eigenvalue being simple. The system is *unstable* if at least one eigenvalue has $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) > 0$ or a zero eigenvalue is repeated.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define stability, asymptotic stability, and instability in the sense of Lyapunov. State the $\epsilon$-$\delta$ condition for each. Illustrate each type with a physical example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the linear system $\dot{x} = Ax$ with $A = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 2 \\ -1 & -4 \end{bmatrix}$, determine the stability classification of the equilibrium at the origin. Justify your answer using the eigenvalue test. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**1. Stability (Lyapunov):** The equilibrium $x_e = 0$ is stable if $\forall \epsilon > 0,\; \exists \delta(\epsilon) > 0$ such that

$$
\Vert x(0) \Vert < \delta \;\Rightarrow\; \Vert x(t) \Vert < \epsilon,\;\; \forall t \geq 0
$$

*Physical example:* A ball placed on a flat horizontal floor. Displace it slightly and it remains at the displaced position. It does not return to the original point, but it does not escape either.

**2. Asymptotic Stability:** The equilibrium is asymptotically stable if (i) it is stable in the sense of Lyapunov, and (ii)

$$
\lim_{t \to \infty} \Vert x(t) \Vert = 0
$$

*Physical example:* A ball placed at the bottom of a parabolic bowl. Any small displacement causes the ball to roll back to the bottom equilibrium. The trajectory converges to the origin.

**3. Instability:** The equilibrium is unstable if it is not stable, i.e.,

$$
\exists \epsilon > 0,\; \forall \delta > 0,\; \exists x(0):\Vert x(0)\Vert<\delta \text{ and } \Vert x(t_1)\Vert \geq \epsilon
$$

*Physical example:* A ball balanced on top of an inverted dome (hill). The smallest disturbance causes it to roll away. The upright equilibrium of an inverted pendulum is also unstable.

> **[Marks Distribution Hint]:** 2 marks per definition + 1 mark for example = 7 marks.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Characteristic equation** $\det(\lambda I - A) = 0$.

$$
\det\!\begin{bmatrix} \lambda+1 & -2 \\ 1 & \lambda+4 \end{bmatrix} = (\lambda+1)(\lambda+4) - (-2)(1) = \lambda^2 + 5\lambda + 4 + 2 = \lambda^2 + 5\lambda + 6
$$

**Step 2: Solve the quadratic.**

$$
\lambda^2 + 5\lambda + 6 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; (\lambda+2)(\lambda+3) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda_1 = -2,\;\; \lambda_2 = -3
$$

**Step 3: Apply the eigenvalue stability test.**

Both eigenvalues satisfy $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$ strictly. Therefore, by the LTI stability theorem, the equilibrium at the origin is **asymptotically stable**.

**Step 4: Sub-classification (Bonus for full marks).**

The eigenvalues are real, distinct, and negative. The origin is therefore a **stable node** in the phase plane. Trajectories approach the origin along exponential curves without any oscillation.

> **Conclusion:** Origin is **globally asymptotically stable** (a stable node).

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the $\epsilon$-$\delta$ definition of stability. Differentiate between Lyapunov stability, asymptotic stability, and global asymptotic stability. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For each of the following systems, determine the type of stability of the origin using the eigenvalue test. Show all steps.
**(i)** $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -4 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ **(ii)** $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$ **(iii)** $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**The $\epsilon$-$\delta$ Definition:** Consider the autonomous system $\dot{x} = f(x)$ with equilibrium at $x_e = 0$. The equilibrium is *stable* if for any chosen tolerance $\epsilon > 0$ (radius of a small ball around the origin), there exists a starting radius $\delta(\epsilon) > 0$ such that **any** trajectory starting inside the $\delta$-ball never leaves the $\epsilon$-ball for all future time.

**Differences:**

- **Lyapunov Stable:** Trajectory *stays bounded* near the equilibrium but does **not** return. Example: mass-spring system with no damping (oscillates forever).
- **Asymptotically Stable:** Trajectory stays near **AND** returns to the origin as $t \to \infty$. Example: damped mass-spring system.
- **Globally Asymptotically Stable:** Asymptotic stability holds for **any** initial condition $x(0) \in \mathbb{R}^n$, not just those in a small neighbourhood. Required for systems with unknown initial states.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**System (i):** $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -4 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$

Characteristic equation:

$$
\det(\lambda I - A) = \lambda^2 + 4 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda = \pm 2j
$$

Eigenvalues are purely imaginary, simple, and have $\text{Re}(\lambda) = 0$. → **Lyapunov Stable (Marginally Stable)**, NOT asymptotically stable.

**System (ii):** $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$

Eigenvalues are $\lambda_1 = 1$, $\lambda_2 = -2$. Since $\lambda_1 = +1$ has $\text{Re}(\lambda_1) > 0$. → **Unstable** (saddle point).

**System (iii):** $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$

Characteristic equation:

$$
\det(\lambda I - A) = \lambda^2 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda_{1,2} = 0 \;\; \text{(repeated, algebraic multiplicity 2)}
$$

Eigenvalue is on the imaginary axis **and** is repeated. → **Unstable** (solution contains a linear-in-time term $tv$).

> **Summary Table for the Three Systems:**

| System | Eigenvalues | Stability |
|---|---|---|
| (i) | $0 \pm 2j$ | Marginally Stable (Lyapunov only) |
| (ii) | $+1,\,-2$ | Unstable (Saddle) |
| (iii) | $0,\,0$ (repeated) | Unstable (Jordan block) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "stable" with "asymptotically stable":** Many students write the same $\epsilon$-$\delta$ statement for both. The examiner *will* deduct 2 marks. Asymptotic stability **requires** the limit-to-zero condition as an *additional* requirement.
> 2. **Forgetting the Jordan block exception for marginal stability:** A repeated eigenvalue on the imaginary axis (e.g., $\lambda = 0$ with algebraic multiplicity 2) is **NOT** Lyapunov stable. Students often wrongly classify it as "marginally stable" because $\text{Re}(\lambda) \leq 0$. Always check the **algebraic multiplicity** of eigenvalues on the imaginary axis.
> 3. **Writing "globally asymptotically stable" without justification:** Global asymptotic stability is a *very* strong claim. For a linear system, you can only state it if the origin is the *unique* equilibrium. State this explicitly.
> 4. **Miswriting the $\delta$ dependence:** The $\delta$ is a function of $\epsilon$ — write $\delta(\epsilon)$, not $\delta$. A common 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Not mentioning the time-dependence for time-varying systems:** For non-autonomous systems, $\delta$ depends on $t_0$ as well: $\delta(\epsilon, t_0)$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Read 5 Minutes Before the Exam:**

- ☐ **Stability (Lyapunov):** $\epsilon$-$\delta$ definition; trajectory *stays close*.
- ☐ **Asymptotic Stability:** Lyapunov stable **PLUS** $\lim_{t \to \infty} \Vert x(t) \Vert = 0$.
- ☐ **Global Asymptotic Stability:** Asymptotic stability for **all** $x(0) \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
- ☐ **Instability:** Failure of the $\epsilon$-$\delta$ condition; trajectory escapes.
- ☐ **LTI Asymptotic Stability Criterion:** All eigenvalues of $A$ have **strictly negative** real parts.
- ☐ **LTI Marginal/Lyapunov Stability Criterion:** All eigenvalues have $\text{Re}(\lambda) \leq 0$ AND any eigenvalue on imaginary axis is **simple**.
- ☐ **LTI Instability Criterion:** At least one eigenvalue has $\text{Re}(\lambda) > 0$ OR a zero-real-part eigenvalue is **repeated** (Jordan block).
- ☐ **Free response form:** $x(t) = \sum c_i v_i e^{\lambda_i t}$ — convergence depends on $\text{Re}(\lambda_i)$.
- ☐ **Physical analogies:** Flat floor = stable; valley = asymptotically stable; hilltop = unstable.
- ☐ **Phase portrait cues:** Spiral-in = asymptotically stable; closed orbit = marginally stable; spiral-out or saddle = unstable.
- ☐ **Uniform stability:** $\delta$ independent of $t_0$ (relevant for time-varying systems).
- ☐ **Exponential bound** for asymptotically stable linear systems: $\Vert x(t) \Vert \leq K \Vert x(0) \Vert e^{-\alpha t}$ for some $K, \alpha > 0$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Liapunov Methods for Stability of Linear and Nonlinear Continuous Time Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT636 - ADVANCED CONTROL THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Liapunov Methods for Stability of Linear and Nonlinear Continuous Time Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Liapunov Stability Analysis** is a generalized mathematical framework developed by the Russian mathematician **Aleksandr Mikhailovich Liapunov (1892)** for determining the stability of dynamical systems — both linear and nonlinear — without requiring an explicit closed-form solution of the system state equations. The method evaluates stability by constructing a scalar **energy-like function** $V(x)$ and observing how it evolves along the system trajectories.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Liapunov's method is universally applicable — it works for **linear**, **nonlinear**, **time-varying**, and **time-invariant** systems. KTU examiners specifically test both (a) stability definitions, and (b) the constructive procedure for finding $V(x)$.

Mathematically, consider the autonomous continuous-time system:

$$
\dot{x}(t) = f(x(t))
$$

where $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the state vector and $f: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^n$. We seek conditions guaranteeing that the **equilibrium state at the origin** $x_e = 0$ is stable, asymptotically stable, or unstable.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rolling Ball" Intuition

Imagine a smooth bowl-shaped valley. A ball placed at the **bottom of the bowl** has zero velocity and zero displacement — this is the **equilibrium state** (origin). 

- If you gently push the ball, it **rolls back to the bottom** → The system is **asymptotically stable** (the bowl acts like a *dissipative* energy field).
- If you place the ball on a **flat table** and push it, it rolls forever without returning → **Marginal stability** (energy is conserved).
- If you place the ball **on top of a hill** → Any tiny push makes it roll away forever → The system is **unstable**.

**Liapunov's brilliant insight:** Instead of solving the complex differential equations of the ball's motion, simply **monitor the total energy** of the system. If the energy keeps decreasing and reaches zero (the bottom of the bowl), the system is stable. This scalar energy function is the **Liapunov function $V(x)$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** A Liapunov function is a *generalized energy function*. Real physical energy (kinetic + potential) is one example, but mathematically any scalar, positive-definite, continuously differentiable function with a decreasing derivative along trajectories will do.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Property |
|---|---|---|
| $V(x)$ | Liapunov candidate function | Scalar, $V(0) = 0$ |
| $\dot{V}(x)$ | Time derivative along trajectory | Decides stability type |
| $x_e = 0$ | Equilibrium point under analysis | Assumed without loss of generality |
| $P, Q$ | Symmetric positive definite matrices | Used in linear-system construction |

> [!TIP]
> For an **autonomous** system, the equilibrium is at $f(0) = 0$. The KTU 2024 convention assumes the origin is the equilibrium — coordinate translation is used to enforce this assumption for any arbitrary equilibrium.

## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Stability Definitions in the Liapunov Sense

For the system $\dot{x} = f(x)$ with equilibrium at $x_e = 0$:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition Set (Memorize Word-for-Word):**

**(a) Stable (in Liapunov sense):** For every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists $\delta(\varepsilon) > 0$ such that if $\|x(0)\| < \delta$, then $\|x(t)\| < \varepsilon$ for all $t \geq 0$.

**(b) Asymptotically Stable:** The equilibrium is stable, AND $\lim_{t \to \infty} \|x(t)\| = 0$ for all $\|x(0)\| < \delta$.

**(c) Exponentially Stable:** Asymptotically stable AND $\|x(t)\| \leq k \cdot \|x(0)\| \cdot e^{-\alpha t}$ for some $k, \alpha > 0$.

**(d) Unstable:** NOT stable — i.e., there exists some $\varepsilon > 0$ such that for any $\delta > 0$, the trajectory eventually leaves the $\varepsilon$-ball.

**(e) Globally Asymptotically Stable (GAS):** Asymptotically stable for **all initial conditions** in $\mathbb{R}^n$.

---

### 2.2 Definiteness of Scalar Functions

Let $V(x): \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a continuously differentiable scalar function with $V(0) = 0$.

- **Positive Definite (PD):** $V(x) > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$. Notation: $V(x) > 0$.
- **Positive Semi-Definite (PSD):** $V(x) \geq 0$ for all $x$, with $V(x) = 0$ for some $x \neq 0$. Notation: $V(x) \geq 0$.
- **Negative Definite (ND):** $-V(x)$ is positive definite. Notation: $V(x) < 0$.
- **Negative Semi-Definite (NSD):** $-V(x) \geq 0$. Notation: $V(x) \leq 0$.
- **Indefinite:** Takes both positive and negative values arbitrarily close to the origin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sylvester's Criterion (for quadratic forms $V(x) = x^T P x$):** A symmetric matrix $P$ is positive definite if and only if **all leading principal minors are strictly positive**. This is the standard KTU 2024 tool for verifying definiteness.

For a $3 \times 3$ symmetric matrix $P = \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} & p_{13} \\ p_{12} & p_{22} & p_{23} \\ p_{13} & p_{23} & p_{33} \end{bmatrix}$, the Sylvester conditions are:

$$
\Delta_1 = p_{11} > 0
$$

$$
\Delta_2 = \begin{vmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} \\ p_{12} & p_{22} \end{vmatrix} = p_{11} p_{22} - p_{12}^2 > 0
$$

$$
\Delta_3 = \det(P) > 0
$$

---

### 2.3 Liapunov's Main Stability Theorems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorems Below are Board-Favorite — Memorize the Conditions and Conclusions Exactly**

**Theorem 1 (Stability):** If a scalar function $V(x)$ exists such that:
- $V(x)$ is **positive definite** (PD)
- $\dot{V}(x)$ is **negative semi-definite** (NSD)

Then the equilibrium at the origin is **stable**.

**Theorem 2 (Asymptotic Stability):** If:
- $V(x)$ is **PD**
- $\dot{V}(x)$ is **ND**

Then the equilibrium is **asymptotically stable**.
*(If $V(x) \to \infty$ as $\|x\| \to \infty$, then asymptotic stability is **global**.)*

**Theorem 3 (Instability):** If:
- $V(x)$ is **PD**
- $\dot{V}(x)$ is **PD** (positive definite, not negative)

Then the equilibrium is **unstable**.

**Theorem 4 (Linear System Criterion):** For $\dot{x} = Ax$, the origin is **GAS if and only if** for any symmetric positive definite matrix $Q$, the unique solution $P$ of the **Liapunov equation**

$$
A^T P + P A = -Q
$$

is **symmetric positive definite**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** When $Q = I$ (identity), the equation becomes $A^T P + P A = -I$. This is the most commonly tested variant. KTU boards expect students to solve for $P$ explicitly and verify $P \succ 0$ via Sylvester.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **Topic** | **Formula / Condition** | **Stability Verdict** |
|---|---|---|
| Liapunov function (linear) | $V(x) = x^T P x$, $P = P^T$ | Energy-like quadratic form |
| Liapunov equation (LTI) | $A^T P + P A = -Q$ | Solved for $P$ given $A, Q$ |
| Sylvester's criterion | All $\Delta_k > 0$ | $P$ is positive definite |
| Stability theorem 1 | $V > 0,\ \dot{V} \leq 0$ | Stable |
| Asymptotic stability | $V > 0,\ \dot{V} < 0$ | Asymptotically stable |
| Instability | $V > 0,\ \dot{V} > 0$ | Unstable |
| Sylvester's $2 \times 2$ check | $p_{11} > 0,\ p_{11}p_{22} - p_{12}^2 > 0$ | $P \succ 0$ |
| Sylvester's $3 \times 3$ check | $\Delta_1, \Delta_2, \Delta_3 > 0$ | $P \succ 0$ |
| Global asymptotic stability | $V(x) \to \infty$ as $\vert x \vert \to \infty$ | Plus $\dot{V} < 0$ |
| Region of attraction | $V(x) < c$ where $\dot{V} < 0$ in region | Estimate of domain of stability |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notation Caution:** Never use the vertical bar `|` inside the markdown table for absolute value. Always use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lVert x \rVert$ in LaTeX. This is enforced to prevent table-rendering failures in the KTU digital notes format.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power system stability analysis** (e.g., transient stability of generators during faults).
- **Robotics and manipulator control** — joint-position stability of nonlinear arm dynamics.
- **Aerospace systems** — attitude stability of satellites and aircraft under nonlinear aerodynamic effects.
- **Chemical process control** — reactor stability under nonlinear reaction kinetics.
- **Neural network dynamics** — convergence of recurrent network equilibria.
- **Adaptive control design** — Liapunov-based direct adaptive laws for parameter convergence.

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the Liapunov Equation for Linear Systems

**Starting Point:** Consider the linear time-invariant (LTI) autonomous system:

$$
\dot{x}(t) = A x(t), \quad x(0) = x_0
$$

**Step 1 — Propose a quadratic Liapunov candidate:**

$$
V(x) = x^T P x
$$

where $P$ is a real symmetric matrix ($P = P^T$) to be determined.

**Step 2 — Compute the time derivative along the system trajectory** using the chain rule and the product rule:

$$
\dot{V}(x) = \dot{x}^T P x + x^T P \dot{x}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\dot{x} = Ax$ into the expression above:**

$$
\dot{V}(x) = (Ax)^T P x + x^T P (Ax) = x^T A^T P x + x^T P A x
$$

**Step 4 — Factor out the state vector** (using $(AB)^T = B^T A^T$ and associativity):

$$
\dot{V}(x) = x^T (A^T P + P A) x
$$

**Step 5 — Enforce the Liapunov stability requirement.** We want $\dot{V}(x) < 0$ for all $x \neq 0$ to guarantee asymptotic stability. This means the matrix $A^T P + P A$ must be **negative definite**. By convention, we set it equal to $-Q$ where $Q$ is symmetric positive definite:

$$
A^T P + P A = -Q
$$

This is the **Liapunov Equation** for continuous-time systems. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Liapunov Stability Test for a $2 \times 2$ System

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard Problem (Likely 7 or 14 Marks) — Solve Completely**

**Problem:** Test the stability of the origin for the system

$$
\dot{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & -2 \end{bmatrix} x
$$

using the Liapunov function $V(x) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$ (i.e., $Q = I$).

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the system matrix:**

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad A^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Propose symmetric matrix $P$** with unknowns:

$$
P = \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} \\ p_{12} & p_{22} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Set up the Liapunov equation $A^T P + P A = -I$:**

$$
A^T P = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} \\ p_{12} & p_{22} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -p_{12} & -p_{22} \\ p_{11} - 2p_{12} & p_{12} - 2p_{22} \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
P A = \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} \\ p_{12} & p_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & -2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -p_{12} & p_{11} - 2p_{12} \\ -p_{22} & p_{12} - 2p_{22} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Add the two matrices:**

$$
A^T P + P A = \begin{bmatrix} -2p_{12} & p_{11} - 2p_{12} - p_{22} \\ p_{11} - 2p_{12} - p_{22} & 2p_{12} - 4p_{22} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Equate entries to solve for $P$:**

From element (1,1):

$$
-2p_{12} = -1 \implies p_{12} = 0.5
$$

From element (1,2):

$$
p_{11} - 2p_{12} - p_{22} = 0 \implies p_{11} - 1 - p_{22} = 0 \implies p_{11} = 1 + p_{22}
$$

From element (2,2):

$$
2p_{12} - 4p_{22} = -1 \implies 1 - 4p_{22} = -1 \implies p_{22} = 0.5
$$

Therefore $p_{11} = 1 + 0.5 = 1.5$.

**Step 6 — Assemble $P$ and verify positive definiteness via Sylvester:**

$$
P = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Check: $\Delta_1 = 1.5 > 0$ ✓

$$
\Delta_2 = (1.5)(0.5) - (0.5)^2 = 0.75 - 0.25 = 0.5 > 0 \ \checkmark
$$

**Step 7 — Conclude stability:** Since $P$ is symmetric positive definite and $Q = I$ is positive definite, by Liapunov's Theorem 4, the origin is **globally asymptotically stable**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Krasovskii's Method of Constructing $V(x)$

**Motivation:** For nonlinear systems, finding a Liapunov function is the central challenge. Krasovskii proposed a systematic construction.

**Consider the nonlinear autonomous system:**

$$
\dot{x} = f(x), \quad f(0) = 0
$$

Assume $f(x)$ is differentiable, with $J(x) = \partial f / \partial x$ the Jacobian.

**Krasovskii's Candidate:**

$$
V(x) = f(x)^T f(x) = \Vert f(x) \Vert^2
$$

Equivalently,

$$
V(x) = \dot{x}^T \dot{x}
$$

**Compute $\dot{V}(x)$:**

$$
\dot{V}(x) = \dot{f}(x)^T f(x) + f(x)^T \dot{f}(x) = 2 f(x)^T \dot{f}(x)
$$

Using the chain rule, $\dot{f}(x) = J(x) \dot{x} = J(x) f(x)$:

$$
\dot{V}(x) = 2 f(x)^T J(x) f(x) = f(x)^T \left[ J(x) + J(x)^T \right] f(x)
$$

**Stability Verdict:**
- $V(x) > 0$ for $x \neq 0$ (assuming $f(x) \neq 0$ for $x \neq 0$).
- If $J(x) + J(x)^T$ is **negative definite**, then $\dot{V}(x) < 0$ for $x \neq 0$, and the origin is **asymptotically stable**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Construction Methods

| **Method** | **Best Suited For** | **Candidate Form** | **Difficulty** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direct (Trial) | Polynomial nonlinearities | $V = a x_1^2 + b x_2^2$ or $a x_1^2 + b x_1 x_2 + c x_2^2$ | Low (educated guess) |
| Krasovskii | Differentiable nonlinear systems | $V = f(x)^T f(x)$ | Medium (need Jacobian) |
| Variable Gradient | General nonlinear systems | $\nabla V = $ unknown vector field | High (must integrate) |
| Linear Matrix Inequality | Modern / advanced problems | $A^T P + P A \prec 0$ | Requires software |

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Krasovskii's Method on a $2$D Nonlinear System

**Problem:** Apply Krasovskii's method to

$$
\dot{x}_1 = -3 x_1 + x_2^2
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2 = -x_1 x_2 - x_2
$$

**Step 1 — Write the state vector form:**

$$
f(x) = \begin{bmatrix} -3x_1 + x_2^2 \\ -x_1 x_2 - x_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Jacobian $J(x) = \partial f / \partial x$:**

$$
J(x) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_2} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -3 & 2 x_2 \\ -x_2 & -x_1 - 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $J(x) + J(x)^T$:**

$$
J(x) + J(x)^T = \begin{bmatrix} -6 & 2x_2 - x_2 \\ 2x_2 - x_2 & -2x_1 - 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -6 & x_2 \\ x_2 & -2x_1 - 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Krasovskii's stability criterion.** For $\dot{V}$ to be negative definite, the symmetric matrix $S = J + J^T$ must be negative definite. By Sylvester's criterion (negative definite when leading principal minors alternate, starting negative):

$$
\Delta_1 = -6 < 0 \ \checkmark
$$

$$
\Delta_2 = \det(S) = (-6)(-2x_1 - 2) - x_2^2 = 12 x_1 + 12 - x_2^2
$$

For $\Delta_2 > 0$, we need $12 x_1 + 12 > x_2^2$, which holds in a bounded region (a valid region of attraction). 

**Conclusion:** The origin is **asymptotically stable** within the region defined by $12 x_1 + 12 > x_2^2$. $\blacksquare$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Krasovskii's method is the KTU 2024 standard for "constructing a Liapunov function" problems in Part B. It avoids the need for guesswork.

## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Liapunov Stability Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: System and Equilibrium] --> B{Choose Candidate V of x}
    B --> C[Step 1: Verify V greater than 0 for x not equal to 0]
    C --> D{Is V Positive Definite?}
    D -- No --> E[Reject V. Try another candidate]
    D -- Yes --> F[Step 2: Compute time derivative of V along trajectory]
    F --> G{Analyze sign of dV by dt}
    G --> H[Case 1: dV by dt less than 0 ND]
    H --> I[Origin is Asymptotically Stable]
    I --> J{V tends to infinity as norm x tends to infinity?}
    J -- Yes --> K[GLOBAL Asymptotically Stable]
    J -- No --> L[LOCAL Asymptotically Stable]
    G --> M[Case 2: dV by dt less or equal to 0 NSD]
    M --> N[Origin is Stable in Liapunov Sense]
    G --> O[Case 3: dV by dt greater than 0 PD]
    O --> P[Origin is UNSTABLE]
    E --> B
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Liapunov Analysis

```mermaid
graph TB
    inputA[Input: System Matrix A of size n by n] --> block1[Block 1: Select symmetric positive definite Q, typically Q equals I]
    block1 --> block2[Block 2: Formulate Liapunov Equation A transpose P plus P A equals negative Q]
    block2 --> block3[Block 3: Solve the linear system of n of n plus 1 by 2 algebraic equations for entries of P]
    block3 --> block4[Block 4: Apply Sylvester Criterion on P]
    block4 --> decision1{All Leading Principal Minors greater than 0}
    decision1 -- Yes --> outA[Output: P is Positive Definite. System is Globally Asymptotically Stable]
    decision1 -- No --> outB[Output: System is Unstable or Marginally Stable]
    outA --> block5[Block 5: Optional: Compute V of x equals x transpose P x and its time derivative]
    block5 --> endA[End: Report Conclusion with Energy Decay Rate]
    outB --> endA
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Comparison of Stability Definitions

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Equilibrium at Origin] --> B[Stable]
    A --> C[Asymptotically Stable]
    A --> D[Exponentially Stable]
    A --> E[Unstable]
    B --> F[Trajectory stays within epsilon ball]
    C --> G[Trajectory stays in epsilon ball AND returns to origin]
    D --> H[Trajectory decays exponentially fast]
    E --> I[Trajectory exits any bounded region]
    G --> J[Stronger than mere stability]
    H --> K[Stronger than asymptotic stability]
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Liapunov Function Construction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start[Start: Given nonlinear system dot x equals f of x] --> step1[Method A: Direct Trial of Quadratic V]
    start --> step2[Method B: Krasovskii V equals f transpose f]
    start --> step3[Method C: Variable Gradient Method]
    step1 --> test[Apply Sylvester and Sign Test on dV by dt]
    step2 --> test
    step3 --> test
    test --> result{Stability Verdict}
    result --> stable[Stable]
    result --> asymp[Asymptotically Stable]
    result --> global[GAS]
    result --> un[Unstable]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**Q1. Define the following terms with respect to Liapunov stability analysis:**
**(a) Positive definite function**
**(b) Negative semi-definite function**
**(c) Asymptotic stability**

**Model Answer:**

**(a)** A scalar function $V(x)$ is **positive definite** if $V(0) = 0$ and $V(x) > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$ in some region containing the origin. **Example:** $V(x) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$.

**(b)** $V(x)$ is **negative semi-definite** if $V(0) = 0$ and $V(x) \leq 0$ for all $x \neq 0$, with $V(x) = 0$ for at least one $x \neq 0$. **Example:** $V(x) = -x_2^2$.

**(c)** The equilibrium at the origin is **asymptotically stable** if it is stable in the Liapunov sense AND $\lim_{t \to \infty} \|x(t)\| = 0$ for all initial conditions in a neighborhood of the origin.

---

**Q2. State Liapunov's stability theorem for asymptotic stability of an equilibrium point. What is the role of the Liapunov equation $A^T P + P A = -Q$ in linear system analysis?**

**Model Answer:**

**Liapunov's Theorem (Asymptotic Stability):** If there exists a scalar function $V(x)$ with continuous first partial derivatives such that (i) $V(x)$ is positive definite, and (ii) $\dot{V}(x)$ is negative definite, then the equilibrium at the origin is asymptotically stable. If in addition $V(x) \to \infty$ as $\|x\| \to \infty$, the stability is global.

**Role of the Liapunov Equation:** For the LTI system $\dot{x} = Ax$, the Liapunov equation $A^T P + P A = -Q$ provides a constructive way to test stability. The origin is globally asymptotically stable if and only if for any symmetric positive definite $Q$, the unique solution $P$ of the equation is also symmetric positive definite. This avoids computing eigenvalues of $A$ directly.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester, Module 4 Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Liapunov Equation and Linear System Stability

**(a)** Derive the Liapunov equation for the continuous-time system $\dot{x} = Ax$. State clearly the assumptions on the matrices involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the system $\dot{x} = Ax$ with

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Using the Liapunov function $V(x) = x^T P x$ with $Q = I$, determine whether the origin is asymptotically stable. Verify $P$ using Sylvester's criterion. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:** The derivation is exactly as shown in Section 3.1 of this document. The student is expected to:
- Propose $V(x) = x^T P x$ with $P = P^T$ (symmetric).
- Differentiate: $\dot{V} = \dot{x}^T P x + x^T P \dot{x}$.
- Substitute $\dot{x} = Ax$ to obtain $\dot{V} = x^T(A^T P + P A) x$.
- Set $A^T P + P A = -Q$ (negative definite) for asymptotic stability.
- **Assumptions:** $P$ symmetric, $Q$ symmetric positive definite, $A$ Hurwitz (eigenvalues in left half plane).

**(b) Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify $A$ and $A^T$:**

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad A^T = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Form the Liapunov equation $A^T P + P A = -I$:**

$$
A^T P = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 1 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} \\ p_{12} & p_{22} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -p_{11} & -p_{12} \\ p_{11} - 2p_{12} & p_{12} - 2p_{22} \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
P A = \begin{bmatrix} p_{11} & p_{12} \\ p_{12} & p_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -p_{11} & p_{11} - 2p_{12} \\ -p_{12} & p_{12} - 2p_{22} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Add and equate to $-I$:**

$$
A^T P + P A = \begin{bmatrix} -2 p_{11} & p_{11} - 3 p_{12} \\ p_{11} - 3 p_{12} & 2 p_{12} - 4 p_{22} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $P$:**
- Element (1,1): $-2p_{11} = -1 \implies p_{11} = 0.5$
- Element (1,2): $p_{11} - 3p_{12} = 0 \implies 0.5 - 3p_{12} = 0 \implies p_{12} = 1/6$
- Element (2,2): $2p_{12} - 4p_{22} = -1 \implies (1/3) - 4p_{22} = -1 \implies p_{22} = (1/3 + 1)/4 = 1/3$

**Step 5 — Assemble $P$:**

$$
P = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 1/6 \\ 1/6 & 1/3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Sylvester's criterion:**
- $\Delta_1 = 0.5 > 0$ ✓
- $\Delta_2 = (0.5)(1/3) - (1/6)^2 = 1/6 - 1/36 = 5/36 > 0$ ✓

**Step 7 — Conclusion:** $P$ is symmetric positive definite, and $Q = I$ is positive definite, so by Liapunov's theorem the origin is **globally asymptotically stable**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Nonlinear System Stability via Krasovskii's Method

**(a)** Explain the Krasovskii method of constructing a Liapunov function for a nonlinear system $\dot{x} = f(x)$. State the stability condition derived from this method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using Krasovskii's method, investigate the stability of the origin for the nonlinear system

$$
\dot{x}_1 = -x_1 + x_2^3
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2 = -x_1^2 x_2 - x_2
$$

Determine the region of asymptotic stability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Krasovskii's Method:** (As derived in Section 3.3 of this document.)
- Candidate: $V(x) = f(x)^T f(x) = \Vert f(x) \Vert^2$.
- Time derivative: $\dot{V}(x) = f(x)^T [J(x) + J(x)^T] f(x)$.
- **Stability Condition:** If $J(x) + J(x)^T$ is negative definite in a region around the origin, then $\dot{V}(x) < 0$ and the origin is asymptotically stable in that region.

**(b) Step-by-step Krasovskii Analysis:**

**Step 1 — Form the vector field:**

$$
f(x) = \begin{bmatrix} -x_1 + x_2^3 \\ -x_1^2 x_2 - x_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Jacobian:**

$$
J(x) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_2} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial f_2}{\partial x_2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 3 x_2^2 \\ -2 x_1 x_2 & -x_1^2 - 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $J(x) + J(x)^T$:**

$$
J + J^T = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 3x_2^2 - 2 x_1 x_2 \\ 3 x_2^2 - 2 x_1 x_2 & -2 x_1^2 - 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Sylvester for negative definiteness** (alternating signs starting negative):
- $\Delta_1 = -2 < 0$ ✓
- $\Delta_2 = \det = (-2)(-2 x_1^2 - 2) - (3x_2^2 - 2x_1 x_2)^2$

$$
\Delta_2 = 4 x_1^2 + 4 - (3x_2^2 - 2 x_1 x_2)^2
$$

For $\Delta_2 > 0$, we need:

$$
4 x_1^2 + 4 > (3 x_2^2 - 2 x_1 x_2)^2
$$

This is a bounded region around the origin. For **small** $x_1, x_2$, the LHS dominates the RHS, so $J + J^T$ is negative definite in a neighborhood of the origin.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** The origin is **asymptotically stable** in a region bounded approximately by $4 x_1^2 + 4 > (3 x_2^2 - 2 x_1 x_2)^2$, and since the system Jacobian is bounded, we can extend this to global asymptotic stability for this particular system structure.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones — KTU Board Examiner Observations:**
> 1. **Forgetting the assumption $P = P^T$ (symmetry).** If you set up a general $P$ with 4 unknowns for a $2 \times 2$ system, you will get 4 equations for 4 unknowns but lose 1 mark for missing symmetry.
> 2. **Not verifying Sylvester's criterion after solving for $P$.** Solving for $P$ is only worth 3 marks; verifying $P \succ 0$ via Sylvester is worth 2 marks. Skipping it costs heavily.
> 3. **Confusing negative definite and negative semi-definite.** The conclusion **stable (not asymptotically stable)** is valid when $\dot{V}$ is NSD. Students often incorrectly upgrade this to "asymptotically stable", losing 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Krasovskii: Forgetting to compute the Jacobian.** $V = f^T f$ alone is not enough. You MUST compute $\dot{V} = 2 f^T J f$ explicitly.
> 5. **Writing the Liapunov equation incorrectly** as $AP + PA = -Q$ (matrix multiplication is non-commutative, so order matters).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Points — Re-read 24 hours before KTU exam:**

- **Liapunov's method evaluates stability without solving the differential equation.**
- The **equilibrium is assumed at the origin** — translate coordinates if needed.
- $V(x)$ is the **Liapunov candidate** (energy-like scalar function).
- $V(x)$ must be **PD** and $\dot{V}(x)$ must be **ND** for **asymptotic stability**.
- $\dot{V} \leq 0$ (NSD) only gives **stability**, not asymptotic stability.
- $\dot{V} > 0$ (PD) gives **instability** (Chetaev's theorem variant).
- For LTI systems: **Liapunov equation** $A^T P + P A = -Q$ — solve for $P$, verify PD via **Sylvester's criterion**.
- Choose $Q = I$ (identity) for simplicity unless otherwise specified.
- **$P \succ 0$ AND $Q \succ 0$** ⟹ origin is **GAS**.
- **Krasovskii's method** is the standard KTU tool for nonlinear stability.
- **Variable gradient method** integrates $\nabla V$ to find $V(x)$.
- **Global asymptotic stability** requires $V(x) \to \infty$ as $\|x\| \to \infty$ (radially unbounded).
- **Region of attraction** is bounded by the largest level set $V(x) = c$ within which $\dot{V} < 0$ holds.
- **Liapunov equation** is a **linear** equation in the entries of $P$, despite the quadratic appearance.
- **Negative definite** matrices have all eigenvalues strictly negative; check via Sylvester (alternating sign leading principal minors).
- For 2×2 systems: $\Delta_1 < 0$ AND $\Delta_2 > 0$ ⟹ negative definite.
- For 3×3 systems: $\Delta_1 < 0$, $\Delta_2 > 0$, $\Delta_3 < 0$ ⟹ negative definite.
- **Liapunov's method is necessary AND sufficient for LTI systems** — a major advantage over the direct method for nonlinear systems.
- Always check **symmetry** of $P$ before submitting answers.
- If $P$ fails Sylvester's criterion, the system is **not asymptotically stable** — do not attempt to "fix" $P$.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** For 14-mark Part B questions, always structure your answer into **(i) Propose $V$, (ii) Compute $\dot{V}$, (iii) Apply Sylvester / sign test, (iv) State conclusion with theorem reference.** This 4-part structure matches the KTU 2024 marking rubric perfectly.
